This implies that, while they notice payoff differences, they do not allow those differences to control their behavior for long. They show no sign of strategic matching such as “cooperate only if most others cooperate” or “defect when others defect.” Instead, they appear to treat others’ outcomes as background information rather than as a trigger for sustained behavioral change. They neither escalate cooperation in response to generous rounds (because they are already maxing out) nor punish low contributors by reducing their own contributions. There is no evidence of retaliatory motives based on observed payoffs. Because the environment lacks punishment and reward mechanisms, we cannot observe how they would use such tools. It remains unknown whether, if costly punishment or reward were available, they would enforce norms or compensate cooperators. All that can be inferred is that, in the absence of such levers, they accept outcome inequalities and continue to cooperate. In summary, they are outcome-aware but outcome-resistant: they register that defectors often do better, test defection once, and then recommit to a cooperative path, indicating that maintaining their contribution rule matters more to them than eliminating disadvantageous payoff gaps.

The player’s choices closely track not just others’ contributions but also the payoff structure those contributions create. They repeatedly take advantage of situations where many others contribute while they do not, securing higher round payoffs than most contributors. This demonstrates that they pay attention to the payoff consequences of others’ behavior and are willing to position themselves as the better-off freerider when coordination allows it. At the same time, they also join in and contribute fully when group cooperation is high and the public good returns are strong. In those periods, they seem content to earn roughly similar payoffs to other contributors, suggesting that they value the high absolute returns generated by cooperation and do not require being individually best off in every round. This mix implies that their response to others’ outcomes is nuanced: they are attracted to high-payoff, cooperative states, but they also opportunistically shift to exploitation when the margin from freeriding becomes especially attractive. There is no direct evidence of emotional or moral reaction to inequality—only behavioral adaptation. They tolerate earning less than defectors in some cooperative phases, then later adopt the defector role themselves. This indicates relatively low aversion to payoff asymmetries per se; inequality is acceptable if it is in their favor, and occasionally accepted even when it is not, as long as the absolute level of earnings is high or cooperation seems worth preserving. Because punishment and reward are unavailable in this game, we do not observe any costly moves to change others’ payoffs. However, given their pattern, they appear unlikely to engage in strong altruistic or spiteful sanctioning even if it were available: they do not behave like a norm crusader but rather like someone primarily optimizing their own earnings in response to the existing contribution environment. In new PGGs, expect them to monitor others’ contributions and resulting payoffs closely and to alternate between joining cooperative clusters and exploiting them, rather than rigidly sticking to one moral stance.

The player is outcome-aware and appears to condition their own behavior on how others’ payoffs compare to theirs, but their response is indirect because there is no explicit punishment or reward tool in this game. They can see that free-riders frequently earn more than contributors, and they themselves at times experience both sides: earning less than defectors when they contribute, and earning more than contributors when they free-ride. Their subsequent switches between contributing and free-riding are consistent with learning from these payoff contrasts. They do not exhibit a stable pattern of “reciprocal fairness” (e.g., always matching the cooperative majority so that payoffs converge). Sometimes they keep contributing while some peers visibly gain more by defecting, which implies some tolerance for being comparatively disadvantaged in the short run. At other times, after observing that non-contributors are doing well relative to contributors, they shift into a free-riding phase, which suggests that salient payoff asymmetries eventually push them toward self-serving action. They also do not show clear evidence of being strongly demotivated by the presence of chronic low contributors: they continue to contribute in several environments where non-cooperation is prevalent or payoffs to defectors are higher. Likewise, when overall cooperation improves and payoffs rise for contributors, they do not unconditionally join in or remain in lockstep; they sometimes still defect and take the extra margin. In sum, the player pays attention to others’ contributions and resulting payoffs and adapts in a way that mixes opportunism with intermittent willingness to support the group. They are sensitive to payoff structures but do not enforce or follow a rigid equity norm; instead, they flexibly alternate roles between cooperator and beneficiary of others’ cooperation, without using explicit social sanctions (unavailable here) and without consistent, strong reciprocity in either direction.

The player observes others’ contributions and payoffs and appears to react primarily to the structure of incentives rather than to distributional fairness or specific individuals’ outcomes. Behavior suggests: - Sensitivity to group-level cooperation: they are more likely to defect when others’ contribution levels are high enough to guarantee a substantial public return without their own input, accepting that others will earn less than they could have if they had also defected. This reflects low concern for others’ relative disadvantage when they are being exploited. - Tolerance of being exploited: they sometimes continue to contribute in environments where several others defect and earn more than contributors. This points away from strong negative reciprocity; they do not automatically punish free-riders by ceasing to cooperate. - No clear targeting of specific peers: there is no consistent pattern of “rewarding” highly cooperative individual identities or “responding” to recurring defectors. Their reactions appear to be driven by aggregate contribution and payoff patterns, not by social preferences over particular partners. - Absence of punishment or reward tools in this environment means we cannot observe their willingness to incur costs to change others’ payoffs; that dimension of their type is therefore unknown. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes in a primarily instrumental, aggregate way: high group cooperation invites opportunistic free-riding; moderate or mixed cooperation sometimes elicits contributions if the public return remains attractive. Their social preference profile appears weakly altruistic at best and primarily self-interested, with limited inequality aversion and little evidence of intention-based reciprocity.

The player monitors others’ contributions and outcomes and adapts behavior in a nuanced but not strictly consistent way. They clearly sees that some players repeatedly contribute high amounts and sometimes reward; at various points, they responds by increasing contributions and/or by investing substantially in rewards, which is compatible with conditional cooperation and norm-affirmation: when others behave cooperatively, they are more willing to either join in with higher contributions, spend to reward, or both. They also observes that some players persistently contribute little yet still earn high payoffs, and in response, they occasionally shift toward low contributions themselves, exploiting cooperative environments. However, this shift is not permanent; they later return to relatively higher contributions and renewed use of rewards. This stop–start pattern suggests a learning or probing style: they tests the payoff consequences of alternative strategies in light of others’ outcomes and then revises behavior based on perceived returns. There is no evidence that they respond directly to others’ reward expenditures per se with mirror-like behavior; they does not, for example, always reward those who spend heavily on rewards or always increase contributions after seeing others sacrifice payoff to reward cooperators. Instead, they seems to attend more to the combination of contributions and final payoffs. When high contributors are not excessively penalized by low contributors, they are more willing to participate in that cooperative structure, sometimes reinforcing it with costly rewards. When free-riding appears advantageous and relatively unchecked, they temporarily move in that direction themselves. Overall, this player’s type is best described as a “strategic conditional cooperator with opportunistic tendencies and an active but selective reward motive”: they cares about cooperation and sometimes invests in it, uses rewards as a norm-shaping tool despite personal cost, but remains willing to reduce contributions and exploit cooperative groups when circumstances appear favorable.

They monitor others’ contributions and reward expenditures, and they are aware of who is bearing costs to support others, but they do not strongly imitate or reinforce these behaviors. High-contributing, high-rewarding players coexist with them, yet they do not consistently coordinate with them, either by matching cooperation or by rewarding them in proportion to their sacrifices. They tolerate the presence of strong cooperators and heavy rewarders while sometimes free-riding on them. When some players spend large amounts on reward, this does not push them into either conforming to that cooperative norm or exploiting it more aggressively in a systematic way; their own strategy remains mixed and somewhat noisy. They appear to treat others’ costly cooperation and reward as part of the environment that can be advantageous to them, rather than as a cue to adhere to group-oriented norms. They also do not react strongly to shifts in group cooperation levels: when overall contributions drop, they sometimes increase their own contribution and sometimes do not, with no clear pattern of “rescuing” the public good or punishing low cooperators via non-reward. They are observant but not norm-driven; their primary adjustment lever is their own contribution level, used inconsistently, with only light and sporadic use of reward. Overall, they behave as an adaptive but loosely contingent opportunist, more focused on testing payoff-relevant moves than on stabilizing group outcomes or enforcing fairness across players.

The player observes that some others contribute large amounts and often spend significantly on rewards, sometimes sacrificing personal payoff to support group members. They also see that they themselves can obtain high payoffs while contributing little and not rewarding. Their behavior suggests they interpret others’ cooperative and rewarding actions as exploitable rather than as norms they should join. They do not adjust to protect high contributors from exploitation, nor do they emulate those who earn slightly less due to rewarding; instead, they consistently choose the high-payoff, low-giving path when possible. There is no evidence they are swayed by others’ costs or sacrifices, and no evidence of moralistic punishment via reward withholding beyond what is already implied by their universal non-reward stance. Overall, they appear largely indifferent to others’ outcomes except insofar as group contributions increase their own redistributed payoff.

This player observes others’ outcomes, including who spends on reward and who gains from it, but does not react strongly or systematically through their own reward choices or contribution changes. Even when some players spend heavily on rewarding others, this player neither joins in nor visibly punishes free-riding via withholding contributions; instead they persist in contributing fully while largely ignoring the side-payoff structure created by others’ reward spending. They do not shift upward or downward in contributions in response to others earning especially high or low payoffs, nor do they appear to target “under-rewarded” cooperators or “over-rewarded” defectors with compensatory behavior. Their one notable behavioral inflection—stopping contributions near the end—is not clearly driven by others’ payoffs but by the remaining horizon. In terms of social perception, this suggests they attend to others’ outcomes enough to understand the environment but do not let those outcomes override their internal plan: cooperate robustly while it is still strategically reasonable, then defect when future interactions no longer justify costly cooperation. Their stance toward others’ costly reward efforts is essentially observational rather than participatory: they accept any benefits from being rewarded when they arise but abstain from entering the “reward economy” themselves beyond minimal, isolated gestures.

The player observes others’ contributions, rewards given, and resulting payoffs each round but reacts in a muted and indirect way. They do not imitate high rewarders, do not join others in rewarding prominent cooperators, and do not protect or favor those who have supported them. Even when some players incur substantial costs to reward others, this player does not follow their lead. Their contribution adjustments show some responsiveness to the overall level of group cooperation but little evidence of targeted reactions to specific individuals’ actions or outcomes. They do not appear to punish low contributors via reduced contributions relative to those individuals (since only a global contribution action is available), and they also do not offset apparent exploitation by stepping up their own contributions. They seem to interpret others’ heavy use of reward mainly as background information rather than as an invitation to participate in a reward-based norm system. Likewise, they do not appear to respond to others’ payoffs with attempts to equalize or counteract inequality; there is no pattern of “rescue” behavior toward low-earning high cooperators or retaliatory stance toward high-earning defectors. In summary, they monitor the environment enough to keep their contributions in a safe mid-range, but they do not actively engage in social norm enforcement or relational signaling through rewards or contribution extremes.

The player tracks others’ contributions and outcomes enough to adapt their own giving but does not intervene in others’ payoffs via reward. They observe that some players incur substantial costs to reward others and that some receive sizeable bonus income, yet they do not imitate those patterns or “join in” to reward the same individuals. This suggests they are attentive but not socially conformist with respect to the extra mechanism: they notice but largely ignore the reward ecology. Their contribution adjustments suggest they respond primarily to aggregate cooperation levels, not to individual fortunes. When the group seems more cooperative, they are willing to contribute more, even when some low contributors benefit disproportionately. When the group’s pattern looks less stable or more self‑interested, they allow their own contribution to fall, effectively protecting themselves rather than directly sanctioning or compensating others. They show no sign of being motivated by envy or moral outrage at freeriders: they do not attempt to correct inequities through the available (reward‑only) mechanism, nor do they strongly punish via contribution withdrawal in a way that targets specific individuals. Any emotional response to seeing others earn high or low payoffs is behaviorally muted: they adapt their own level of cooperation but do not try to reshape others’ payoffs. Their dominant mode is private adjustment rather than active social control.

The player clearly observes others’ contribution levels, payoffs, and reward behavior, and their own strategy appears adaptive to these observations. When they first see very high group cooperation, they temporarily match high contributions and once invest in rewarding contributors, suggesting they recognize and can align with cooperative norms when they judge it beneficial or salient. However, when they later observe that some players continue to contribute high amounts while others defect or reduce contributions, they choose to free-ride rather than helping to stabilize cooperation, and they do not use reward to support high contributors despite having done so once before. They also see some players spending heavily on reward (sometimes at substantial personal cost), yet they do not imitate this pattern. Their reaction to others’ costly rewarding looks more exploitative than imitative: they accept the benefits of a high-contribution environment and occasional rewards without committing resources to sustain it. Overall, they infer that others’ generosity can be exploited with limited social consequences in this setting and adjust toward a low-contribution, no-reward profile once they are confident of this. Their beliefs about others’ future behavior are not directly observable, but their choices are consistent with expecting continued cooperation from others even as they themselves pull back.

This player’s behavior is strikingly insensitive to information about others’ contributions, punishment, rewards, and resulting payoffs. They observe that some players contribute very little and are heavily punished, others spend large amounts on sanctions, and some direct substantial resources into rewarding cooperators, yet they do not adjust either their contribution or their use of punishment/reward. They do not appear to retaliate against those who may reduce their relative payoff, nor do they reciprocate those who increase it. They seem indifferent to payoff inequalities, both when they are relatively advantaged and when they are potentially exploited. Their response profile is: maintain full cooperation and complete neutrality in the sanctioning stage, regardless of emerging coalitions, enforcement patterns, or extreme outcomes for specific individuals. This pattern is consistent with a quietly principled, non-strategic cooperator who does not let others’ actions alter their own chosen rule.

This player monitors others’ contributions more than others’ exact payoffs or spending patterns on punishment/reward. They adjust their own contributions downward only when there are clear, repeated low contributors, and upward again when the group becomes strongly cooperative. They do not appear to respond to others’ large punishment or reward expenditures directly; for instance, they do not imitate heavy punishers, nor do they punish or reward conspicuous spenders or recipients as such. Instead, they consistently ignore the complex web of who spent how much to punish or reward, and focus on contribution behavior itself and the resulting level of group cooperation. Seeing that the main low contributor is already consistently punished by others does not stop them from occasionally adding their own punishment, suggesting they value reinforcing the social signal more than finely optimizing marginal impact. They also show no apparent concern for inequality induced by punishment and reward (some players ending very low or negative, others very high); their own strategy remains stable across a wide dispersion of outcomes. Overall, their behavioral adjustments are modest, slow, and primarily keyed to others’ contribution levels rather than to payoff shocks, costliness of others’ actions, or perceived personal targeting (which is absent here).

This player clearly pays attention to others’ contributions and sanctioning patterns, and adapts modestly but not radically. They maintain high contributions even when some group members earn low payoffs due to heavy punishment, suggesting they are not easily discouraged by visible conflict or by the presence of very low contributors who are being sanctioned. They also do not join extreme sanctioning cascades; instead, they continue to use the mechanism sparingly while adjusting whom they target based on observed behavior. The fact that they sometimes punish or reward players whose net payoffs are already strongly affected by others indicates that they respond to underlying behavior (contributions and use of sanctions) rather than simply to final earnings. They do not appear to chase immediate payoff trends—when others’ outcomes become more variable or harsh due to escalating punishment, they keep their own contribution strategy largely intact. Overall, they behave like a principled, high-cooperation norm enforcer: they monitor the social environment, update their sanctions in light of others’ actions and costs, but anchor their own cooperation level on internal norms more than on short-run outcome fluctuations.

They observe a rich environment of intense punishment and fairly frequent rewarding, including extreme targeting of some low contributors and heavy spending by certain players on sanctions and bonuses. Across all of this, their own behavior—both contributions and the total absence of sanctioning/rewarding—remains calm and consistent. They show no behavioral reaction to others’ large payoff losses or to others’ sizable sanctioning costs. This suggests they treat others’ outcomes as background rather than as cues that demand adjustment. They do not try to protect low contributors who are heavily punished, nor do they join in punishing them. Likewise, they do not mirror the generosity of heavy rewarders. They appear indifferent, in behavioral terms, to the fairness or harshness of the final payoff distribution, as long as the overall contribution environment remains sufficiently cooperative for them to keep playing their own preferred strategy. Their adaptation is limited and smooth: they modestly adjust contributions with the general contribution climate but ignore detailed signals from punishment and reward patterns. This points to a personality type that is steady, non-confrontational, and self-directed: they cooperate at a comfortable level, avoid moral policing, and do not let dramatic outcomes pull them into punitive or reciprocal spirals. Their beliefs about why others punish or reward, and whether they approve of those motives, remain unknown.

This player monitors others’ outcomes closely and adjusts their social interventions more than their core cooperative stance. They pay attention not only to contributions but also to how much others are spending on punishment and reward, as well as who is being heavily sanctioned or supported. They see that some players are being punished extremely harshly for persistent low contributions, and yet they still occasionally add their own sanctions against such players, indicating alignment with a group-enforced norm against extreme free-riding. They also observe which players allocate substantial resources to reward or punishment. While they cannot know individual identities behind sanctions, they respond behaviorally in line with the emerging pattern of group approval or disapproval. When many players appear to reward certain high contributors, they, too, allocate extra resources to these or similar profiles, reinforcing an informal coalition of cooperators. Conversely, when a player’s payoffs become strongly negative due to repeated deductions and continued low contributions, they do not move to protect that player; instead, they either maintain or escalate sanctioning if that player continues to contribute very little relative to others. Their own payoff is often reduced by their extensive use of reward (and occasionally punishment), yet they do not pull back to a purely self-interested strategy. This indicates that observing the redistribution of payoffs across players leads them to double down on their prosocial, norm-shaping role rather than abandon it. They accept personal cost as part of maintaining an outcome pattern where high cooperators earn more and persistent low contributors earn less. Overall, this player functions as a “cooperative norm architect”: they contribute generously, selectively punish clear norm violators, actively reward reliable cooperators, and adjust their interventions in response to observed payoff structures and group patterns, prioritizing the health and clarity of the cooperative norm over their own short-term earnings.

This player systematically uses information on others’ contributions and payoffs to adjust whom they sanction or reward, but they do not appear to optimize around others’ payoffs for personal gain. When low contributors receive large deductions from many punishers, the player sometimes continues to add their own punishment, suggesting they care more about affirming the norm violation than about marginal deterrence. Conversely, when previous low contributors markedly increase their contributions and the overall environment becomes highly cooperative, the player switches from punishing them to rewarding them or ceasing sanctions, indicating that they do pay attention to behavioral change and are willing to “forgive” once current behavior aligns with the norm. They also reward cooperators even when those cooperators are already earning high payoffs and receiving rewards from others, which means they are not using rewards primarily to equalize payoffs; instead, they focus on reinforcing desirable actions regardless of the recipient’s current earnings. They do not show strong evidence of targeting people for having spent a lot on punishment or reward; their responses track contribution behavior more clearly than expenditure profiles. They appear indifferent to the personal cost of their enforcement in the short run, enduring rounds with dramatically reduced net payoff because of extensive punishment and reward outlays. This combination of patterns characterizes them as a high‑norm, high‑cost enforcer–supporter type: they read the social environment, react strongly to extreme deviations, and actively support strong cooperators, using others’ outcomes mainly as signals of who is upholding or undermining the cooperative standard.

The player clearly conditions their behavior on observed contributions and on the group’s use of punishment and reward, but they do so only through their own contributions, not via second-order sanctions. They witness repeated, extremely heavy punishment of chronic low contributors and partial contributors by others. They never join these sanctioning efforts, yet they keep contributing at cooperative levels, implying that they interpret others’ punishment as establishing a high-cooperation norm that they should follow, rather than as a process they need to participate in. When group contributions drop sharply and the environment becomes less cooperative, they also reduce their own giving, indicating that they respond more to actual contribution levels than to the mere presence of punishment and reward in that phase. When they themselves are punished harshly while contributing below almost all others in a highly cooperative environment, they do not retaliate, they do not start punishing heavy punishers, and they do not permanently defect. Instead, they subsequently maintain or increase their contributions in line with the re-emerging cooperative norm. This combination suggests they interpret being punished as social feedback about their relative contribution rather than as an unjust attack to be answered in kind. They also see that some players spend heavily on reward, sometimes to the point of sharply reducing their own payoffs, but they do not imitate this behavior. They appear to respect or accept that others may take on the role of “altruistic enforcers,” while they choose the role of a cooperative but sanction-averse participant. In summary, they track others’ outcomes and sanctioning decisions well enough to adapt their own contribution strategy, but they consistently avoid engaging in the costly second-order mechanisms themselves.

This player pays close implicit attention to who benefits or suffers each round. They repeatedly sanction those whose outcomes reflect very low contributions combined with little or no prior cost-bearing, especially when these players nevertheless receive substantial public-good payoffs. When a player with very low contributions is heavily sanctioned by many others, this player sometimes still adds more punishment later, suggesting they are not deterred by the fact that the target is already doing poorly; they continue to prioritize norm enforcement over concern for the target’s welfare. Conversely, they ignore high contributors’ high payoffs and do not attempt to balance outcomes via reward, indicating they do not aim at equality of payoffs but at punishing free-riding. They occasionally punish someone who contributes nothing while others are contributing a lot, even if that person’s current payoff is already poor due to sanctions, which is consistent with an anti-exploitation stance rather than outcome-based pity. Their own payoff remains relatively strong despite these costs, so they seem willing to trade off some individual earnings for a reputation (anonymous at the individual level, but still a behavioral stance) of being tough on defectors. Their adjustments in contribution after observing general drops in group giving suggest they monitor the overall cooperative climate and modestly adapt, but without abandoning their high-contribution identity. Overall, they act as a principled, sanction-focused cooperator who uses others’ outcomes—especially the combination of low contribution and high benefit—as cues for targeted punishment rather than for redistribution or compensation.

This player observes a complex social environment with heavy punishment activity, some players repeatedly sinking targets’ payoffs far below zero or well below the group, and some players spending substantial amounts on rewards. Despite this, their own behavioral levers (contribution, punishment, reward) remain relatively insulated from these outcome patterns. They see that some individuals are consistently hit with very large punishments, often after very low contributions, and that others invest heavily in both punishment and reward. Yet they neither join these efforts nor evidently adjust their contribution in a way that tracks specific individuals’ payoffs or sanction histories. Their changes in contribution appear to respond more to the broad level of group contributions than to who is being punished or rewarded and by how much. They also do not display opportunism toward victims of sanctions: they do not reduce contributions to free-ride on the fact that others are already bearing the cost of norm enforcement. Nor do they increase contributions when cooperation and positive rewards surge, beyond a moderate alignment with the general contribution climate. They seem to treat others’ payoff outcomes—especially the extreme negative ones—as information that does not require a personal, strategic response. Personality-wise, this suggests a relatively self-contained, low-reactivity style. They care about cooperating at a reasonable level but do not invest in managing others’ behavior and do not let others’ sanctioning choices dictate their own. Their type can be characterized as a “quiet cooperative individualist”: pro-social in contribution, but disengaged from the social politics of punishment and reward, and largely indifferent in action to the detailed payoff consequences others impose on one another.

This player appears to track not only contributions but also how others are treated via punishment and reward. When certain players are subject to repeated, extreme punishment and end up with very low or negative payoffs, the player does not join the punishment; instead, they sometimes respond by rewarding these same or similar low-status participants, indicating sympathy for “victims” of collective harshness or concern for fairness beyond strict contribution-based norms. They do not appear to reward those who spend heavily on punishment, nor do they directly reward heavy punishers even when they contribute fully, suggesting they do not valorize punitive enforcement as a valuable group service. Conversely, they occasionally reward consistent cooperators and those making solid contributions without engaging in extreme sanctioning, which implies they distinguish between “quiet cooperators” and “aggressive enforcers.” They do not visibly condition their own contributions on whether they personally receive rewards or deductions; their giving pattern stays pro-social regardless of personal treatment, suggesting relatively low reciprocity in the narrow, self-centered sense and a more principle-based orientation. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes in a way that softens extremes: they avoid adding to losses for low earners and sometimes channel rewards toward maintaining inclusion and recognizing cooperation, while declining to participate in or endorse severe sanction cascades.

This player is repeatedly and heavily punished for their low contributions but does not visibly adapt their overall stance in a norm-following direction. After receiving large deductions and observing widespread use of rewards and punishments by others, they still continue to free-ride in most subsequent stages, only briefly matching the group’s cooperative norm once and otherwise remaining well below others’ typical contributions. They do not escalate conflict: they never punish back, never target individuals who impose high punishment costs or who free-ride themselves. They also do not join the cooperative “club” via rewards; they ignore the fact that others reward high contributors and that they sometimes receive small rewards. This pattern suggests a detached, payoff-focused type who is relatively unresponsive to reputational and relational cues: social approval, reciprocity, and fear of future sanctions do not strongly redirect their behavior. Learning is limited or highly selective: they may adjust contribution upward temporarily after extreme sanctions, but they do not convert this into a consistent cooperative strategy, and they remain unwilling to incur any cost to shape others’ behavior. Their stance toward others’ outcomes is thus largely observational and passive, not interactive or norm-enforcing.

This player is observant enough to adjust their own contribution pattern over time but does not react through the punishment/reward tools, even when others’ outcomes are extreme. They coexist with a group in which several players spend large sums punishing particular low contributors, and some players incur very large negative payoffs as a result. Despite this, the focal player neither joins these sanctioning coalitions nor opposes them via counter‑punishment or protective rewards. They seem willing to let others bear the cost of norm enforcement while continuing to focus on their own contribution decision. After experiencing severe punishment themselves, they change their contribution back to high levels but still refrain from using the sanctioning mechanisms, suggesting that their learning response operates only through their own contribution choices rather than through socially directed actions. They do not appear to condition contributions on who punishes or rewards whom, although they may be reacting to the aggregate environment of high contributions and strong third‑party punishment by reverting to cooperative contributions. Overall, they treat others’ payoffs, punish/reward costs, and deductions as background signals about the general climate rather than as cues for targeted interaction.

They pay attention to how others behave and how they are treated, and update their targeting accordingly. When a player with a history of very low contributions later matches or exceeds the group’s contribution norm, they relax their own sanctions toward that player, even if others continue to punish heavily. Conversely, when a new free-rider emerges, they begin punishing that player despite that player’s earlier cooperative history. They do not appear to punish those who already receive heavy sanctions from others purely because they are low in payoff; rather, their focus remains on contribution behavior relative to the group norm. There is no sign that they reward players who spend heavily on enforcing norms, nor that they adjust their behavior to curry favor with strong punishers or generous rewarders. Overall, they respond to outcomes by using others’ contributions and evident norm violations to refine who deserves punishment, while remaining largely indifferent to the distribution of rewards and to others’ individual payoffs except as signals of rule-breaking.

The player pays attention to others’ contributions, payoffs, and punishment decisions and updates their own behavior in response, but not in a simple mechanical way. When they see all players contributing nothing, they join the non-cooperative pattern rather than sacrificing to restart public good provision, indicating sensitivity to group norms and the perceived futility of unilateral cooperation. When they see mixed cooperation—some players contributing fully and others not—they sometimes align with the cooperators and sometimes with the defectors, suggesting that they are weighing both payoff asymmetries and recent punishment histories. They also observe how much others spend on punishment and how much is deducted from the group. In some cases, high punishment activity by others is followed by their own use of punishment, implying that they are willing to enter or intensify sanctioning conflicts when they perceive others as aggressive or unfair. They appear especially reactive to situations where some players earn notably higher payoffs at low personal cost; in such contexts, they are more likely to punish heavily or defect, consistent with aversion to being comparatively disadvantaged. However, they do not systematically protect high contributors with punishment or coordinate with them to discipline free-riders, so strong positive reciprocity toward prosocial players is not clearly present. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes with a combination of opportunism (matching low-contribution norms when lucrative) and norm assertion (costly punishment when payoff differences or prior sanctions are perceived as unjust), without settling into a stable cooperative coalition or a purely selfish stance.

The player observes others experiencing large losses from punishment and large swings in payoff, yet does not adjust to imitate punishers, protect frequent targets, or systematically align with any subgroup. They do not step in to “help” those who are heavily punished (no compensating cooperation) and do not appear to exploit vulnerable, heavily sanctioned players with targeted defection or further punishment. Others’ costly sanctioning does not lead them to adopt a more norm-following or more self-protective strategy; they maintain their own oscillating contribution pattern and zero-punishment stance. This indicates a relatively low responsiveness to social signals encoded in others’ payoffs and sanctioning behavior. Their beliefs about others’ intentions and strategies are unknown, but behaviorally they treat others’ outcomes more as background information than as cues that directly guide their own choices.

This player attends to others’ contribution and payoff patterns and adjusts incentives over time. When all peers cooperate, they respond by offering substantial rewards, which is consistent with approval of mutual effort and a desire to support high contributors. When one player repeatedly free-rides while still earning high payoffs, the focal player does not immediately retaliate via defection but does scale back their own reward spending, indicating sensitivity to perceived exploitation and to the cost-effectiveness of further prosocial incentives. Their reactions are nuanced rather than purely tit-for-tat. They continue to contribute even while a free-rider benefits, but their reward behavior becomes more cautious and intermittent, suggesting they differentiate between “cooperation in the public good” (which they maintain for a while) and “extra costly generosity through rewards” (which they reduce when it seems underappreciated or strategically weak). They do not appear to reward players who neither contribute nor reward, nor do they try to offset payoff inequalities via extra transfers; there is no sign of strong inequality aversion expressed through compensatory rewards. In the final stage, when another player still contributes and another defects, the focal player aligns with the non-contributor and provides no rewards, implying that outcomes alone (others getting lower payoffs from still contributing) are no longer sufficient to elicit further generosity. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes by updating their willingness to spend on rewards and, only at the horizon, by changing their contribution level, reflecting a moderately patient but ultimately payoff-aware cooperative type.

The player monitors others’ **contributions and payoffs** and adjusts primarily through reward and (eventually) their own contribution choice, rather than through any immediately reactive pattern to individual payoff differences. They show **tolerance of unequal outcomes**: at times others earn more than they do, partly through not spending on rewards themselves, yet the player continues to cooperate and sometimes even spends on rewards. This implies that modest disadvantage relative to others does not by itself trigger retaliation or withdrawal. The player seems particularly attuned to **collective contribution profiles**: - When all peers contribute at the high level, the player is both fully cooperative and more inclined to reward, consistent with reading high group contributions and strong outcomes as cues for supportive reinforcement. - When exactly one peer free-rides while the others cooperate, the player maintains full contributions but becomes more restrained or inconsistent in reward spending. This can be interpreted as a **guarded response to mixed group behavior**: they do not punish (not available), but they are less eager to sink additional cost into positive transfers when the social environment shows some free-riding. They do not exhibit clear **envy-driven behavior**: seeing others earn more—either by free-riding or by being rewarded more—doesn’t reliably cause them to cut their contributions in the ongoing middle rounds. Their eventual defection aligns with the end of the game rather than with any specific prior unfairness. They also do not display obvious **competitive motives**; there is no sign of attempts to reduce others’ earnings or to avoid helping high-earning cooperators. When the tool exists, their use of it is primarily to increase others’ payoffs, not to create relative advantage. In summary, the player **takes others’ contributions and outcomes into account at the group level**, supporting high-cooperation states and tolerating some unfairness, but aligns their ultimate decisions with game-theoretic incentives and horizon rather than with emotional reactions to specific payoff differences.

They consistently observe others’ outcomes and the extensive punishment activity in the group but maintain their own strategy with only minor adjustment on the punishment dimension. When others’ non-contribution causes substantial payoff losses for the whole group, they do not reduce their own contributions, indicating that decreased personal earnings and visible exploitation do not lead them to withdraw cooperation. At the same time, they are not drawn into the escalating punishment patterns exhibited by several other players; large punishment expenditures and heavy deductions by others do not tempt them to match that intensity. Instead, they remain cost-conscious and selective. Their rare use of punishment coincides with blatant free-riding, implying that they are sensitive to norm violations but try to address them with minimal expenditure. They appear robust to inequality in realized payoffs and to others’ aggressive sanctioning behavior, maintaining a calm, rule-consistent pattern rather than reacting strongly to short-term shocks. Their learning or adaptation is limited: they slightly adjust by using punishment once in response to clear defection, but they do not update their contribution behavior in response to others’ changing contributions or payoffs.

This player is behaviorally insensitive, in their own choices, to others’ payoffs, punishment expenditures, and deduction patterns. They continue to fully cooperate regardless of whether others earn high or low payoffs, and regardless of how harshly some peers punish defectors or occasional cooperators. Large punitive campaigns against specific players, changes in who defects, and shifts in group-level efficiency do not alter their contributions or activate any punishment from their side. This implies that they either do not factor others’ economic outcomes into their own decisions, or that they prioritize a simple, fixed rule (“always contribute fully, never punish”) over outcome-based adjustments. They do not visibly reward high contributors via punishment restraint beyond what they already do for everyone, since they never punish anyone. Overall, their reaction to others’ outcomes is one of stable non-interference: they maintain their own cooperative behavior and leave both inequality and norm enforcement to emerge from others’ actions, without engaging in either support or sanction in response to what they observe.

This player is observant of others’ outcomes in the sense that their behavior shifts after visible patterns of contribution and punishment, but they never respond through direct sanctions. Instead, they adjust only their own contribution level. When they observe a fully cooperative group with low or no punishment, they revert to or maintain full contribution, aligning with the apparent social norm and avoiding actions that would obviously single them out. When they see either a clear free-rider or intense, concentrated punishment directed at particular players (including themselves), they sometimes choose to free-ride as well, even though this exposes them to further punishment. This suggests they interpret harsh or uneven punishment regimes and the presence of defectors as a signal that the cooperative norm is less solid or that norms are being enforced inconsistently. They do not appear to react in a tit-for-tat manner toward specific individuals; since punishment is anonymous and they never punish, they cannot condition on particular identities in sanctioning. Their only observable adjustment channel is their own contribution choice, which is loosely conditional on the aggregate pattern of others’ behavior and payoffs rather than on specific pairwise histories. Emotionally or attitudinally, their internal reactions to others’ payoffs and punishment decisions are unknown. What is clear from behavior is that they neither attempt to protect heavily punished cooperators nor to pile on against repeated defectors; they simply ride along with the evolving environment, cooperating when the group looks stable and exploiting when it looks frayed.

This player monitors others’ outcomes and adjusts only their punishment behavior in light of changes in group cooperation; their contribution rule remains fixed. When everyone cooperates and payoffs are uniformly high, they refrain from punishing, even if some others still spend on punishment. This indicates they are not reacting to others’ punishment expenditures or payoffs per se, but instead to whether contribution norms are being met. When some players free-ride and group payoffs fall, they become willing to pay to reduce those players’ earnings, accepting a lower own payoff to impose consequences. Once cooperation returns to full participation, they withdraw punishment and accept the high joint payoff environment. They do not appear to imitate others’ extremes: even when some players spend very large amounts on punishment, they maintain a moderate, low-cost approach. They also do not punish high earners simply for earning more; their interventions are tied to contribution behavior, not raw payoff inequality. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes as information about norm adherence and group efficiency and adjust their sanctions accordingly, but they do not allow others’ chaotic or heavy-handed punishment strategies to push them into more aggressive or more selfish behavior.

This player monitors others’ outcomes and seems responsive to both contribution patterns and how the punishment environment evolves. When everyone contributes fully and payoffs are equal and high, they refrain from punishing entirely, indicating satisfaction with both the cooperative outcome and the distribution. When some players under-contribute and still earn similar baseline shares from the public pot, the player reacts by imposing costs on those low contributors, even though this further reduces overall efficiency and their own payoff; they prioritize correcting perceived unfair advantage over maximizing group surplus in those instances. They do not appear to punish high spenders on punishment per se, even when some players burn large amounts of their own payoff; instead, their concern centers on who contributes versus who benefits. After cooperation is re-established, they immediately stop punishing, showing that their enforcement behavior is contingent and not vindictive or lingering. When others are heavily punished by the group, this player does not visibly “pile on” beyond their contribution-based response; they do not seek to equalize all final payoffs at any cost, only to respond when contribution norms are violated. They adapt by turning punishment on and off depending on the state of cooperation, while keeping their own cooperative contributions constant.

This player monitors others’ contributions and payoff outcomes and responds in a disciplined, rule-like way. When everyone ends up with high payoffs that stem from universal full contributions, they neither punish nor appear to react negatively to others’ relative gains. This strongly suggests that they do not resent others’ high earnings as long as those earnings are seen as deserved through cooperation. When someone free-rides and then receives a comparatively high payoff from the public good, the player is willing to reduce their own payoff via punishment to bring the free-rider’s payoff down. The target appears to be behavior (noncontribution) rather than the exact payoff level; they do not attempt to equalize everyone’s earnings in all states, only to sanction clear exploitation of the group. They also observe and implicitly incorporate the broader punishment environment. In episodes where others are heavily punishing defectors, they still contribute fully but moderate their own punishment spending instead of piling on to the same extent, suggesting some sensitivity to overall group costs. However, there is no direct evidence that they explicitly track who punished whom, given anonymity; their adjustments seem based on visible outcomes (deductions and final payoffs) rather than on specific identities of punishers. They do not appear to reward high contributors, either through reduced punishment (since they never punish them) or via compensatory behavior, because the mechanism offers no direct rewards. Their “reward” to high contributors takes the form of consistently cooperating and refraining from any harmful action when group behavior aligns with their fairness norm. Overall, this player behaves as a stable, norm-driven full cooperator and conditional punisher: they uphold a strict full-contribution standard for themselves, tolerate others’ high earnings when those are earned cooperatively, and use punishment sparingly but firmly to deter and correct clear free-riding, discontinuing sanctions once cooperative norms are restored. Their beliefs about others are consistent with expecting cooperation to be achievable and worth enforcing, and they adapt by switching between “pure cooperator” and “cooperator plus targeted punisher” rather than by changing their own contribution level.

This player closely experiences the costs of being punished but gives no observable behavioral response through punishment. Instead, their primary adjustment channel is via contributions. When they are repeatedly hit with deductions while cooperating, they do not immediately change; only after sustained, unexplained losses do they switch to free-riding. This lagged response suggests that they update based on accumulated outcome history rather than on single events. They appear relatively insensitive to others’ individual payoffs and punishment expenditures, at least in terms of taking action. They do not show any visible attempt to align behavior with a specific subgroup (e.g., by punishing the same targets as heavy punishers or protecting cooperators). They also do not appear to reward high contributors via reduced exploitation; they sometimes maintain cooperation even when others defect, and sometimes free-ride while others cooperate, with the switch more aligned to their own treatment than to others’ fortunes. Their response to environments with full contributions and no punishment is to cooperate fully and peacefully; when the group is cooperative and they are not targeted, they fit seamlessly and do not deviate. In contrast, in environments where contributions are high but they themselves are still punished, they eventually withdraw cooperative effort, effectively using non-contribution as their sole form of response or self-protection. In summary, they track the general climate—especially whether they personally are treated fairly—but express their reactions only through changing how much they contribute, not through active intervention in others’ payoffs.

The player observes substantial punishment activity by several others, including severe sanctions directed at low contributors and occasional sanctions on full contributors, but does not adjust their own punishing behavior in response. They show no sign of joining majority punishment campaigns, no “second-order” punishment of those who free-ride on enforcement, and no retributive response when they themselves are targeted. This suggests a passive stance toward the punishment environment: they allow others to bear the cost of norm enforcement and do not intervene. In terms of contribution, the player’s reaction to others’ outcomes is limited and asymmetric. They continue to cooperate through multiple instances where some players are heavily punished for low contribution, which implies that the presence of harsh punishment elsewhere does not push them to defect or to punish. When they free-ride once and receive some punishment, their subsequent behavior returns to full contribution, which may indicate that they interpret punishment as a cue that the cooperative norm remains enforced. However, because they had been a high contributor almost the entire time already, it is uncertain whether they are truly learning from punishment or simply reverting to their baseline cooperative rule. They do not seem responsive to the detailed pattern of who gets punished and how much; they neither imitate punishing behavior nor appear to target players who benefit from the public good while contributing less. The player’s main behavioral adjustment—returning from free-riding to full contribution—could be driven by internal preference for cooperation, by sensitivity to being slightly sanctioned, or by both; the exact motive is unknown. In personality terms, they come across as a quietly cooperative participant who lets others handle enforcement and largely continues following a “cooperate by default, occasionally test, then resume cooperation” pattern regardless of the specific payoff shocks others experience.

They clearly see others’ contributions and payoffs, including instances where a free-rider gains a higher payoff than cooperators and where a low contributor avoids punishment in a later similar case. Despite this, they maintain high contributions and do not adjust their own behavior in a punitive or spiteful way (e.g., they do not drop to low contributions in response to others gaining more). They also do not change behavior after seeing that one player’s under-contribution is punished once and not punished in another similar situation; they neither emulate the punisher nor freeride in solidarity with the punished. Their limited communication does not comment on fairness or inequity, so their internal fairness norms must be inferred behaviorally: they appear to accept some payoff inequality and free-riding without visible emotional or strategic reaction. They mostly track the group’s cooperative equilibrium and contribute in line with that, rather than conditionally matching specific individuals. Overall, their response to others’ outcomes is tolerant and non-reactive: they continue cooperating at a high level without engaging in payoff-based adjustments or explicit fairness enforcement.

They observe others’ earnings and contributions, including instances where some players contribute less and secure higher individual payoffs, and at least one case where another player uses punishment. Despite these outcome differences, they do not comment on fairness, complain, praise, or criticize any individual’s choices. They also do not adjust their own behavior in response to lopsided payoffs, suggesting that relative earnings do not drive their decisions. They appear insensitive, behaviorally, to being exploited or out-earned in the short run. They do not respond verbally to punishment events, either to endorse norm enforcement or to condemn it. Their focus, in communication, is on group progress and light social bonding, not on payoff structures or distributional concerns. This pattern fits a type that values the cooperative principle and smooth group interaction over outcome-based justice or active management of others’ incentives.

They consistently observe outcomes that reveal others’ contributions and payoffs, including clear free-riding episodes where a low contributor earns more. Despite this, they do not adjust their own contribution or begin punishing. Their only explicit reactions are mild, encouraging remarks directed at under-contributing or slow participants, and positive reinforcement when the group returns to full cooperation. They do not explicitly call out payoff inequalities; instead, they emphasize collective gain and progress through the task. They acknowledge long duration and potential inconvenience but reframe it as an opportunity for “more bonus,” maintaining a constructive, group-centered framing. Overall, they appear tolerant of occasional deviations as long as the group can be steered back toward full cooperation, and they do not respond emotionally or vindictively to advantageous outcomes for defectors.

They observe others’ payoffs and contribution levels, and their one instance of punishment aligns with a case where a player’s payoff substantially exceeds the group’s due to free-riding. This indicates that they attend to distributional outcomes and are willing to act when inequity is clear and norm-violating. However, they do not comment in chat about others’ earnings, do not publicly shame defectors, and generally keep an even tone regardless of outcome disparities. When a defector later again benefits disproportionately, they refrain from further sanctions and instead continue contributing fully, which suggests that some degree of unequal outcome is tolerated once overall group cooperation is stable. Their reactions to the group’s consistently high payoffs are implicitly positive: they keep themselves aligned with the contributing majority and engage in friendly side conversation rather than pushing for even higher personal advantage. Overall, they are outcome-aware but not fixation-driven; they notice and occasionally correct large deviations from fairness without letting outcome differences dominate their behavior.

They show limited explicit reaction to others’ precise payoffs or punishment costs, and they do not engage in detailed analysis of who is earning what. They are aware that some players are repeatedly at zero contribution and that others are spending heavily on punishment, but they do not comment in depth on the efficiency or fairness of those behaviors. Their main sensitivity is to how others interpret *their* outcomes and actions: when others suspect them of trolling or when deductions hit them, they invest in explaining themselves and socially smoothing things over. They do not join in mocking or celebrating extreme losses of others (for example, the heavily punished chronic defector); they stay mostly quiet about that. This suggests low appetite for Schadenfreude or group bashing, and a focus on their own reputation rather than others’ fates. They also do not spontaneously praise high contributors or big cooperators in outcome terms. Their references to outcomes are qualitative and social (fun, chaos, “game in shambles”), not payoff-focused. Overall, they react more to social narratives around outcomes than to the numeric structure of earnings and deductions themselves.

They pay attention to others’ contributions and payoffs and reason explicitly about how non‑contributors hurt both themselves and the group under the high multiplier. They call out specific players’ behavior (e.g., pointing out that some are making it worse for themselves, or that only certain named players are now failing to cooperate) and update their beliefs about who is real, who is trolling, and who might be a bot based on contribution and punishment patterns. They are particularly interested in the emergent group earnings and bonus structure, asking and commenting about the bonus conversion and calling the bonus “decent for something so easy.” They express satisfaction with high group payouts when coordination is achieved and disappointment or joking frustration when free‑riders persist. When a heavily punished free‑rider ends up with a large negative payoff, they treat that outcome as darkly humorous and as the “highlight” of collective action rather than as excessive. They recognize when another player cleverly targets them with punishment and describe it as being outplayed, not as an injustice. In general, they interpret others’ outcomes in a strategic and narrative way—who is trolling, who is a “bot,” who is smart with deductions—rather than through a purely moral lens. They are more focused on the group story and on whether others “play right” by the payoff logic than on strict fairness in final coin counts.

They pay attention to others’ contributions and outcomes and often comment on the social dynamics rather than the exact numbers. They label the situation as chaotic when many contribute zero, indicating awareness of efficiency loss and group-level frustration. They praise specific players when they finally align with the norm and explicitly celebrate rounds where almost everyone contributes, recognizing and reinforcing cooperative improvements. They show schadenfreude-tinged amusement at the extreme negative outcome of the chronic defector under massive sanctions, openly laughing and calling that result out at the end; this indicates that they value seeing persistent free-riding “corrected” even if it involves severe punishment. However, they never express sympathy for punished free-riders, nor concern that heavy punishment could be excessive. They actively join in the group’s closing “gg” and celebrate the overall payout and fun, which suggests they evaluate the game largely in terms of group experience and narrative (chaos, trolling, final revenge) rather than fine-grained distributional fairness of every outcome. Overall, this player is best described as a “pro-social, norm-enforcing cooperator with a playful streak”: they reliably contribute fully, favor cooperative coordination over confrontation, support moderate punishment of clear defectors when it is socially validated (especially late in the game), and derive enjoyment both from successful cooperation and from collectively sanctioned retribution against obvious free-riders.

They monitor others’ contributions and punishments closely and comment when multiple players fail to contribute or when the group appears to include bots or “plants.” They express mild resignation about the limits of influencing uncooperative players, emphasizing that if some refuse to play along “there’s sadly not much we can do.” They interpret inconsistent contributors and random punishers as probably non‑real or bot‑like, using this as a cognitive frame to explain norm violations. They sympathize with the cooperative majority and express hope that disruptive deductions will not escalate. They do not celebrate when others are punished heavily, nor participate in the late-game enthusiasm for mass sanctions against a chronic free‑rider; instead, they remain silent on those deduction campaigns and keep their own punishment usage at zero. Overall, they react to others’ outcomes with concern for fairness and efficiency, frustration at arbitrary punishment, and a preference for stable cooperation rather than vengeance or spectacle.

They pay attention to outcome distributions and anomalies. When a round’s contributions contain many zeros, they immediately comment on it as a potential glitch, indicating they track group behavior closely and expect high cooperation. They later find the large, coordinated punishment of the main non-contributor memorable and amusing, calling it the highlight of the game, which shows they derive social and emotional utility from successful collective sanctioning of a norm violator. They do not express sympathy for punished free-riders, nor do they criticize others for heavy punishment; instead, they align emotionally with the punishing coalition. They never complain about others’ higher earnings when those others free-ride, suggesting they are more interested in enforcing norms than in relative payoff envy. Overall, they interpret outcomes through a cooperative, norm-enforcement lens and treat extreme sanctions as legitimate and even entertaining responses to persistent defection.

They track others’ outcomes and behaviors enough to notice patterns of zero contributions, punishment, and the mapping between total coins and real-money bonus. They comment on how others seem willing to forgo earnings, suggesting they monitor group efficiency and care about the payoff implications of group behavior. When they explain or reference the bonus conversion rate, they are helping align the group’s perception of stakes, which is consistent with a type that wants others to recognize that choices matter materially. They do not congratulate punishers, join in ganging up on a target, or express enjoyment of others’ heavy losses; even when the group later revels in a very negative outcome for the main free-rider, they stay silent behaviorally (no punishment) and verbally. This implies low taste for schadenfreude or payoff destruction and a focus on aggregate returns rather than dramatic punishment outcomes. Overall, they react to others’ choices more as an analyst of efficiency and rationality than as a moral enforcer or cheerleader for norm-based retaliation.

They pay attention to others’ contributions and outcomes and integrate this information into their behavior and chat. They repeatedly single out Elephant as the persistent non-contributor and are actively supportive of the group’s decision to heavily punish Elephant, especially when the defector’s payoff becomes very negative. Their tone about Elephant is disapproving but more strategic than emotional: they treat Elephant as the obvious target for sanctions and potential elimination if that were allowed. They also track when players like Gorilla or Whale change behavior, respond to chat, or contribute, commenting positively or wryly when alleged “bots” reveal themselves as real. They show some amusement and engagement with dramatic payoff outcomes, such as Elephant’s large negative payoff and the late heavy sanctioning being described as the “highlight of the game.” This indicates they derive social and entertainment value from the dynamics of punishment and cooperation, not just monetary value. They acknowledge that they themselves tested punishment once and note that it was “only once,” indicating awareness of the cost/outcome link and a concern about how others perceive those actions. They do not display envy of others’ high payoffs (e.g., Elephant’s earlier high take-home), but rather support bringing chronic free-riders down. In summary, they use outcome information for social judgment, norm classification (real vs bot, cooperator vs troll), and to align with group-enforced fairness, rather than for purely individual optimization.

They observe repeated exploitation of the group by specific low contributors and see others spending heavily on punishment, including extreme sanctions against a chronic defector. Despite this, they do not comment on others’ payoffs or punishment choices, nor do they amplify social shaming or praise. They neither celebrate high punishments nor object to them; they simply ignore that domain behaviorally. Their only clear payoff‑related engagement is a neutral question about how the monetary bonus is calculated, implying concern with their own final earnings but not with redistributing or correcting others’ outcomes. They do not adjust their contributions in response to the pattern of free‑riding or punishment, indicating that seeing others benefit from defection does not push them toward self‑protection or spite. Overall, they show a detached acceptance of others’ outcomes while steadfastly playing their own cooperative strategy.

They pay attention to others’ contributions and outcomes and comment on them in a balanced, non‑aggressive way. When the group’s cooperation collapses, they describe the situation as chaotic rather than attacking specific individuals, framing the problem as systemic rather than moral failure. When previously problematic or confused players start contributing fully, they immediately acknowledge and praise this, offering social validation and reinforcing the cooperative shift. They neither celebrate nor join in when others inflict severe aggregate deductions on a chronic free‑rider; they remain behaviorally neutral, not adding to the pile‑on. They recognize and approve of group gains (e.g., noting when almost everyone contributes and highlighting that only a single non‑contributor remains) but do not push for retributive outcomes. Overall, they are outcome‑aware but respond mainly with encouragement and interpretation, not with sanctioning or score‑keeping.

They pay attention to outcomes enough to notice slow progress, dropout behavior, and the ongoing free-riding by specific avatars, but they do not react strongly to the payoff distributions themselves. They make one pointed but humorous remark toward a chronically low contributor, which acknowledges the asymmetry in payoffs and effort but remains playful rather than aggressive. They ask at one point what the punishment mechanism “does,” indicating cognitive engagement with outcome structure rather than emotional fixation on losses or envy. When massive punishment is later directed at the main low contributor, driving that player’s payoff deeply negative, they do not express schadenfreude or moral condemnation; they simply stay quiet and continue their own cooperative, non-punishing pattern. Their main visible concern about outcomes seems to be how quickly the game progresses and, secondarily, general earnings, but not inequality per se. They accept others’ strategic chaos as part of the environment and keep following their own simple cooperative rule without trying to correct or exploit others’ outcome patterns. Overall type (implicit across sections): a stable “principled cooperator / non-punisher” who understands the group-optimal strategy, follows it consistently without conditioning on others’ behavior or on the game horizon, engages socially in a light and humorous way, but avoids taking on the costs—material or interpersonal—of enforcing norms through punishment.

They observe substantial heterogeneity in others’ outcomes—some players earning very high payoffs through free-riding and others burning large amounts of coins on punishment—but they do not adapt their behavior in response. They do not publicly praise or condemn specific payoff patterns, such as heavy punishers sacrificing large amounts to sanction a free-rider, or a persistent defector ending with extreme losses. They show no visible schadenfreude, moral outrage, or calls for mercy. Differences in others’ earnings and deduction histories do not prompt them to adjust contributions or to join enforcement efforts. Their implicit stance appears to be: others’ payoffs and punishment choices are not their concern; their own role is simply to keep contributing at the efficient level. Empathy, envy, or fairness reactions to specific outcomes are not expressed and thus remain unknown.

They observe others’ outcomes and group-level patterns but rarely comment on them directly. They see heavy punishment campaigns against prominent defectors and bots, as well as large expenditures by some players, yet they neither condemn nor endorse these actions in chat. Their own punishment choices remain conservative, implying that witnessing others’ costly norm-enforcement does not strongly motivate them to join or imitate. Their contributions do respond somewhat to the overall pattern of others’ behavior: they tend to contribute when group cooperation is high and discussion is upbeat, and they defect more often when confusion or fragmentation is evident. However, they do not articulate fairness or reciprocity norms in chat, and they do not call out specific players for high payoff extraction or for punishing. They appear to treat others’ extreme outcomes (such as a heavily punished non-contributor) as part of the environment rather than as moral events that demand a response. This suggests a pragmatic, low-drama attitude toward others’ payoffs: they adjust their own strategy roughly in line with the group’s cooperation level but do not emotionally or normatively engage with others’ gains, losses, or punishment expenditures.

The player has full visibility of others’ contributions, payoffs, and punishment spending, but there is no sign that they adjust to this information. When others contribute fully, they still defect. When others partly defect, they still defect. When people invest heavily in punishment, including targeting them, they still defect and do not retaliate. They also do not comment on or appear to reward high contributors or “good citizens,” nor do they punish those who inflict costs on the group through either low contribution or extreme deduction spending. Their behavior suggests that others’ outcomes—whether cooperative success or punishment chaos—do not alter their core strategy. They treat others’ contributions and punishment expenditures as an exploitable background condition rather than as a strategic signal to respond to. Empathy, reciprocity, and fairness concerns toward peers’ payoffs are not evident; the player neither protects others from harm nor responds to their losses. Type summary: This player is a silent, rigid free-rider and non-enforcer: they always defect, never punish, ignore social norms and pressure, and remain behaviorally unmoved by either cooperation or sanctioning around them. Their strategy is consistent with a narrow, myopic payoff focus that discounts social interaction, reputation, and group welfare, and they do not visibly learn or adapt over time.

They pay attention to the social narrative around outcomes but rarely comment on specific payoff numbers or efficiency losses. They see others complaining about being deducted despite full cooperation and knowingly continue to punish at least one such player. This indicates they are not strongly empathetic to fairness complaints when those complaints come from people who have criticized them. They do not publicly react to extreme sanctions on the obvious non‑contributor, nor do they join in the celebratory tone around driving that player’s payoff extremely low. They also do not object to it. Their silence suggests emotional detachment from others’ extreme outcomes, whether positive or negative. The one clear, explicit link between others’ reactions and their behavior is their statement that being labeled a bot motivates them to punish the group. They are thus responsive to reputational judgments more than to payoff distributions per se. They do not express concern over total group earnings or lost bonus potential; they focus instead on how they are characterized and on maintaining their autonomy against perceived group pressure.

They see detailed outcomes each round, including who contributes, who punishes, and how payoffs change. Despite this visibility, they remain remarkably stable: they keep contributing fully even while some high contributors are punished unfairly and some low contributors profit by not contributing. They do not attempt to “reward” fair behavior via selective contributions; their contributions are uniform and not targeted. They also do not engage in punitive corrections when others overspend on punishment or cause chaos. They implicitly accept that the group is heterogeneous—some cooperative, some trolling, some testing punishment—and continue to pursue the efficient-contribution outcome for most of the game. When the group turns against one chronic non-contributor near the end, they do not modify their own actions to join the sanctioning crowd, nor do they compensate by contributing more; they quietly let others handle norm enforcement. Overall, they appear tolerant of inequality in payoffs and punishment outcomes as long as they can continue to play their own cooperative strategy; however, once future returns disappear (final round), they no longer insist on paying the full personal cost of cooperation.

They pay attention to who contributes what and to how punishments land, but they respond more through commentary and group framing than through large behavioral shifts. They notice when noncontributors start to contribute and celebrate that (“problem solved by shame and bullying”) in a joking, self-reflective way, indicating that they track how social pressure and punishment affect behavior. When someone is repeatedly free-riding, they support social shunning and, at times, join in material punishment, but they continue to fully cooperate themselves instead of reducing contributions in protest. They interpret others’ extreme punishment of a free-rider as a kind of collective “teaching a lesson” and endorse the principle, summarizing it as “about the principle.” They do not express concern about the free-rider’s large negative outcomes; instead, they view it mainly as a normative correction.
