Any emotional responses (e.g., frustration, resignation, moral satisfaction) are unobservable; all that can be inferred is that their contribution strategy is remarkably resilient to persistent outcome inequalities and to clear evidence that uncooperative strategies can be more individually profitable in this environment.

The player observes others’ contributions and payoffs but reacts in a muted, non-punitive way since no formal punishment or reward is available. Their adjustments are modest: they occasionally switch to free riding in environments where others’ contributions remain high and payoffs from defection are clearly superior, but they do not launch prolonged or coordinated “counterattacks” on low contributors. They tolerate the presence of consistent defectors in the group and still often choose to contribute fully even when those defectors earn higher payoffs. This indicates that inequality in outcomes alone does not compel them to abandon cooperation. Their main response to others’ outcomes appears to be opportunistic rather than moralistic: they sometimes copy the logic of free riding when it is obviously profitable, yet they do not pursue a strict reciprocity rule of “you defect, so I defect until you change.” There is no sign of strong spite or envy; they do not remain in a permanently non-cooperative mode after seeing others gain from free riding. Instead, they revert to cooperative choices even after rounds in which defectors are clearly better off. This suggests a personality style that is relatively forgiving, pragmatic, and efficiency-oriented, with only mild responsiveness to exploitation rather than a strong drive to enforce fairness or equal payoffs. Because direct punishment and reward are absent, their “response” to others’ outcomes manifests entirely through their own contribution choices. Within that constraint, they display a blend of cooperative bias and opportunistic adaptation, but with limited emotional reactivity inferred from the data (true emotional state: unknown, low confidence).

The player’s behavior is strikingly insensitive to others’ outcomes and contribution patterns. Across periods where others’ payoffs are higher due to free-riding or lower due to full cooperation, the focal player’s contribution remains unchanged. They do not reduce contributions when others earn more by contributing less, nor do they ramp up from an already-maximal level when others become more cooperative; they are already at the maximum and remain there. This indicates an absence of observable envy or competitive adjustment: higher payoffs for defectors do not trigger retaliation via reduced giving. It also indicates no visible effort to reward cooperators by changing their own behavior; with no punishment or reward mechanisms available, their only lever would have been to adjust contributions, which they do not use conditionally. Their underlying beliefs about others are unknown, but their pattern is consistent with one of the following: (a) they hold a strong personal norm to contribute fully regardless of others’ choices; (b) they focus primarily on aggregate efficiency and treat others’ free-riding as secondary; or (c) they follow a fixed heuristic that does not incorporate feedback about others’ payoffs. There is no sign of learning, calibration, or strategic adaptation in response to shifts in group cooperation levels. In interpersonal terms, this maps onto a highly forgiving, non-vengeful style: they continue to provide the public good even when others systematically benefit more by contributing less, and they show no observable attempt to discipline or coordinate the group through changes in their own actions.

The player sees that free-riders consistently earn more than contributors in many rounds, and that this pattern persists over time. Despite regularly being on the losing side of this payoff comparison, they very rarely adjust to mirror the more profitable free-riding strategy. Their occasional defections appear as limited, low-frequency responses to the experience of being exploited, but there is no sustained attempt to equalize payoffs or retaliate against low contributors. Once these brief tests are over, they revert to full contribution even though payoff inequalities remain and some peers continue to free-ride. This suggests that envy-based or disadvantage-averse motives are present, if at all, only weakly. Because explicit punishment and reward mechanisms are absent, all responses must be via own contributions. The player does not use this indirect channel aggressively to discipline the group: they do not reduce contributions in a way that tracks particular individuals’ behavior or the aggregate defection level in a stable manner. Instead, their main “response” to others’ outcomes is to keep contributing at a high level regardless of persistent inequity. In belief terms (inferred, low confidence), they likely either (a) accept that some will free-ride but still prioritize maximizing total group output, or (b) have a simple rule like “I should contribute” that they follow even when they see that others gain more by not doing so. Their actions do not show clear evidence of sophisticated payoff-based belief updating about others; rather, the behavioral pattern is consistent with a principled or rule-based cooperator who only occasionally tests the alternative when inequity is salient but then returns to cooperation.

The player observes full contribution profiles and payoffs of others but does not respond with strong conditional moves tied to specific outcomes. When others’ free-riding yields them visibly higher payoffs, the player does not consistently mirror that behavior; they continue to cooperate more often than not, indicating relatively low spite and low envy. They accept repeated situations where defectors earn more than contributors without escalating into persistent defection, which points to a relatively forgiving or outcome-tolerant mindset. Their occasional defections do not neatly track any single other’s payoff advantage or a sudden deterioration in group contributions, making it unlikely that they are closely tracking individual fairness or attempting to punish particular patterns through their own contribution level. Instead, they seem more attuned to the general environment being “cooperative enough” and are willing to maintain contributions in the presence of some exploitation. There is no direct mechanism for punishment or reward in this game, and the player does not attempt to approximate these through extreme shifts in their own contributions in response to others’ payoffs. Overall, they show mild sensitivity to the cooperative climate but limited reactivity to fine-grained payoff differences between players. Their response style is best described as broadly cooperative, lightly opportunistic, and only weakly driven by observed inequality.

The player observes that non-contributors consistently earn more than contributors in mixed groups and behaves in line with that payoff logic. They are willing to free-ride while many others contribute, accepting that many cooperative peers receive lower payoffs than they do. There is no sign that disadvantageous or advantageous inequality on others’ side triggers a systematic change in their own choices. They do not appear to “reward” cooperative groups by reliably increasing their contributions when total payoffs are high, nor do they appear to “discipline” defectors by contributing more when cooperation falls. Instead, they maintain their basic pattern across a range of group outcomes: persistent willingness to take the private benefit of non-contribution, occasionally choosing to contribute despite it lowering their own payoff relative to free-riding. Because there are no punishment or reward mechanisms in this environment, their response to others’ outcomes is visible only through changes in their own contributions. These changes are modest and do not track any clear threshold of group behavior or payoff distribution. Any sensitivity to others’ earnings or fairness norms is weak or ambiguous: they do not consistently align their strategy with equalizing payoffs, nor do they systematically chase maximum relative advantage beyond what non-contribution already provides. In new PGG environments, this implies a player who notices payoff structures and acts on them primarily to protect or increase their own earnings, with only limited and inconsistent adjustment based on how well or poorly others are doing.

This player visibly observes others’ outcomes, but there is little fine-grained behavioral adjustment that tracks specific payoff patterns. They see that free-riders systematically earn more than contributors in mixed rounds, including times when they themselves are on the losing side of that comparison. Despite this repeated evidence, they mostly continue to cooperate rather than pivot into a sustained free-riding strategy. Occasional free-riding episodes do align with states where others are contributing at relatively high rates and receiving lower payoffs than free-riders. In those moments, they exploit the cooperative environment and momentarily join the higher-earning free-riders. However, they do not then “lock in” to defection after seeing its profitability; instead, they revert to cooperation. This suggests that, while they notice and sometimes act on the payoff advantage of defection, they are not purely payoff-maximizing in response to outcomes. Because explicit punishment and reward tools are absent, we cannot observe their willingness to incur costs to shape others’ outcomes; this aspect of their type is unknown. Indirectly, their willingness to keep contributing after repeatedly observing exploitative peers indicates some tolerance for inequality and exploitation. They do not appear to engage in informal “punishment via abandonment” of the public good in any sustained way. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes with only weak strategic adaptation: they sometimes exploit a cooperative state but largely continue to bear the costs of cooperation even when it is objectively dominated by free-riding in the short term. This is consistent with a player who values cooperation and is only loosely guided by immediate payoff signals.

The player observes others’ outcomes and can see that non-contributors often earn more in individual rounds than full contributors. Despite this, they never adjust their own behavior to mimic freeriders, which indicates that relative payoff comparisons do not drive their decisions in this setting. They do not react in a retaliatory manner when others consistently benefit from their cooperation. There is no behavioral sign of resentment, envy, or a desire to restore payoff equality through reduced contributions. Similarly, they do not strategically reward cooperative peers via higher future contributions, since their own contribution is already at the maximum and remains unchanged regardless of others’ patterns. Because no punishment or reward mechanisms are available, direct responses via sanctions or bonuses cannot be observed. Indirect responses (through changing contribution amounts) are also absent. Thus, their response to others’ outcomes is best characterized as non-reactive: they register no observable behavioral change when others exploit the public good, when cooperation rises, or when outcome distributions shift. From this, the inferred type is someone who maintains a fixed cooperative rule even under visible exploitation and unequal payoffs. They appear outcome-tolerant and norm- or rule-focused, rather than payoff- or reciprocity-focused. Their learning or adaptation to the group’s contribution profile is either minimal or nonexistent within the observed environment.

The player monitors the partner’s contribution behavior and at least initially reacts when something seems off, asking for clarification. Once mutual full cooperation is established, they do not comment on payoffs or relative earnings, even though they can see outcomes. They show no expressed concern with distributional fairness beyond maintaining mutual contribution; as long as both are cooperating, they do not invoke fairness or complain. When the partner defects at the end and earns strictly more, the player does not adjust behavior (their contribution remains full) or comment on the inequity, showing a high tolerance for being exploited in this one-shot end situation or a reluctance to introduce conflict. They also ignore the reward channel that could be used to signal approval or disapproval. Overall, they are attentive enough to notice behavior changes but refrain from punitive or confrontational responses, prioritizing cooperative stability and politeness over payoff enforcement.

The player clearly monitors others’ contributions and likely infers their payoff implications, but there is no sign of reacting to the fairness or unfairness of the reward system with counter-rewards or with punish-like behavior (which is not available anyway). They continue to cooperate strongly despite seeing one player often contributing little and still earning good payoffs, as well as seeing another player spend heavily on rewards in ways that do not strictly track contribution levels. Over time, however, as non-cooperative or erratic behavior persists, they reduce their own contributions and finally free-ride while others still contribute substantially. This suggests that others’ outcomes influence their willingness to keep being a “workhorse”: when they perceive that their high contributions are not matched by a stable, efficient group pattern, they eventually abandon high cooperation and focus on maximizing their own payoff. They show no visible concern for redistributing payoffs via rewards and do not appear to react aimfully to others overspending on rewards; their main adjustment lever is their own contribution level, not any costly response to how others allocate or receive rewards.

They consistently observe that some players earn higher payoffs while contributing less and that others actively spend on rewards, but they do not change either their contributions or their use of reward in response. They do not try to compensate disadvantaged high contributors, do not sanction or withhold special treatment from low contributors (beyond the universal refusal to reward anyone), and do not appear to chase relative payoff standing. Their behavior suggests indifference to payoff rankings and to how others allocate resources via reward, focusing solely on their own cooperative rule. They treat others’ outcomes and spending patterns as non‑diagnostic for how they themselves should play. Any internal emotional reaction (resentment, approval, confusion, or detachment) is unknown; behaviorally, they remain fixed in their initial cooperative but non‑interventionist stance.

The player observes others’ contributions and payoffs and adjusts behavior in ways consistent with payoff‑oriented, conditional cooperation, but they never comment explicitly on outcomes. When others are relatively heterogeneous in their contributions, they take an intermediate stance, avoiding both extreme cooperation and pure free‑riding. When several peers declare higher cooperative intentions and some follow through with substantially larger contributions, the player initially exploits this by contributing less while still benefiting from the group’s generosity, thereby increasing their own payoff. Later, once high cooperation is clearly established across the group, they fully match that cooperative level. This suggests that they positively respond to strong cooperative norms when implementation is visible, but they do so without any observable moralistic response to earlier deviations (their own or others’). There is no textual evidence of praising high contributors, criticizing low contributors, or expressing satisfaction or frustration with payoff distributions; affective reactions to inequality or exploitation remain unknown. Overall, their response to others’ outcomes is primarily behavioral and strategic rather than verbal: they opportunistically under‑contribute when others are credibly cooperating, then converge toward the cooperative pattern as it becomes the group standard. They show no attempt to socially reward or punish through communication, and given the absence of explicit sanction tools in the game, their only lever is their own contribution level, which they use adaptively rather than norm‑enforcingly.

This player’s behavioral adjustments reflect close attention to others’ contributions and payoffs, but they do not express any explicit emotional or moral reaction in chat. When others contribute more and end up worse off or more vulnerable, the player does not move to “compensate”; instead, they maintain or refine a payoff-advantaged position. When the group’s behavior is inconsistent or includes very low contributions, the player stays guarded and continues to secure a relatively high payoff. Once others converge on very high, coordinated contributions, the player reciprocates more substantially but still retains a slight self-serving edge, indicating a preference to share in the gains without fully equalizing outcomes. There is no evidence of punitive sentiment, resentment, gratitude, or attempts to shame or reward others—only quiet adaptation of their own strategy in response to observed patterns. Their type is best characterized as an observant, payoff-maximizing conditional cooperator who updates behavior based on revealed group norms, but who shows no overt concern for correcting inequities or aligning payoffs perfectly.

The player visibly tolerates inequity in their own payoff without altering their contribution behavior, but they respond to others’ outcomes mainly through the punishment channel. When others gain from contributing less, they do not copy this strategy; instead, they sometimes increase their use of costly sanctions. This indicates that they care about the structure of outcomes—who benefits and whether that benefit is deserved—rather than just their own absolute payoff. They also experience episodes where they themselves are net losers due to incoming punishment. Despite this, they do not shift toward non-cooperation or withdraw from the game; instead, they maintain full contribution and continue to punish in some later rounds. This suggests they interpret being punished not as a cue to renegotiate norms by defecting, but as either noise to be absorbed or as part of a broader punitive environment in which they still want to uphold high cooperation. Their behavior is consistent with a “moralist cooperator” type: they want high group contributions, are willing to accept personal loss, and use punishment as their main adjustment to others’ payoffs when they see unfair advantage or exploitation. Because individual punishment links are hidden, how precisely they track specific others’ histories is unknown. However, their continued use of punishment in periods where deviations are present implies they attend to others’ behavior and are willing to act on it, even late in the game and even when it does not obviously improve their own earnings.

The player continues to cooperate even when others’ payoffs differ sharply due to punishment. They observe that some low contributors get hammered with large deductions while some high contributors preserve or increase their earnings, yet they do not swing into either extreme mimicry or spite. Heavy punishment campaigns by others, including chaotic or extremely costly ones, do not lure them into joining large-scale “punishment frenzies” or abandoning their cooperative behavior. When they are targeted and lose income despite contributing fully, they do not respond by reducing contributions for more than a single brief episode, and they do not launch obvious revenge punishment against the group. This implies some tolerance for being treated unfairly and a reluctance to destabilize the public good in response. They seem to interpret extreme outcomes (very low payoffs from heavy punishment) as noise or as the product of others’ strategies rather than as a signal to abandon cooperation. Their own occasional punishment coincides with visible under-contribution and with those players already being subject to sanctions from others, suggesting that they attend to outcome patterns (who benefits from the group while giving little, who repeatedly free-rides) and are willing to add some pressure to align behavior. However, the relatively low frequency and intensity of their sanctions suggests they do not feel compelled to correct every inequity they observe; they tolerate some unfairness and prioritize maintaining their cooperative norm over equalizing payoffs across players. In sum, this player is a principled, resilient cooperator who lightly supports punishment norms, absorbs some unfair losses without major behavioral shifts, and keeps focusing on group efficiency and self-consistent cooperation rather than on outcome equalization or revenge.

They pay attention to others’ contributions and eventual payoffs, and they adjust punishment targets in line with observed undercontribution and apparent free-riding. When others’ payoffs are boosted by low contributions combined with the group’s high contributions, this player is more likely to impose deductions, indicating sensitivity to perceived unfair earnings advantages rather than to absolute payoff levels alone. They do not seem to reward high contributors (no positive mechanism is available) nor do they refrain from punishing when defectors already earn relatively low payoffs: they are willing to push low contributors into very poor outcomes, suggesting that they value norm compliance over mercy once someone is classified as a free-rider. They show no evidence of punishing high contributors or players who simply spend a lot on punishment themselves; they avoid “antisocial” or competitive punishment against cooperators. This selective pattern indicates they track the structure of outcomes: who is benefiting from the public good without paying their share. They do not visibly soften their stance when some low contributors are already heavily punished by others; they sometimes add their own punishment on top of existing sanctions, indicating a preference for reinforcing group norms rather than economizing on enforcement once others are already punishing. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes in a way consistent with a fairness- and norm-based social preference: they accept and support high payoffs for contributors, oppose windfall gains for free-riders, and are willing to incur repeated costs to align others’ outcomes with their cooperative standard. Their learning and adaptation appear limited: they refine targets in line with contribution patterns but maintain the same basic cooperative and punitive stance throughout the game.

They observe others receiving varied payoffs and experiencing substantial punishment costs, with some players frequently burning large amounts of coins to sanction others. Despite this, they do not alter their main strategy of free-riding or adopt punishment themselves. Being one of the most heavily punished players overall, they repeatedly see their own payoffs reduced by sizable deductions while cooperative players often end rounds with higher, more stable payoffs. Yet, they only occasionally respond with temporary full cooperation, and even then, they quickly revert to non-contribution. This suggests that: - They are relatively insensitive to social sanctions as a learning signal. - They place primary weight on immediate, high-payoff opportunities rather than on maintaining a reputation or avoiding future punishment. They also show no “emotional” behavioral markers, such as retaliatory punishment or a sharp, sustained switch to cooperation after extreme targeting. Their pattern is flat: absorb punishment, keep free-riding, occasionally contribute fully, then revert. This indicates a low responsiveness to others’ outcomes and actions beyond minimal, short-lived experimentation, and a persistent orientation toward personal payoff over group welfare or reciprocity. In sum, this player type is a punishment-averse, non-retaliatory free-rider with high tolerance for being sanctioned, weak social norm internalization, and limited behavioral adjustment to others’ contributions or payoffs.

They react more to others’ contribution decisions than to their final payoffs per se. High payoffs of cooperators do not trigger punitive responses; they coexist comfortably with others earning as much or more than themselves when those others contribute at or near the full amount. Conversely, low contributors who still end up with decent or even high net payoffs continue to receive punishment from them, indicating that perceived unfairness in contribution, not earnings level alone, drives their sanctions. They show resilience in the face of receiving punishment. Being punished themselves does not cause them to stop contributing or to dramatically change their sanctioning pattern; they largely maintain their cooperative behavior and moderate enforcement. This suggests a relatively stable strategy rather than short-run emotional reactivity. They also display some learning or updating: after exploring defection and seeing that it brings punishment and lower net payoff relative to cooperative peers, they revert to high contributions. Their punishment intensity remains bounded and does not escalate in response to the chaotic and sometimes extreme punishment behaviors of certain others. This implies they interpret such outcomes as noise or overreaction rather than as something to mirror. Overall, their type is: a principled, conditional cooperator who is willing to bear real costs to sustain cooperation and punish free-riding, but who becomes somewhat more opportunistic and experimentally self-interested as the game approaches its end, without devolving into pure defection or indiscriminate punishment.

This player appears relatively insensitive in their own decisions to the detailed pattern of others’ payoffs, punishment expenditures, or deductions. They observe a group where some participants spend heavily on punishment (sometimes to self-harming extremes) and where several low contributors are repeatedly, sometimes harshly, sanctioned. Despite this, they neither join in punishment nor change their own contribution pattern in any sustained way. They do not seem to reward “nice” players or protect punished cooperators via any available mechanism; with no reward tool in the game and no punishment from them, their only lever is their own contribution, which they keep high regardless of who is winning or losing. This implies that other players’ specific payoffs do not directly steer their actions. They also do not visibly avoid exploitation of others when they briefly defect: they free-ride once at a time when others are mostly contributing, suggesting at least some willingness to capitalize on others’ cooperative behavior, tempered by their overwhelmingly cooperative track record. When they are personally punished for deviating, they appear to interpret this as a strong signal: they immediately return to full cooperation and stay there. This is the clearest instance where others’ outcomes (their own deduction and the existence of sanctions) influence their behavior. The effect is corrective rather than retaliatory. They do not escalate conflict but instead restore norm-conforming contributions. Their overall response style is thus: they are robust to noise and chaos in others’ use of punishment, stick to a cooperative norm almost regardless of distributional outcomes, and, when sanctioned, respond by re-aligning with the cooperative standard rather than by punishing back or defecting further. Their beliefs about the precise fairness or justice of specific punishment episodes are unknown, but their behavior fits a cooperative, non-confrontational, low-enforcement persona who lets others handle conflict while focusing on their own consistent contribution choices.

They observe large, frequent punishment waves and large payoff swings among others but remain behaviorally calm and mostly unchanged in their own sanction behavior. Players who heavily punish or get heavily punished do not elicit a clear shift in this player’s strategy: they neither join in collective punishment campaigns nor protect or shield any specific individuals. Their punishment response to others’ outcomes is essentially flat. Their contributions are also largely insensitive to others’ payoffs and punishment costs. They continue to cooperate even when free-riders appear to escape sanction or when punishers burn substantial resources going after them. This suggests that they do not treat observed punishment success or failure as a strong learning signal. Collective norms or “coalitions” that might be inferred from others’ mutual punishment patterns do not seem to guide their behavior. The one consistent way they react to the environment is through their own experience of unfair treatment: after being punished while behaving cooperatively, they become more willing to defect in some later rounds. This indicates that they monitor how they themselves are treated more than how others’ payoffs evolve, and adjust their willingness to contribute accordingly. They do not retaliate against specific offenders (since punishers are anonymous), but they do seem to reduce their “gift” to the group after being harmed. Overall, this is a norm-driven, high-cooperation, low-punishment type: a “quiet cooperator” who values contributing and efficiency, is tolerant of others’ free-riding and of noisy sanctioning, avoids costly punishment almost entirely, and only occasionally withdraws cooperation as a personal response to being treated unfairly, rather than as an end-game or payoff-maximizing calculation.

They observe others’ outcomes and appear sensitive to payoff asymmetries, but they do not express concern when they are the one earning more via free-riding. When another player systematically benefits from under-contributing, they call that player “mean” in a joking way at the end, indicating recognition of exploitation and a mild negative judgment, tempered by humor. Their adjustment to others’ actions is asymmetric: they enthusiastically support full cooperation in chat and contribute fully when others are doing so, but they also exploit these same contributions by occasionally giving much less. They do not express guilt or obligation to compensate those they just exploited, and they do not use communication to apologize or justify their own opportunism. This suggests that fairness norms are applied more strongly to others than to self. They respond positively when others align with the cooperative plan (e.g., commenting approvingly when everyone hits the cooperative target) and seem to value the aesthetic or emotional satisfaction of seeing coordinated high contributions. Yet they do not escalate against defectors via hostility or withdrawal from the group; instead, they rely on light teasing and continued willingness to cooperate in later rounds. With no punishment or reward tools available, their main response channel is social signaling, which remains friendly and relatively low-intensity.

The player repeatedly experiences situations where mutual full contributions give equal, high payoffs, and situations where uneven contributions produce unequal payoffs. They continue interacting with the group without visible emotional or strategic overreaction to inequality in either direction. When others free‑ride more than they do, they do not retaliate verbally; any adjustment appears only as later contribution changes, which are also mixed and not clearly targeted at any one individual. When they themselves earn higher payoffs due to under‑contributing relative to others, they do not express guilt or attempt to compensate via chat; they sometimes return to full cooperation afterward, but this looks more like rejoining a profitable group pattern than repairing perceived unfairness. They do not comment on others’ outcomes and offer no social feedback, positive or negative. Since punishment and reward are structurally unavailable, their only channel of reaction is future contribution choices. These choices suggest that they notice and exploit opportunities but do not commit to sustained punishment via long‑term defection when others deviate. Their sensitivity to others’ payoffs appears moderate and instrumental rather than moralistic: they care enough to adjust contributions opportunistically, but not enough to enforce norms or voice concerns.

In this environment, no explicit punishment or reward mechanisms are used, and this player never has the option to spend on sanctions. Their reactions must be inferred from contribution adjustments and chat rather than from direct payoff modifications. They appear to track how others’ outcomes respond to group contribution patterns and explicitly reason that mutual high contributions maximize everyone’s payoffs. They do not overtly complain when others earn more by free-riding, nor do they single out specific players as targets of criticism. Instead, their behavioral response to others’ gains from low contributions is to sometimes lower their own contributions later, effectively protecting themselves from being the consistent “sucker” without escalating into visible conflict. When others benefit disproportionately from contributing less during a cooperative phase, the player does not verbally retaliate but becomes more variable and, at times, exploitative themselves. This indicates a *quiet reciprocity and payoff-monitoring* style: they notice distributional consequences and adjust, but they refrain from direct social confrontation. Their closing remarks remain positive despite unequal outcomes in the final round, suggesting they do not prioritize retribution or moral accounting over keeping a congenial atmosphere once the game concludes.

They observe substantial chaos in others’ outcomes: some players burn enormous amounts of coins on punishment, others receive very heavy deductions, and several experience dramatically reduced or even negative payoffs in certain rounds. Throughout this, the focal player does not adjust in ways that mirror these extremes. They do not join in mass punishments, do not protect severely victimized players with counter-punishment, and do not seem to exploit weakened players by dropping contributions when others are struggling. Their own contribution path often stays high even when punishments lead to obviously inefficient outcomes for others. This suggests a separation in their mind between “how others are behaving with punishment” and “how much I should contribute.” They appear to prioritize maintaining a reasonably cooperative contribution level over reacting to fluctuations in others’ payoffs or enforcement behavior. They also do not selectively reward high cooperators via absence of punishment (since they never punish anyone) or via extra-high contributions targeted to specific subgroups (not possible in this mechanism). Their response to others’ outcomes is thus primarily indirect: they track the general contribution climate and adjust marginally, but largely disregard the complex punishment/payoff dynamics.

They pay attention to others’ outcomes enough to make targeted normative judgments. Their occasional punishments tend to fall on players whose contribution levels or apparent free‑riding are out of line with a high‑cooperation standard, not on those who are already being severely punished or clearly cooperating. They also do not join in on large punishment “piling on” when others massively over‑punish some target; they remain restrained even when others spend huge amounts on punishment. This implies they are not swayed by group punishment cascades or emotional contagion. They seem indifferent to relative payoffs in the sense that they do not try to knock down high earners or protect their own ranking; they stick to full contribution even when it leaves them with lower payoffs than less cooperative but less‑punished peers. At the same time, they do not intervene to protect exploited players or to counteract unjust punishment; they simply do their own mild norm enforcement and otherwise ignore outcome inequality. Their learning and adaptation are limited: dramatic swings in others’ contributions and payoffs (including low‑contribution and high‑volatility phases) do not lead them to alter their cooperative or light‑punishing profile. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes in a narrowly normative way (under‑contribution sometimes triggers light sanction) rather than in a payoff‑oriented or emotionally reactive way.

The player pays clear attention to others’ contributions, punishment spending, and payoffs, and updates their strategy in response. When they see broad, high contributions and relatively calm punishment, they tend to join or maintain cooperative contribution levels. When they observe heavy punishment activity, erratic contributors, or low contributions from some participants, they sometimes shift to lower contributions or focus their sanctions on particular players. They appear sensitive not only to who contributes what, but also to who is already being punished and how heavily. They occasionally allow others’ heavy sanctioning of a target to continue without joining in, and sometimes they add their own small punishment to a player who is already under sanction. This mix suggests they perceive and incorporate the emergent reputation structure, but their own contribution to “piling on” is measured rather than maximal. They do not systematically try to protect high contributors from being punished, nor do they use punishment to equalize payoffs; the pattern is more about reacting to perceived under-contribution or disruptive behavior than outcome inequality per se. They also do not adjust to favor or spare those who are spending a lot on punishment; there is no clear deference to “strong enforcers.” In summary, they track others’ outcomes and adapt in a nuanced but somewhat noisy way: steering toward more cooperation when the group is coordinated and stable, and becoming more opportunistic or sanction-focused when they perceive instability, defection, or chaotic punishment dynamics.

The player visibly monitors the group context enough to time some punishment into periods with lower average contributions, but their own contributions stay fixed, suggesting that others’ outcomes influence their enforcement behavior more than their cooperation level. When others invest heavily in punishment and suffer large payoff losses, the player does not imitate this pattern or escalate alongside them; they often keep their own punishment at zero in such turbulent rounds. This points to a relatively independent strategy: they notice others’ expenditures and outcomes but are not easily pulled into punishment spirals. They do not join coalitions of heavy punishers who concentrate attacks on specific low contributors over many rounds; instead, they alternate between enforcing in some episodes and remaining a passive cooperator in others. There is no clear evidence that they favor or protect particular individuals based on high contributions or victimization; any such preferences are unknown. They do not visibly reward via lenient treatment—reward is not available—but they also do not target high contributors for aggression. On balance, they respond to others’ outcomes by maintaining high cooperation, occasionally stepping in to sanction deviations, and otherwise allowing others’ conflicts and payoff swings to play out without systematically intervening.

They are exposed to detailed outcome information, including who spends on punishment and who gets heavily sanctioned, yet they never join the punishment activity nor visibly coordinate with others’ sanctioning patterns. They do not mimic aggressive punishers or “pile on” when someone is being hit by multiple sanctions. Their contribution adjustments appear to respond to diffuse features of the environment (overall cooperation level, general turbulence) rather than specific individuals’ payoffs or sanction histories. In cooperative phases, they maintain or resume high contributions even when some players gain relative advantage or remain unpunished free-riders, implying that outcome inequality alone does not provoke them into punitive or exploitative behavior. They seem to treat others’ outcomes primarily as background information for gauging the state of the group, not as targets for strategic or emotional intervention.

They observe rich information about others’ payoffs and punishment expenditures and appear to use these outcomes in a selective, emotionally inflected way rather than via a clean, rule-based inference. At times, when others maintain high contributions and punishment activity stabilizes or becomes moderate, they align with the cooperative majority and refrain from punishing, which suggests they recognize and respond positively to a high-cooperation, low-conflict environment. However, they do not systematically “reward” high contributors by sparing them from punishment; some high-contributing players are still sanctioned by them in certain conflict episodes. This indicates that others’ good outcomes do not automatically protect against their punitive responses. Conversely, when they see others spending heavily on punishment, especially in ways that reduce their own payoff, they are willing to incur large additional costs to change others’ outcomes, even at high cost to themselves. This is consistent with a player who is highly attentive to relative outcomes, perceived slights, and group conflict, and who uses the punishment mechanism to re-balance or express disapproval more than to optimize aggregate payoffs. They do not clearly condition their own contribution on others’ monetary success alone; high overall payoffs in the group can coexist with either high or low contributions by them. Their primary reactions seem to be to patterns of treatment and to the fairness or hostility they infer from others’ punishment and contribution behavior, rather than to raw payoff levels.

This player continuously observes heavy punishment activity by others, including extreme overuse where some players burn large amounts of coins to impose large deductions, sometimes even driving payoffs to zero or negative. Their own strategy does not mimic these aggressive patterns. They do not join multi-party punishment campaigns in any systematic way, nor do they pile on when someone is already being heavily punished. Instead, they appear to use others’ outcomes mainly as a coarse signal of the overall climate: when the group’s contributions and payoffs are broadly high and stable, they stay fully cooperative; when there is widespread under-contribution or chaotic punishment, they occasionally reduce their own contribution, but only modestly and temporarily. They do not overreact to individuals’ extreme losses or gains. They also do not specifically direct punishment at the apparent “villains” of the payoff landscape (e.g., those who repeatedly inflict huge deductions on others). This suggests they are more focused on maintaining personal and group-level payoff through steady cooperation than on rebalancing individual injustices via punishment. Type-wise, they resemble a “quiet high cooperator” who treats others’ erratic outcomes as noise to be worked around rather than as cues for confrontation. They update loosely to the group’s aggregate contribution level but show little fine-grained, player-specific contingent behavior based on who is punishing whom or how much each individual gains or loses.

The player clearly attends to others’ contributions and punishment activity, as evidenced by their timing: dips in their own contribution and rare use of punishment tend to coincide with periods when others are either under‑contributing or engaging in extreme punishment. They never imitate the extreme punishers; instead, they remain moderate and avoid large, payoff‑destroying sanction costs. They are not visibly motivated by schadenfreude or envy: when others earn very high payoffs, they do not automatically retaliate, and when others are seriously punished, they do not pile on. Likewise, they do not attempt to “protect” heavily punished individuals with their own behavior, but they also refrain from joining group attacks. Their stance toward others’ outcomes is observant and adjusting, not aggressively interventionist. In terms of learning, they appear to use the pattern of group outcomes to recalibrate their own cooperation level. When the environment is highly cooperative and relatively calm on punishment, they converge to full contributions. When the environment is noisy—with many low contributors or heavy punishers—they give themselves more leeway to under‑contribute and are slightly more willing to punish select targets. This makes them an adaptive, climate‑sensitive cooperator rather than a rigid rule‑follower or a pure payoff maximizer.

The player observes substantial heterogeneity in others’ contributions, heavy and sometimes extreme punishment expenditures by several peers, and large, often negative shocks to others’ payoffs due to sanctions. Despite this, they show no detectable behavioral response: no selective reciprocity, no support for “altruistic punishers,” no defensive withdrawal from cooperation in the face of chaos, and no opportunistic exploitation of low-contributing or heavily punished peers. Others’ high or low payoffs, and whether those payoffs arise from cooperation or from punishment dynamics, do not appear to guide their own choices. They do not move to protect high contributors, discipline low contributors, or align themselves with any emerging coalition of punishers or non-punishers. Overall, they behave as if others’ detailed outcomes are largely irrelevant to their own decision rule, which remains: contribute (almost) fully, do not punish, and ride out whatever the group does around them.

This player pays close attention to who gains and who sacrifices via punishment. They react strongly when they see others burning large amounts of coins to hammer specific targets or to send disproportionate signals. When high punishers repeatedly degrade others’ payoffs, this player starts using punishment themselves, often against those same heavy punishers or conspicuously low contributors. They appear sensitive to both contributions and punishment costs: someone who contributes modestly but punishes massively is more likely to be treated as a problem than someone who merely contributes slightly below average without weaponizing punishment. Conversely, when outcomes are relatively even, with high contributions and low or moderate punishment, the player largely refrains from sanctioning and maximizes cooperative gains. They do not show evidence of rewarding others (not available in this setting), but they implicitly “reward” cooperative behavior by returning to full contribution and withholding sanctions. They seem comfortable with others earning higher payoffs when those higher payoffs arise from legitimate cooperative behavior; their punitive focus is more on perceived norm violators than on simple inequality. Overall, they adapt to outcome patterns: periods of widespread aggression or opportunism trigger more of their own punishment and occasional contribution cuts, whereas calm and equitable outcomes elicit stable high cooperation and restraint from punishing.

They clearly observe that others incur large punishment costs and that some players are driven to very low or even negative payoffs through extreme punishment exchanges, yet they do not join in. They do not adjust their own behavior to mimic these aggressive strategies, nor do they appear intimidated into permanent high contributions; instead, they keep mixing high cooperation with occasional protective under-contribution. This suggests that they interpret heavy punishment use as either irrational or as background noise rather than as a directive. They do not appear to favor or punish particular individuals in response to their outcomes; there is no selective response to players who earn unusually high payoffs, to those who are frequently punished, or to those who spend a lot to punish others. Their adjustment is coarse and group-focused: when the group as a whole is contributing less or outcomes are more chaotic, they become more wary; when the group stabilizes at high contributions and punishment softens or concentrates elsewhere, they comfortably return to full cooperation. They seem relatively indifferent to others’ individual fortunes and more attuned to overall cooperation levels and their own earnings, while still maintaining a pro-social baseline.

The player pays close attention to both others’ contributions and their punishment activity and adjusts only their punishment decisions, not their cooperation level. When they see others spend heavily on punishment, they tend to join norm-enforcing coalitions rather than oppose them, focusing on the same low-contribution or disruptive players. When group cooperation is high and punishment flows are low or moderate, they stop punishing and simply enjoy the cooperative equilibrium, keeping contributions maximal. When some players earn noticeably higher payoffs through under-contribution or through costly attacks on cooperators, the focal player is willing to incur substantial expense to cut those payoffs down, even at significant personal cost. This indicates a strong concern for fairness and for the relative standing between cooperators and defectors. They are not payoff-maximizing in a narrow sense; their behavior is better described as cooperative, norm-driven, and willing to enforce pro-social norms even when that enforcement is expensive and sometimes only expressive rather than strictly instrumental.

This player pays no observable cost to influence others’ outcomes through punishment, and there is no reward option, so their influence is entirely via contributions. They seem responsive at a coarse level to the overall effectiveness of group cooperation: when the environment is orderly with high contributions and moderate punishment, they reliably match that high level and enjoy the resulting high payoffs. When payoffs become more erratic due to scattered or heavy punishment by others, they sometimes lower their contributions, which mildly shifts payoffs in their favor relative to full cooperation, but they do not attempt to surgically shape who gains or loses. They do not differentially react to players who are heavily punished or to chronic low contributors; those players’ specific outcomes do not provoke targeted behavior from them. Their stance toward others’ outcomes is thus broadly pragmatic and impersonal: they respond to the overall state of the public good (cooperative versus chaotic) but refrain from micro-managing or norm-enforcing others’ payoffs.

They pay attention to the distribution of punishments and outcomes and respond primarily with humor and social commentary rather than moralizing. They notice when no one punishes and celebrate it as a collective “sweetheart” moment. Later, when a specific player becomes heavily targeted, they join the group’s joking narrative, teasingly interpreting interpersonal punishment as evidence of underlying “hate” or conflict between specific players. Their comments are light and affiliative, signaling group bonding rather than condemnation. They show no visible envy or resentment toward high earners or free-riders, nor do they attempt to mobilize others against heavy punishers or low contributors. When another player is disproportionately punished, they respond with amusement and mild sympathy but do not intervene materially. They also show interest in others’ interpretations of the experiment’s purpose rather than focusing on individual payoffs, which reinforces the picture of someone more motivated by the shared social experience and intellectual curiosity than by outcome comparisons. Overall, they infer social meaning from outcome patterns (e.g., linking punishment to interpersonal tensions or group dynamics) and use these outcomes as fodder for jokes and reflection, not as input into a hard-edged strategic or moral campaign.

They observe a rich pattern of others’ contributions and punishments: some players repeatedly under-contribute, some become repeated punishment targets, and some engage in dramatic, costly sanctioning episodes. Despite this, the focal player never joins in, never shifts to punishing the revealed “villains,” and does not clearly reward “heroes” through behavior beyond sustaining high contributions. They do not vocally endorse or criticize any punishment campaigns, including extreme ones against particular players. Nor do they chase short-term advantage by reducing their own contributions to match the least cooperative players once free-riding becomes salient. Instead, they mostly maintain their cooperative strategy with small, late adjustments that appear driven by overall contribution trends and end-game uncertainty, not by specific individuals’ payoffs. This pattern suggests that they primarily respond to aggregate group behavior rather than to individual trajectories. Others’ outcomes influence them only in a diffuse way: as the group’s average cooperation erodes and the game drags on unpredictably, they allow themselves some moderate self-serving adjustment, but they never flip into cynical exploitation or targeted sanctioning. They are robustly pro-cooperative, quietly non-punitive, and relatively unconcerned with the fine-grained social drama of who “deserves” what.

They pay attention to the pattern of others’ contributions and punishment, as shown both by their targeted punishment choices and their comments about who is “making money.” They notice that one player contributes low but accumulates high payoffs, and they frame this admiringly rather than condemningly, indicating a nuanced, somewhat playful view of strategic free‑riding. They also notice that another player bears heavy punishment and join the group’s joking narrative around that person. When confronted in chat about punishment patterns directed at that player, they deflect moral responsibility with humor (“knows how to make money”), indicating they are comfortable with harsh outcomes for outliers when those outcomes fit the emergent group story. They show no explicit sympathy for punished free‑riders but do participate in the light “poor X” banter once the group has clearly converged on a victim. They appear more interested in the social dynamics and narrative (who’s clever, who’s serene, who’s the butt of the joke) than in tracking strict payoff fairness across all players. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes with awareness, humor, and a willingness to let social labeling and perceived strategy determine who “deserves” high or low payoffs, rather than rigid adherence to an equal‑payoff fairness rule.

They show awareness of group‑level trends (everyone contributing a lot, absence or presence of deductions, overall enjoyable cooperation), but do not comment on specific payoff differences, punishment magnitudes, or individual losses/gains. They react positively to rounds with no deductions, celebrating the group as “sweethearts” indirectly via chat and reinforcing the cooperative mood. They laugh along with the developing social drama around one player being heavily punished but do not join in condemning or defending any side; instead, they focus on how entertaining and funny the interaction is. They do not express envy, resentment, or moral outrage about others earning more when they free‑ride, nor sympathy about those heavily punished except a mild “poor cow” echo via others (their own stance is limited to amusement at the situation and enjoyment of the social interaction). Overall, their reaction to others’ outcomes is emotionally positive and socially oriented, not justice‑ or enforcement‑oriented; they treat payoff patterns and punishment spikes as part of an interesting social experiment rather than as issues that require corrective action.

They monitor the group outcomes and integrate them into behavior. They continue high contributions when the group is highly cooperative and no one is being deducted, and abstain from punishment in those harmonious phases, which shows they condition their enforcement on others’ outcomes and behavior. When some players start consistently contributing less and absorbing punishment from others, they do not express sympathy or outrage in chat; instead, they quietly increase their own punishment spending, suggesting a preference to adjust incentives mechanically rather than argue. They do not pile on the “villainization” of the most heavily punished player in chat, but they also do not defend them, implying emotionally neutral but norm‑focused judgment. As the game progresses and more varied patterns emerge (some players oscillating contributions, some burning large amounts on punishment), they slightly lower their own contributions in a few rounds while still punishing, indicating that they update their expectations: others may be less reliably cooperative, and the enforcement environment has become more chaotic. Yet even then, they keep contributions high by group standards and do not join in extreme sanctions or social ganging‑up behavior, reinforcing an image of a principled, steady cooperator–enforcer who tracks others’ outcomes and responds proportionally rather than opportunistically.

They follow others’ outcomes and sanctions and interpret them socially rather than strategically. Notable patterns: - They comment when there is a round with no deductions, praising the group and reinforcing a “we’re all sweethearts / good job everyone” narrative. - They notice when a specific player repeatedly targets another (e.g., substantial punishment inflicted on one player by another) and remark on it with surprise, then engage in joking commentary about that dynamic with others. - They explicitly raise the question of why one player is targeted and, later, why another seems to be targeting them, indicating attention to fairness and social logic behind punishment patterns. - When the group’s behavior toward a frequently punished player becomes an ongoing joke (e.g., jokes about eating that player’s real-world analogue), they participate in the humor but also show some sympathy (“poor X” reactions appear from others; this player does not openly gloat). Their joking exclusion of that player is framed as playful rather than meant to encourage further punishment. They also engage with meta-interpretations of the game (e.g., agreeing that chat might increase cooperation, analogies to “the bad place”), showing that they track the emergent group story and not just payoffs. They do not seem to use others’ contribution or punishment behavior to adjust their own contributions or start systematic punishment. Information about others’ outcomes primarily feeds into their social commentary and moral curiosity rather than an enforcement strategy. Overall, they respond to others’ actions with: - Social bonding (shared jokes, “we’re in this together” comments). - Lightly moralized curiosity (why are you doing this to that person / to me?). - Continued unconditional cooperation rather than strategic adaptation. In sum, this player is an “unconditional cooperator, non-punitive, socially engaged humorist”: they reliably maximize contributions, largely abstain from punishment, absorb unfair treatment without retaliation, and invest heavily in friendly, joking, reflective interaction about the group dynamics and the meaning of the game.

The player observes a group that is, for much of the game, highly cooperative and minimally punitive among themselves. They see that their own pattern leads others to single them out with large punishment costs and deductions, and they are aware of the social narrative in chat that frames them as the odd one out. Nonetheless, they do not publicly criticize others’ punishment choices or attempt to shame large punishers, even when those punishers incur huge personal costs. They also show no explicit sympathy or praise toward cooperators and high contributors in chat; they leave most social reinforcement (jokes, compliments, coordination reinforcement) to other players. Their few written comments are about interpersonal tone (“loves everybody”), not about outcomes, contributions, or who “deserves” what. Implicitly, however, they adapt only weakly to others’ actions. The high cooperation levels by others do not elicit stable full cooperation from them; heavy punishment does not elicit stable compliance. This suggests that they treat others’ outcomes and spending as environmental constraints rather than moral signals: they register that they can still earn well despite being punished and that cooperation by others remains high, and they continue to exploit that environment. In sum, they acknowledge group dynamics socially but keep their strategic calculus largely unchanged by either others’ generosity or hostility, showing a detached, payoff‑focused response to others’ outcomes.

They clearly attend to others’ outcomes and punishment patterns. They comment on the possible purpose of the experiment (willingness to sacrifice), and their questions about round length and debrief indicate that they are thinking in terms of long-run cumulative payoffs and the structure of incentives. They also engage in meta-commentary when the group speculates about the social effects of chat and punishment, echoing the idea that social punishment of outliers is central. Despite this awareness, they do not publicly judge or moralize about particular individuals’ outcomes, including heavy punishers like the very aggressive enforcer who repeatedly targets a single player. They join in the joking, somewhat dark humor about one target player (e.g., “this is the bad place”) but do not themselves initiate ridicule or express animosity. Their reaction to seeing others punished harshly is to lean into group banter rather than to defend or condemn. They also do not seem envious of others earning more within a round; there is no evidence of them lowering contributions simply because someone else is making more through lower contributions or strategic punishment. Their occasional free-riding appears more related to fatigue, uncertainty about how long the game will continue, and the evolving contribution norms, rather than reactive envy or fairness outrage. In summary, they are observant and reflective about others’ payoffs and punishment behavior, but emotionally even-tempered: they neither strongly campaign against high earners or defectors, nor crusade for strict equality. Their responses to others’ outcomes are mainly cognitive (reasoning about the experiment and group dynamics) and social (sharing jokes, maintaining positive rapport), rather than moralistic or adversarial.

They pay attention to group patterns (contributions, punishment waves, and the social treatment of specific players like COW and CROCODILE), and they comment at a conceptual level. They refer to “social punishment of outliers” and compare the group behavior to classic studies of intergroup conflict and norm enforcement, indicating that they notice and mentally model how others are using punishment and deviating from norms. However, they do not express moral outrage, praise, or condemnation at specific payoff outcomes. When some players are heavily punished or when contribution levels drop, they do not shift into a moralizing or policing role. Instead, they remain amused, reflective, and committed to their own cooperative strategy. They also acknowledge that the chat likely influences high contributions, showing that they link observed outcomes to the communication environment. In sum, they track others’ outcomes and interpret them through a social-science lens, but they stay behaviorally stable: no tit-for-tat, no opportunistic free-riding when they could exploit others, and no joining in collective punishment campaigns. Their primary adjustment to others’ outcomes is cognitive (updating their theory of what the experiment is about), not strategic.

The player appears attentive to the overall pattern of contributions and sanctions, and adjusts only their punishment behavior in response, not their contribution level. Observing that some players bear substantial sanctioning costs and that a consistent low contributor is heavily punished does not lead them to free-ride or to protect that player; instead, they later add their own punishment against that same low contributor when the deviation becomes more extreme. They show no effort to offset penalties with rewards and do not intervene to correct others’ prior sanctioning choices. This suggests they accept or share the emergent norm that very low contribution is inappropriate. They do not appear reactive to others’ final payoffs in a competitive or envious way; their behavior is not aimed at overtaking specific individuals but at disciplining the most non-cooperative participant. Overall, they behave as a principled high contributor who tolerates moderate heterogeneity but responds to glaring free-riding with targeted, costly punishment while remaining uninterested in using rewards or in fine-tuning others’ outcomes beyond clear norm enforcement.

The player is observant of other players’ contributions and the subsequent punishment landscape, and they adjust their sanctioning behavior once they have seen how others perform and how punishment is being used. Initially, they see heterogeneous contributions and substantial punishment directed at a moderate contributor, yet they do not react with either imitation or counter-punishment. This indicates some independence from prevailing punitive norms; they do not automatically follow the group’s punitive pattern. Later, after observing both persistent low contribution by a particular individual and generally high contributions among others, they choose to punish precisely the most pronounced free-rider. This implies that their response is more anchored in comparative performance (who stands out as least cooperative) than in others’ final payoffs or in copying others’ use of sanctions. They do not use others’ high payoffs or generous behavior as a cue for reward, reinforcing the idea that they treat good behavior as baseline and bad behavior as warranting response. They do not appear to react to how much others spend on punishment or reward per se; there is no sign of punishing over-punishers, defending victims, or rewarding punishers. Their focus is on the direct relationship between contribution level and the legitimacy of sanctions. Emotional states cannot be directly inferred, but behaviorally they resemble a calm, principle-driven enforcer who tolerates some inequality but intervenes when deviations from cooperative norms are especially stark, even when there is no future strategic benefit to doing so.

This player attends to others’ contributions and to how heavily others are being sanctioned. They avoid redundant punishment when a low contributor is already facing substantial deductions from others, and later they join in punishing when an extreme defector continues to benefit relative to the group. This indicates a sensitivity to perceived unfair advantage and to whether the “norm-enforcement load” is already being carried by others. Their own payoff remains relatively high across rounds, suggesting they balance sanctioning with self-interest rather than sacrificing large amounts to police the group. When they reward, they choose someone who both cooperates well and ends up with a strong payoff, which may reflect a preference for reinforcing successful cooperators rather than compensating the disadvantaged. Overall, they behave like a mildly prosocial, norm-enforcing player who watches the emerging payoff structure and steps in with small, strategic interventions rather than large, unconditional ones.

They observe others’ contributions and the pattern of punishment and reward but respond in a muted and somewhat asymmetric way. They punish a player who is otherwise doing well, while ignoring the lowest contributors and not rewarding the highest ones, indicating that they are not simply aligning with visible enforcement trends in the group. When later confronted with punishers who spend heavily to sanction others, they do not imitate this pattern, nor do they counter-punish or reward those who spared them. They do not adjust their final-round contribution upward in response to widespread high contributions and significant group enforcement against low contributors; instead, they modestly reduce their own giving while refraining from further social interventions. This suggests a type who notices group-level prosocial and punitive activity but primarily optimizes their own payoff within a loose cooperative band, rather than actively coordinating with or reinforcing the emergent norm structure. Overall, they are a moderately cooperative, individually oriented player who is willing to punish idiosyncratically once, then disengages from further sanctioning as the game concludes.

This player appears to focus primarily on others’ contribution choices rather than on the final payoff structure or who is spending on sanctions. They do not respond in any observable way to being punished themselves: their contribution remains maximal, and their sanctioning logic continues to track others’ under-contribution rather than who may have targeted them. This suggests relative indifference to personal payoff losses from punishment and a lack of retaliatory motive. They also do not adjust behavior in response to others incurring punishment costs or reward costs; there is no evidence of trying to coordinate, mimic, or counteract others’ sanctioning strategies. Their attention seems to be on enforcing a cooperation norm, not on redistributing final payoffs or managing status via sanctions. Overall, they tolerate personal payoff reductions—including from being punished—while maintaining a strong cooperative and punitive stance toward those who fall short of their implicit contribution standard.

The player observes others’ final payoffs and the costs they incur through punishment and reward, yet does not adjust into any active social control. They see low contributors being heavily punished and high contributors being punished or left alone, but they neither join in nor counteract these patterns. There is no sign of them trying to protect high cooperators with rewards, to defend low cooperators, or to coordinate with apparent punishers. Their own actions remain unchanged in terms of punishment/reward usage. This suggests a primarily payoff- and contribution-focused orientation: they track their own contribution and payoff decisions rather than trying to manage the broader social landscape of who gains or loses through sanctions. They do not appear reactive to others’ suffering or gains in a way that translates into costly behavior; any emotional or evaluative reaction is unknown. Overall, they behave as a cooperative, non-interventionist type: they contribute generously themselves but stay neutral regarding how others’ outcomes are shaped by sanctions.

The player pays attention to how others behave and what they earn, and their actions reflect sensitivity to visible deviations. Very low contributions combined with good payoffs trigger punishment, even when group contributions remain high overall, which shows that relative unfairness matters to them more than absolute group success. They do not react to others’ heavy use of punishment by copying it broadly; instead, they continue to focus on the most obvious low contributor, suggesting they process outcome information in a targeted, norm-based way rather than simply mirroring the group’s punitive intensity. Receiving a reward does not lead them to start rewarding others, implying that they do not adopt reciprocity in the reward domain based solely on payoff information. Overall, they interpret outcome patterns through a fairness and norm-compliance lens, adjusting punishment and contribution but not adopting costly positive reciprocity.

They see detailed outcome information each round, including others’ contributions, rewards given and received, and payoffs. Behaviorally, they clearly attend to this information enough to maintain persistent low contributions even while observing that others are frequently giving substantially more and often rewarding each other. Their continued low contributions indicate that witnessing high cooperation and active rewarding does not induce them to match the group’s generosity. At the same time, they do not react by further exploiting others (e.g., dropping to zero contribution in a sustained way once they see others are very cooperative); instead they maintain a small but non-trivial contribution. Thus, they are a “soft free-rider” rather than an extreme exploiter. They also do not react strongly against conspicuously unbalanced reward behavior by others (for example, some players engage in heavy, often self-sacrificial rewarding of certain targets). The focal player neither increases contributions to join those informal coalitions nor ceases rewarding entirely in protest. They maintain their own low but stable pattern of play, which suggests they are relatively unresponsive to social structure changes or emerging coalitions in the outcome data. They seem content to treat others’ outcomes as background conditions: sources of benefit and occasional small reciprocal gestures, but not as drivers of substantial strategic revision. Because they do not comment in chat, there is no emotional or normative commentary on others’ outcomes—no expressed frustration with low contributors, no praise of high contributors, and no discussion of fairness. Their personality, as inferred from reactions to outcome information, appears pragmatic, self-focused in payoff terms, somewhat detached from group norms, but not aggressively exploitative or spiteful.

They pay attention to others’ contributions and outcomes, as indicated by their conditional contributions and selective rewarding. When others persistently contribute high amounts, they are willing to match or even exceed the average and to reinforce such behavior through rewards. When it becomes possible to benefit substantially from others’ high effort by reducing their own contribution, they sometimes do so, indicating that they are sensitive to payoff structures and not purely fairness-driven. Their reward use in conjunction with others’ payoffs suggests they track both who is bearing costs for the group and who is spending on rewards. They occasionally reward in ways that increase others’ already strong payoffs, which indicates they are not focused on equalizing final earnings; their concern seems more about encouraging desirable behavior (like robust contribution) than about redistributive fairness. They do not verbally comment on others’ spending or payoffs, nor do they visibly react by punishing via non-cooperation specific individuals over multiple rounds in a clearly targeted way; their contribution changes look more like responses to the overall cooperation climate than targeted retaliation. Overall, they interpret others’ outcomes and spending as signals of group norms and use those signals to adjust both their own contribution level and their reward allocations, in a way that blends self-interest with a real but bounded interest in supporting the public good.

The player observes others’ contributions, payoffs, and reward choices each round and adjusts in a gradual, not highly volatile way. They continue to contribute at a cooperative level even when some players free-ride or contribute minimally, indicating tolerance for noise and exploitation. Their own reward spending persists even when others either spend heavily or almost not at all, suggesting that they are not strongly deterred by seeing others hold back on rewarding. At the same time, they never escalate to maximal contributions or maximal reward spending in reaction to others’ high cooperation or generosity, which implies a bounded, measured response style rather than emotional overreaction. They do not publicly comment on others’ outcomes, so their emotional reactions are unknown; behaviorally, they appear steady, norm-guided, and relatively robust to variation in others’ payoffs and actions.

They see detailed outcome information but never comment in chat, so their explicit evaluative stance is unknown. Behaviorally, they continue high contributions even when low contributors achieve relatively high payoffs, indicating they tolerate some free-riding without visible retaliation or withdrawal. They also continue rewarding even when the group includes both cooperators and clear low contributors, which suggests they focus their attention on supporting cooperative peers instead of reacting against defectors. They do not use outcome information to punish, shame, or strategically starve particular players; instead, they use it (as far as can be inferred) to identify and encourage those whose behavior aligns with their cooperative preferences. Their reaction to players who spend very heavily on rewards is not clearly distinct; there is no strong behavioral pivot in contributions or rewards after seeing such outcomes. They appear relatively stable and non-reactive, interpreting outcome feedback more as information for fine-tuning support than as a signal to change their fundamental strategy.

The player closely observes the outcome table (implied by their selective reward behavior) but remains unmoved in their contribution level by others’ high costs and high contributions. They continue to free-ride on players who contribute much more and sometimes spend heavily on reward, indicating low conformity to fairness norms that would push them toward sharing the burden. At the same time, they are willing to incur small personal costs to reward others, which shows some responsiveness to others’ beneficial actions and an interest in acknowledging them. They never react in chat to others’ high or low payoffs, to exits, or to complaints about time, so they do not provide emotional commentary or moral judgments. Their pattern is: note who looks cooperative or generous, occasionally send a cheap positive signal via reward, but keep their own core behavior (very low contribution) fixed. They do not use others’ outcomes as feedback to experiment with different strategies; their learning and adaptation appear minimal, suggesting either satisfaction with their payoff position or low engagement with the strategic depth of the game.

The player pays attention to how others’ actions affect payoffs and adjusts in ways that exploit these outcomes when advantageous. They are willing to join cooperators who previously benefited from others’ contributions and also to free-ride on those who continue contributing after cooperation has become uneven. They do not appear to try to “correct” unfair outcomes; instead, they capitalize on them.
