The player repeatedly observes that others who contribute more often end up with lower payoffs than themselves, yet they continue to free-ride in most rounds. This indicates that they respond more to the payoff gradient (defection is individually advantageous) than to any norm of reciprocity or mutual support. They do not move toward higher or more stable contributions even when the group’s cooperative level is relatively high for multiple consecutive rounds. They do not appear to condition their behavior on specific individuals’ outcomes; their pattern does not track any one peer’s shifts. Instead, they seem to respond at most to the overall payoff structure: defection yields higher individual returns given the group’s contributions. When they do choose to contribute, they do not follow this with a new cooperative streak despite others still bearing costs, which suggests that any inequality they observe does not strongly motivate them to correct it. There is no discernible pattern of “rewarding” cooperative phases with sustained cooperation or “punishing” group-wide defection by withdrawing contributions; their own payoff remains the central reference point. With no explicit punishment or reward mechanisms available, their reaction to others’ outcomes manifests only through their own contribution decisions, and those decisions show minimal pro-social adjustment. They treat others’ lower payoffs relative to theirs as acceptable collateral of the game structure rather than as a signal to change course. Their beliefs about others’ behavior are not directly observable, but their persistence in free-riding despite ongoing contributions from many peers is consistent with expecting that others will continue to contribute enough to make defection profitable.

This player repeatedly observes that non-contributors receive higher payoffs than contributors, sometimes by a substantial margin. Despite this, they do not systematically switch to free riding, indicating a relatively muted payoff-reactivity. They do notice and briefly imitate the profitable strategy of not contributing once, which shows they are sensitive to relative earnings and can adapt behavior in that direction. However, their rapid return to cooperation suggests that material payoff differences alone do not override their underlying cooperative stance. They do not have access to punishment or reward tools in this setting, so their response to others’ outcomes is expressed only through their own contribution decisions. They never engage in sustained retaliatory defection against low contributors, nor do they escalate defection in response to persistent inequality. This implies a low tendency toward payoff-based retribution and a relatively high tolerance for being taken advantage of. Their pattern is consistent with a player who tracks that free riding is individually lucrative but continues to prioritize maintaining a cooperative norm. They seem to accept that some group members will earn more and still choose to contribute, implying that they value group efficiency or adherence to a cooperation norm more than strictly maximizing their own or correcting others’ payoffs. Their learning is therefore conservative: they register others’ higher earnings, briefly test the exploitative strategy, but ultimately default back to cooperative behavior rather than re-optimizing around others’ superior payoffs.

The player’s cooperation is insensitive to others’ payoffs and contribution patterns. As others alternate between relatively cooperative and more free-riding profiles, including periods where free riders earn strictly more than contributors, the player does not adjust. They neither reduce their contributions to avoid being exploited nor appear to reward cooperative phases by increasing from a previously lower level; their behavior is flat. They observe that some players systematically benefit from contributing nothing while still receiving substantial returns from the group. Despite this, they do not respond by imitating the profitable strategy or by withdrawing their own support. This suggests that either they do not prioritize payoff comparison, or they do but choose not to act on it. There is no evidence of emotional reciprocity (retaliation, “teaching a lesson,” or strategic forgiveness) because they never change their action in response to others’ outcomes. Because there is no punishment or reward mechanism available in this environment, we cannot observe how they would allocate costly sanctions or bonuses; their latent willingness to punish defectors or reward cooperators is unknown. All that can be inferred is that, when the only lever they have is their own contribution, they do not use it as a contingent response to others’ earnings or behavior. Their type is best characterized as a steadfast, non-conditional cooperator whose choices are effectively independent of others’ outcomes.

They consistently maintain full cooperation despite observing clear payoffs from free-riding and substantial heterogeneity in others’ reward spending. When others earn high payoffs by contributing nothing, they do not retaliate or reduce their own contributions. When some players incur large personal costs to reward the group, they do not visibly “match” these efforts, either by upping their own rewards or by adjusting contributions (which are already maximal). The presence of highly generous rewarders does not induce them into a competitive generosity spiral; they stay with low or zero reward spending. This suggests a relatively detached stance toward others’ payoff outcomes: they note them (it is visible) but do not allow them to reshape their own core behavior. There is no evidence of envy, revenge, or payoff-based conditionality. Their response profile is best characterized as stoic and rule-driven: others’ gains, losses, or sacrifices have minimal observable impact on their own contribution and reward decisions. Whether they internally approve, disapprove, or simply ignore others’ payoffs remains unknown.

They observe that some others occasionally defect and that some peers spend heavily to reward the group, but they do not materially adjust their own economic behavior in response. When another player free-rides, they maintain full contribution and refrain from retaliatory measures (which in this design would have to be indirect, since punishment is unavailable). Their comments suggest awareness of greed and acknowledgment of uneven contribution, but this awareness does not translate into strategic countermeasures. When others spend substantial amounts on reward, including generous “gifting” toward them, they do not reciprocate with their own rewards; instead, they continue to follow their existing contribution-only strategy. They do engage verbally with group dynamics (e.g., playfully referencing a player who alternates between cooperating and defecting, and joining the group’s ongoing commentary on that player), which shows social awareness of who is doing what. However, their behavioral response is inertial: they hold cooperation constant, ignore reward as a tool, and seem content to let others’ costly reward behavior be their choice. In sum, they are responsive at the conversational level but behaviorally steadfast, neither punishing nor rewarding through the available mechanism, and relying on the baseline structure of the PGG and their own principled cooperation rather than outcome-contingent adjustments.

They pay attention to others’ contribution behavior and occasionally comment on it, but they do not explicitly reference others’ specific payoffs or reward expenditures. Their reaction to a consistently low contributor is to label that behavior as greedy in chat, indicating a negative normative judgment of free-riding—at least when performed by someone else. When later singled out by another player for not contributing, their response is not to correct course or justify rationally, but to respond playfully and embrace their avatar identity, effectively downplaying the seriousness of the accusation. They do not publicly acknowledge the substantial reward costs borne by certain group members, nor do they express gratitude or concern for players who spend heavily on rewarding others. Despite being a net recipient of rewards over the game, they offer no explicit verbal thanks or reciprocal reward, suggesting that others’ generosity does not substantially shift their strategy. Overall, they notice and comment on conspicuous deviations from cooperation when it is someone else defecting, but they treat both others’ heavy spending on rewards and the group’s tolerance of their own free-riding as exploitable features rather than signals that warrant changing their own behavior.

They observe others’ contributions and payoffs but do not materially adjust their own contribution level in response; defectors and free-riders do not lead them to reduce cooperation. Likewise, they do not switch to rewarding or withholding rewards in a clearly contingent way based on who previously rewarded them or how much others spent. They sometimes receive rewards but do not visibly react by changing either their reward spending or contribution behavior, which remains uniformly cooperative. When others keep their full endowment while the rest cooperate, they continue to contribute fully and, in some periods, still end up rewarding the entire group, including previous freeriders, suggesting they are not focused on correcting inequality through their own actions. They show no punitive instincts; they neither retaliate against those who benefit more nor withhold group-wide generosity to discipline them. Overall, their response to others’ outcomes is characterized by steadfast cooperative behavior and occasional, non-selective generosity, rather than by strategic adaptation to others’ payoffs or attempts to restore fairness through differential treatment.

They pay attention to outcome information but react in a measured and analytic way, with very little emotional volatility. When others contribute less or behave unexpectedly, they respond by reframing the situation as still profitable and downplaying the damage, emphasizing that everyone remains “in profit” so long as the overall cooperation level is high. When deduction patterns are confusing or apparently misaligned with contributions, they quickly suggest alternative explanations such as system-driven zeros or lag, and they generalize this into a hypothesis that the study may be deliberately manipulating contributions or deductions to test trust and conflict. They show no signs of anger or calls for retaliation, even when high contributors are punished or when they themselves lose coins, and they do not join in shaming of specific low contributors. Instead, they maintain the message that the game remains advantageous and that trusting one another is still the rational path. They show interest in the payoff structure and conversion to real money, doing back-of-the-envelope reasoning about how coins translate into currency and speculating about total earnings, but this curiosity does not lead them to squeeze others or adjust their behavior in a predatory way. Overall, they interpret others’ outcomes through a cooperative, slightly experimentalist lens: anomalies are data points or design quirks rather than moral offenses, and the primary response is to keep the group coordinated and focused on maximizing the shared surplus rather than to redistribute payoffs via punishment.

The player tracks others’ outcomes and deductions enough to comment but reacts in a measured, prosocial way. When one player undercontributes while everyone else invests fully, they join others in mild social disapproval, framing it as “had to ruin it,” but do not escalate to costly punishment themselves. When that same or another player later corrects course or apologizes, they quickly shift tone to empathy and apology, indicating that their negative reaction is conditional and easily reversed by cooperative signals. When another player receives unexplained punishment, they identify who the apparent punisher is based on the scoreboard and call this out, but without advocating revenge. Later, when the supposed punisher denies it and confusion spreads, the focal player floats the idea that the admin or system might be responsible. This is a key response pattern: they reinterpret ambiguous bad outcomes as possible artifacts rather than evidence of bad intentions, in order to preserve group trust and the cooperative equilibrium. They also engage with payoff information in a pragmatic way, discussing total earnings and conversion rates and expressing hope for many more rounds because cooperation is lucrative. Their comments about being near a high real-money payoff suggest they are motivated by earnings but still prioritize group cooperation as the best path to that goal. In summary, when others do well and adhere to norms, they respond with encouragement and shared excitement; when others do poorly or are punished, they show a mix of mild norm-enforcement sentiment and quick forgiveness, with a strong bias toward maintaining cohesion and optimism about the group.

They track others’ outcomes enough to notice deviations (e.g., identifying a specific low contributor by name once), but they rarely comment on detailed payoff differences or who is being punished. They do not express outrage or sympathy when others receive heavy deductions, nor do they complain when noise (suspected “rigging”) appears in punishment data. Instead, they continue behaving consistently: full contribution, no punishment. Their single direct social response to another’s behavior is a brief call-out of a player who contributed less than the emergent norm; they do not follow this with explicit threats or coordination around punishment. This hints at a mild norm-enforcement instinct: they publicly mark deviance, then let the group (and anonymous punishment mechanism) handle the rest. They appear largely outcome-robust: seeing others over-earn from free-riding or under-earn from being punished does not change their strategy. They neither exploit high contributors nor pity low earners enough to adjust their own behavior. In effect, they treat others’ outcomes as background information rather than as a primary driver of their own actions, so long as the group-level payoff environment remains strong. In new PGG environments, this type tends to be a quiet, consistent high contributor who quickly gravitates toward the cooperative optimum, occasionally uses light punishment to anchor norms early, then relies on stable contributions rather than ongoing sanctioning or emotional reactions to others’ payoffs.

They pay close attention to the outcome screen, noticing both payoffs and punishment flows. They quickly infer which player has spent coins on punishment and publicly point this out when others are confused, suggesting they monitor the meta‑information quite closely. When they or other full contributors lose coins to punishment, they react with surprise and mild frustration but remain cooperative and non‑vengeful, and they explicitly defend a punished cooperator (“cute red bird”) while gently teasing the punisher. They are sympathetic when someone claims to have misclicked or been confused by the interface, and they verbally extend forgiveness, helping to keep that person socially integrated. They also react positively to others’ earnings and side‑hustle stories, calling someone “genius” for doing parallel surveys and celebrating the collective “breaking the bank.” Overall, they interpret outcomes through a group‑benefit lens, try to protect trust when the system or unexplained punishments could undermine it, and use outcome information primarily to coordinate and reassure rather than to police or punish.

They pay close attention to who contributes what and to who is deducted but interpret outcomes through a cooperative, charitable frame. When they see a player contributing less or zero, their initial reactions are light and social (teasing or surprise) rather than harsh; when that player later cooperates, they explicitly praise the improvement. When others are heavily punished despite contributing fully, they voice confusion and concern, question who might be doing it, and then gravitate toward the explanation that the system may be manipulating punishments to sow distrust. Once they adopt that belief, they reduce interpersonal blame and instead emphasize that people who appear to be punishing might be victims of the interface or design, reinforcing trust and discouraging witch-hunts. They are quick to accept apologies and explanations for mistakes (such as mis-clicked contributions) and support forgiving interpretations. They also respond positively to others’ success or hustle outside the game context, joining in jokes rather than moralizing. Overall, they interpret others’ outcomes in a way that preserves cooperation, assume good faith when plausible, and refuse to let punishment data destabilize the cooperative norm.

They show minimal overt emotional reaction to payoff differences or to the punishment structure. When they are called out for undercontributing earlier in the game, they do not defend themselves or argue; instead, they quietly revert to high contributions and continue. When they later receive substantial deductions, they do not complain or seek revenge, suggesting either tolerance for noise and unfairness or a decision not to invest attention in the punishment phase. Their comments about the game focus on the mechanics of contribution and timing rather than on who lost coins or who punished whom. They do not join group efforts to identify punishers or to discuss fairness of particular deductions, even though other players are actively doing so. This indicates a low level of engagement with outcome politics and interpersonal scorekeeping. Instead, their primary reaction to the emerging outcome pattern is to lean further into the simple cooperative heuristic (“just click the max, click done, and do it quickly”). They conceptualize the game as something that can be made more profitable for everyone by reducing delays and confusion, not as a venue for expressing anger or enforcing justice. They implicitly accept that some noise in outcomes (including random‐seeming punishments) is part of the environment and not worth responding to. In sum, this player’s type is a norm‐following, efficiency‐oriented cooperator: they mostly contribute at the group standard once it is clear, refrain from punishing, accept both rewards and sanctions with little visible emotional reaction, and use communication mainly to keep the group coordinated and moving quickly rather than to enforce norms or litigate fairness.

They pay close attention to the outcome panel and other players’ experiences, often commenting on who is being deducted and questioning why. When cooperators are punished, they express puzzlement and mild disapproval and work in chat to correct misperceptions (“don’t deduct from anyone who does 20”) and to exonerate apparent “victims” of the punishment system. When ambiguous or seemingly random deductions occur, they lean toward an experimental-design explanation rather than blaming specific individuals, which makes them forgiving and helps maintain trust in the group. When someone deviates unintentionally and apologizes, they react with light scolding followed by quick forgiveness and reassurance, indicating that their concern is about maintaining the norm, not ostracizing individuals. When they themselves are punished, they do not retaliate or reduce contributions, and they show no visible anger; they simply continue their cooperative and prosocial pattern. They also frequently praise the group when outcomes look good, reinforcing the connection between cooperative behavior and high payoffs. Overall, they interpret outcomes through a cooperative, system-focused lens and use them as opportunities to stabilize norms rather than to personalize conflicts.

The player is clearly attentive to the outcome stage, but their responses are coarse-grained and not finely contingent on others’ payoffs, punishment, or reward spending. They continue to fully contribute even after being heavily punished and after seeing others burned by extreme sanctions, indicating that they do not respond to negative outcomes by withdrawing cooperation. This suggests either principled commitment to cooperation or low sensitivity to hostile actions when punishers cannot be identified. They do not systematically comfort victims or compensate underdogs via targeted rewards, despite several clear episodes where some players incur very large punishment costs or deductions. While they sometimes reward widely after seeing cooperative behavior, they do not focus extra benefits on those who previously suffered losses. Thus, empathy-based redistribution toward those most harmed is not strongly evident. When others engage in extreme punishment campaigns or in heavy reward spending, the player does not reliably mirror these behaviors in the next stage. Sometimes they respond with no use of the mechanism, sometimes with their own large-scale aggression, and sometimes with broad rewards. There is no stable reciprocity pattern such as “reward those who reward the group” or “punish those who punish excessively.” Given anonymity and the noisy environment, they seem to treat the outcome screen more as a global signal of chaos or order than as actionable individual histories. Their occasional choice to punish nearly everyone, including high cooperators, coincides with environments where previous punishment patterns are already erratic. This suggests that when they perceive the system as unfair or unpredictable, they respond with group-wide sanctions rather than withdrawing or targeting specific sources of unfairness (which they cannot reliably identify). Conversely, in calmer stretches with high cooperation and more modest use of the mechanism, they can flip into a more supportive mode, rewarding many at once. Overall, they adapt at a very coarse level: they keep contributing to maintain social efficiency, and they sometimes use punishment or reward in large, non-selective bursts that reflect their general impression of the group climate rather than careful tracking of individuals. Their type is a robust cooperator with erratic, low-precision social control behavior, willing to sacrifice payoff to express approval or disapproval but not reliably aligning those expressions with conventional fairness standards.

The player observes a rich environment of rewards and punishments: some peers spend heavily on reward, others on punishment, and some players are repeatedly targeted. Despite this, their behavior toward contributions stays relatively stable—mostly full contributions with sporadic free-riding—indicating they do not dramatically adjust their own cooperation level in response to the pattern of others’ payoffs, punishments, or rewards. They continue contributing even when they personally receive unexplained deductions, showing a tolerance for “noise” and an ability to decouple their contribution decision from short-term perceived unfairness. They do not appear to mirror others’ styles: they do not start rewarding after seeing others do it, and their punishment choices do not align with the main punishers’ apparent norms. They also do not systematically shield or support heavily punished victims through reward. Instead, they act as if others’ redistribution behaviors are largely exogenous noise, except in rare cases where they single out a player for heavy sanctions. The criteria for those sanctions are not observable in the transcript; they are not directly linked to current contributions or obvious deviations from the group pattern. Their responses thus look more like sporadic expressions of strong disapproval or frustration than like ongoing norm-driven governance. Overall, this type responds to others’ outcomes predominantly by maintaining their own cooperative baseline and occasionally venting through strong, focused punishment, while otherwise ignoring the complex reward–punishment ecology around them.

They appear behaviorally indifferent to the pattern of punishment, rewards, and resulting payoffs among others. They continue their own contribution norm even as others spend extremely large amounts on punishment and reward, sometimes in seemingly arbitrary ways. They do not adjust contributions upward when rewarded, downward when punished, or differentially when others are heavily punished or highly rewarded. They also do not “join in” when clear coalitions of punishers or rewarders emerge; they remain on the sidelines of that dynamic. Being punished and rewarded themselves does not change their sanction behavior (which stays at zero) or their general contribution pattern (which remains high). There is no evidence of strategic alignment with particular players (e.g., supporting those who are targeted, or avoiding contributing when certain players are active punishers). They neither protect victims of harsh sanctions nor pile onto them. Their observable response is thus: maintain stable, near‑maximal contributions and ignore the entire second‑order economy of punishment and reward, treating those flows as exogenous noise that may alter their payoff but not their strategy. This implies that their beliefs likely include some combination of: “others’ sanctioning patterns are unpredictable or not worth responding to,” “my best role is just to keep cooperating,” or “side‑payments are not my responsibility.” In personality terms, they present as steady, non‑confrontational, and minimally reactive to social approval/disapproval signals, focused instead on following their own cooperative rule and maximizing earnings by conserving coins otherwise.

The player monitors others’ outcomes and reacts in ways that reflect concern for group norms more than concern for relative payoff ranking. They do not appear to chase the highest possible personal payoff; they accept reduced earnings from both punishment and frequent rewarding while maintaining maximum contributions. When they observe others heavily punishing or being heavily punished, they do not systematically join in, pile on, or shield specific individuals. They sometimes act in line with the group’s apparent moral sentiment (for example, supporting the cooperative norm by rewarding consistent contributors), but other times remain neutral even when others spend large amounts on sanctions. This indicates that they process the outcome structure but maintain their own internal criteria rather than blindly following majority sentiment. They do not visibly adjust their contributions downward in response to seeing others profit from defection or from extreme use of sanctions. Nor do they escalate punishment when they themselves are targeted; their response to personal deductions is muted, with no clear retaliatory spike against likely punishers. This suggests relatively low sensitivity to personal harm from sanctions and a higher priority on overall cooperative structure. When some players bear very large punishment costs or deductions, the focal player does not move to compensate them with offsetting rewards, nor do they exploit weakened players. Their behavior remains consistent: full contribution, occasional selective punishment, and modest, dispersed rewards. This stability suggests a personality that is principled, norm‑driven, and resistant to being swayed by short‑term shifts in others’ fortunes. In a new PGG, this player therefore behaves as a steadfast high cooperator who uses sanctions primarily to express and uphold an internal fairness/cooperation norm, and who relies more on positive reinforcement than on harsh punishment to shape the group, while tolerating personal payoff losses in pursuit of that cooperative ideal.

The player attends to others’ contribution choices more than to the fine details of their payoff outcomes. Their behavior adapts to who free-rides versus who cooperates, but does not clearly track how much each peer has been punished or rewarded, except in one notable case where they give a small reward to a player who has previously spent a very large amount on punishment and later on reward while continuing to cooperate. Heavy punishment expenditure by others does not lead them to join in or escalate; they remain comparatively restrained even when others severely sanction defectors. They do not retaliate when they themselves receive deductions: they continue full cooperation and do not respond with counter-punishment or reduced giving. They also do not chase high payoffs or low payoffs with their interventions; their targets are tied to contribution behavior, not to who is currently rich or poor. In periods where everyone contributes fully and outcomes are relatively equal, they mostly switch off both punishment and reward, suggesting they are satisfied and see no need to intervene further. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes mainly insofar as those outcomes reflect adherence or deviation from a shared cooperation norm, with a strong bias toward maintaining their own cooperative standard rather than constantly reshaping others’ payoffs.

This player is behaviorally indifferent to others’ payoffs, punishment, and reward activity in the sense that these observations do not alter their own strategy. They observe severe punishment of some players, heavy reward-giving by others, occasional free-riding, and spontaneous waves of cooperation and sanctioning, yet their own pattern—always full contribution, no sanctions, no rewards—remains unchanged. They do not appear to reward those who reward them, nor punish those who deduct from them. This suggests they either (a) do not place weight on fairness in second-order interactions, focusing only on their own contribution norm, or (b) consciously separate “what I do” (contribute fully) from “what others do,” choosing not to engage in payoff-based responses. Their lack of behavioral adjustment in response to being occasionally targeted with deductions implies a high level of tolerance for inequality and noise: they absorb unfair or unexplained hits without attempting to correct them or signal disapproval. At the same time, they do not exploit others’ generosity; they never take advantage of reward-heavy or punishment-enforced cooperative phases by secretly defecting. Instead, they seem to treat the entire environment—others’ contributions, punishments, and rewards—as background conditions, while they adhere rigidly to a simple personal policy: always cooperate fully, and never spend on social control. In summary, this player is a “principled non-enforcing cooperator”: they consistently maximize their contribution to the group, ignore both temptations to free-ride and opportunities to sanction or reward, and remain unaffected in behavior by the complex and sometimes extreme payoff patterns generated by others.

The player observes clear differences in payoffs, including free-riders earning more and certain cooperators repeatedly spending on rewards. They never comment on these outcome patterns, do not criticize defectors, and do not praise or discourage heavy rewarders. They also do not adjust their own contribution, punishment, or reward decisions in response to observed exploitation or altruism. This indicates a non-reactive stance toward others’ outcome profiles: they hold their own cooperative rule constant and appear indifferent, or at least behaviorally unresponsive, to the evolving distribution of payoffs. Whether they experience fairness concerns internally is unknown; externally, their behavior remains calm, consistent, and non-confrontational.

The player sees that others sometimes gain higher payoffs by not contributing and that some players spend substantial amounts repeatedly on rewards. They also see that this spending reduces the rewarders’ own payoffs. Despite this, they do not visibly react—no chat comments, no shifts in contributions, no move to punish free-riders, and no decision to start rewarding strong cooperators. They do not join in when another player suggests a group punishment norm against non-contributors, even though they initially verbally support full cooperation. Their behavior suggests a certain detachment from outcome-based social control: they maintain their own cooperative choice irrespective of whether others profit more or less, or whether others are “overpaying” to reward. They neither exploit the rewarders’ generosity by changing behavior (they are already maximally cooperative) nor support them by sharing the cost of rewards. Their sensitivity to distributional fairness at the payoff level appears low in terms of action: they tolerate outcome inequalities without behavioral adjustment, focusing solely on their own rule of full contribution.

They are attentive to others’ behavior and outcomes in a qualitative sense but rarely comment on specific payoffs or costs. When another player is spending heavily on rewards, they respond with a supportive remark highlighting the kindness of that behavior rather than its inefficiency. This indicates they value generosity and moral framing over strict payoff optimization in social commentary. They do not protest or criticize when one group member repeatedly free rides and earns higher payoffs; instead, they quietly continue to cooperate and do not adjust contributions downward in retaliation. They also do not react publicly to the large sacrifices made by the generous reward-giver, beyond a single positive comment and one small reward. Overall, they notice others’ efforts and deviations but maintain a non-confrontational, affirming stance, prioritizing group harmony and prosocial norms over explicit enforcement or payoff-based critique.

The player monitors others’ contribution patterns and aggregate outcomes and uses rewards to shape the environment rather than changing their own contribution level. They observe persistent free-riding by some players who accumulate high payoffs while contributing nothing or very little; nevertheless, they continue to give full contributions and do not redirect rewards toward these high-payoff non-contributors. High payoff alone does not attract their support; visible cooperative effort does. They also see that some peers bear large reward costs, sometimes to the point of markedly reducing their own payoffs. The focal player does not imitate this extreme level of expenditure, but they consistently adopt a “middle-high” reward-spending profile, indicating that they are willing to share the burden of sustaining cooperation without competing to be the most generous at all costs. Their behavior suggests they interpret others’ reward spending as a positive signal of cooperative intent and reliability, which reinforces their own willingness to keep contributing and rewarding. They do not appear to adjust contributions downward when others’ payoffs from free-riding exceed theirs; envy or inequality aversion in the sense of reducing cooperation is not evident. Their main response channel is to keep rewarding cooperators, sometimes more intensely when contribution by others is low, suggesting they see themselves as responsible for helping keep cooperation alive in the group rather than reacting defensively to exploitation. Overall, they behave as a robust, norm-driven cooperator and active pro-social rewarder, moderately reciprocal but strongly anchored in a personal standard of full contribution and conditional rewarding of cooperative behavior.

The player watches a wide range of outcomes—costly punishment cascades, heavy use of reward by some, free-rider windfalls followed by sanctions, and extreme spending by a few “altruistic” or erratic participants—without visibly reacting in chat or changing their core contribution strategy. Their single early punishment act coincides with a prior low contributor who had just been sanctioned, implying that they are sensitive to patterns of deviation and willing to “pile on” enforcement in defense of the cooperative scheme. Their later three-person punishment act aligns with a period of complex reward and punishment that distorts simple cooperative payoffs; this suggests they attend to payoff structure and perceive overactive or misaligned use of secondary mechanisms as problematic or unfair. However, they do not begin rewarding after seeing others earn large gains via reward, and they do not copy hyper-punitive or hyper-rewarding styles. They learn primarily one lesson from others’ actions: free‑riding draws real and substantial sanctions, making it unprofitable; consequently, they stick with full contributions. Overall, they respond more through quiet adjustment of their own behavior (ending defection and using rare norm-enforcing punishment) than through active experimentation with the broader incentive tools others are using.

They pay attention to others’ outcomes and the group’s enforcement dynamics but keep their own behavior simple. They acknowledge and lightly praise cooperative behavior (e.g., early positive comments about others “coming in clutch” or about “max time”) and verbally endorse the idea that coordination yields higher payoffs. When others are punished heavily for defection, they do not express sympathy or outrage; they mostly ignore the drama and keep focusing on max contributions and finishing quickly. They recognize the existence of bonus payments and discuss them in chat, aligning with others’ focus on collective payoff and time-efficiency. When the group debates mutual reward schemes to exploit the fact that rewards are net-positive, they verbally signal willingness to participate but fall back to the safer, simpler equilibrium of just maxing contributions without extra mechanisms, especially once others express concerns about trust and complexity. When some players are punished multiple times or spend heavily on rewards/punishments (with visible large payoff losses), they do not adjust their strategy to exploit or compensate those players. Their response to others’ costly behavior is essentially: maintain cooperation, avoid using costly mechanisms themselves, and keep the game moving. Overall, they act as a stable, non-enforcing, non-manipulative cooperator who recognizes group-level incentives but prioritizes low-friction, straightforward play over fine-tuned optimization.

They pay some attention to others’ outcomes and behavior, but their reactions are restrained. They make lighthearted comments when someone behaves ideologically (“let’s not be tories”), and later, when a known defector is punished heavily, they publicly state distrust of that player, aligning verbally with enforcement without spending coins themselves. When others discuss punishments, rewards, and losing points, they do not moralize strongly or escalate conflict; they let others discuss penalties and simply continue to cooperate. They participate in meta‑discussion about the study’s design and bonus structure, and even proactively share the completion code for the benefit of the group, indicating concern for others’ real‑world payoffs. Overall, they appear attentive to fairness and group earnings but prefer soft social signals (agreement, distrust statements, sharing helpful information) over directly manipulating others’ in‑game payoffs.

They pay attention to others’ contributions, punishments, and rewards and reference global patterns and bonuses more than individual micro-outcomes. They notice and verbally reinforce the fact that everyone is contributing maximum amounts, highlighting “big bonus” potential and celebrating reaching threshold totals. Their focus is on collective success and bonus payments, not on tracking who is slightly ahead or behind. When one player defects early by contributing nothing, they accept and join the group’s harsh punishment episode. Their subsequent comments emphasize that non-cooperation hurts the defector more and that there is bonus money tied to points, implicitly reinforcing that defection is both irrational and socially discouraged. When some players later use reward and punishment heavily and distort payoffs (others ending rounds with very low or very high net points), they do not comment on those extremes, nor do they intervene with counter-punishment or compensatory reward. Their response is effectively “stay the course”: continue contributing max and not get involved in complex payoff engineering. They do not express hostility toward any individual, including defectors, but they do emphasize that the group gets a bonus according to accumulated points, implicitly valuing cooperative outcomes and long-term earnings. Their reactions to others’ outcomes thus show: (1) endorsement of punishing blatant free-riding when the group is aligned, (2) indifference to small, noisy deviations, and (3) an overarching orientation toward stable cooperation and shared monetary benefit.

They pay attention to the distribution of punishments and rewards and to how others’ payoffs are affected, but their reactions are mostly implicit and expressed via their own reward choices or light commentary rather than explicit judgments. They do not join in the joking “pile‑on” against low contributors, nor do they verbally condemn harsh punishments; instead, they quietly counterbalance by rewarding a heavily punished participant substantially and giving smaller boosts to steady cooperators. This indicates concern for fairness and aversion to excessive inequality, even when it costs them. They also support the group’s focus on maximizing shared bonus earnings and on avoiding coordination schemes (like universal rewarding) that create exploitable asymmetries or slow the game down. When others worry about timing, crashing, or study design, they respond with humor and reassurance, reinforcing group cohesion rather than anxiety. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes in a calm, prosocial, and efficiency‑oriented way: maintain full cooperation, correct perceived over‑punishment with positive transfers, avoid drama, and keep the group moving.

They attend to and interpret others’ payoffs and sanction flows, and occasionally comment in a way that reveals a norm-enforcement mindset. They correctly perceive which avatar had previously defected (“green bird”) and bring that history up later, suggesting they track reputations across rounds. Their own punishing choices align with this tracking: they hit known low contributors even when others are also punishing them, implying they deliberately reinforce the cost of deviation. When the group is discussing multipliers, bonuses, and the possibility of reward schemes, they show awareness of how the structure might be exploited but favor simple, robust full cooperation over more fragile side-arrangements. They refrain from punishing or rewarding once outcomes stabilize at near-perfect equality, indicating sensitivity to the idea that further sanctioning would be unnecessary or inefficient in that environment. Overall, they interpret others’ outcomes through a lens of “are they upholding or breaking the group-efficient, fair pattern?” and act accordingly, even at personal monetary cost. Overall type: a principled, high-cost norm enforcer and unconditional cooperator who uses both punishment and reward to stabilize group-wide full contribution, respects group welfare over individual payoff, and maintains a calm, lightly reflective social presence.

The player clearly tracks others’ actions but responds mostly through contribution stability and occasional sanctions rather than chat. They observe and react to: (a) deviations from full contribution, which trigger punishment; (b) restoration of cooperation by former deviants, after which they stop punishing and even later reward; and (c) large punishment and reward expenditures by others, which they do not imitate. They do not join episodes of heavy rewarding or punishing that are not directly about first‑order contribution, indicating that they are not drawn into cycles of escalating enforcement or generosity. They also do not adjust their own contribution level downward in response to others’ earning more through strategic rewards or free‑riding; their cooperative norm is robust against local inequality. There is no evidence of envy or of opportunistic mimicry of free‑riders once those free‑riders receive high net payoffs through others’ actions. Instead, they continue with a simple rule: contribute fully, punish clear defectors, occasionally reward clear cooperators, and ignore the rest. This suggests a principled, rule‑based type who focuses on contribution norms rather than on fine‑tuning individual payoffs.

They do not comment directly on others’ payoffs, punishments, or rewards in a way that shows moral judgment or empathy. They ignore episodes where others spend heavily on punishing or rewarding, and they do not praise or criticize extreme behaviors (for example, players devoting many coins to reward/punish in mid-game). They show no interest in punishing dominant free-riders elsewhere, nor in celebrating players who use reward altruistically. Their only explicit discussion of outcomes is at the meta-level: they emphasize that participants have “already been paid,” casting the in-game bonuses as less critical. In the reward-strategy debate, they focus on expected marginal gain per person and the complexity of fair allocation, treating others’ potential outcomes in a calculative rather than emotional way. They seem largely indifferent to the fine-grained distribution of payoffs across players, as long as group cooperation remains high enough to keep returns good. Their primary reference group is “we” at the level of everyone contributing 20, but when it comes to sanctions and extra mechanisms, they step back and treat them as optional and somewhat unimportant add-ons rather than central moral concerns. Overall type label: “Pragmatic Cooperator with Opportunistic Deviations, Non-Punishing and Non-Rewarding” They typically cooperate by contributing the maximum, mainly because the group coordinates on that and the payoffs are solid. They occasionally defect when they judge that the personal downside is tolerable, especially after reminding themselves and others that core payment is already secured. They avoid both punishment and reward entirely, treating those mechanisms as not worth the personal cost, and respond to sanctions with practical acceptance rather than anger, while maintaining a lightly rebellious, humorous communication style.

They observe a wide range of others’ outcomes, including heavy punishment of free riders, costly reward experiments, and later, fine‑tuned sanctions targeting a specific unreliable cooperator. They almost never comment directly on specific payoff patterns or target choices. Their single explicit response related to mechanisms is to the proposal of a mutual reward scheme: they frame their rejection in terms of effort and time rather than fairness or trust. They do not publicly pressure defectors, applaud punishers, or criticize those using the mechanisms in idiosyncratic ways. Instead, they quietly stick with max contributions and endorse the simple cooperative “just max out” approach in line with others’ comments. They also do not exploit knowledge of others’ outcomes to improve their private payoff beyond the one occasion of contributing nothing (which could be exploratory, convenience‑driven, or simply opportunistic; motivation is unknown). After that, they ignore the possibility of targeted free‑riding or strategic reward/punishment based on observed histories. In sum, they monitor the environment enough to agree with the group’s high‑cooperation equilibrium and to understand that extra mechanisms exist, but they choose not to respond to individual outcomes with tailored strategic behavior. Their dominant reaction to others’ outcomes is to maintain stable, simple cooperation and favor fast, low‑effort play.

They pay attention to others’ outcomes and the effect of reward and punishment, and they comment on them in ways that reveal both norm concerns and payoff thinking. When others free‑ride or get heavily punished, they react with light joking mixed with normative framing (e.g., references to collective responsibility and loss of bonus). When a chronic deviator is hit with strong sanctions, they do not defend that player; instead, they emphasize the monetary consequences of such behavior (damage to the bonus), aligning more with the punishers’ perspective than with the punished. They show sensitivity to group‑level earnings: they talk about the conversion of points to money, express interest in total bonus size, and support strategies that maximize shared payoffs with minimal complexity. When someone notes extreme sanctioning of a defector, they do not question whether the punishment is excessive; instead they focus on the impact on the defector’s own outcomes and on the group’s incentives. They also engage with meta‑outcomes—how the experimenters might interpret the group’s cooperative behavior and the role of chat. They speculate that coordination and consistent cooperation are likely part of what the researchers want to observe. This indicates a higher‑order perspective: they view payoffs and outcomes not only as individual gains but as data in a study about social behavior. In sum, this player is a “non‑punitive, mostly unconditional cooperator with occasional generosity and mild opportunism,” oriented toward group efficiency, light norm enforcement via talk, and simple, stable strategies rather than complex enforcement regimes.

The player exhibits tolerance of inequality and limited behavioral retaliation. They continue to contribute generously even when low-contributing or zero-contributing peers earn substantially higher payoffs than them. This implies a relatively low propensity to respond to payoff disadvantage with sharp defection. Their small downward adjustment in contribution appears to respond to the combination of others’ low contributions and their resulting high payoffs, but the response is measured rather than punitive. They do not mirror the lowest contributors’ behavior, nor do they try to equalize payoffs by drastically reducing their own cooperation. Instead, they nudge their behavior toward more self-protection while still allowing free-riders to benefit from the public good. Because no explicit punishment or reward mechanisms are available, their only observable “response” channel is their own contribution level. They use this channel weakly and conservatively: they signal some dissatisfaction with persistent free-riding via a partial reduction in generosity, but they stop short of a full breakdown in cooperation. Their behavior suggests they may interpret others’ free-riding as disappointing but not sufficient reason to completely abandon their cooperative norm. Their beliefs about others’ intentions or types are unknown, but their actions are consistent with viewing cooperation as a value in itself, not solely as a reciprocated exchange.

The player appears attentive to others’ outcomes and responsive to observed payoff asymmetries. When low-contributing individuals receive comparatively high payoffs, the focal player later adopts a similar low-contribution, payoff-maximizing approach. This suggests that they interpret such payoff structures as a cue that free-riding is both feasible and normatively “permitted” in practice, even if not desirable. They do not have access to direct punishment or reward tools in this setting, so their primary means of responding to others is through their own contribution choices. Their shift away from high cooperation functions as an indirect sanction: instead of continuing to subsidize others, they withdraw support and align their behavior with the more self-interested strategies they have observed being rewarded. Their adaptation over time indicates that they update beliefs about group behavior based on outcome patterns rather than stubbornly holding to an initial strategy. They seem neither naïvely trusting nor permanently resentful; rather, they calibrate their level of cooperation to the realized incentive environment. Emotional states are unknown, but behaviorally they demonstrate a pragmatic, outcome-sensitive stance: if others benefit from contributing less, they are willing to emulate that approach, especially once future cooperation is less valuable.

This player repeatedly experiences higher payoffs than most high contributors, yet does not move toward matching their generosity. When others contribute a lot and earn relatively less, the player continues to contribute at a low level, implying comfort with payoff asymmetries that favor them. They do not appear to be motivated by inequity aversion in a way that would reduce their advantage; there is no clear effort to sacrifice payoff to bring their earnings closer to others’. As some peers later reduce their contributions and obtain higher payoffs through more blatant free-riding, the player does not escalate to extreme free-riding themselves, but also does not shift upward to compensate for the public good losses. Their response to changing others’ outcomes is muted: they slightly adjust contributions but keep the same general low-contribution, payoff-maximizing stance. This suggests limited emotional or normative reactivity to others’ gains or losses and a relatively stable, self-focused strategy. Any inferences about their internal beliefs, moral judgments, or feelings beyond this behavioral stability are unknown or low confidence.

This player observes that some peers consistently contribute much more and earn less, while others contribute much less and earn more, yet they do not adjust in response to these outcome patterns. They neither move toward the high-contribution norm of the more cooperative players nor toward the lower-contribution strategy that yields higher payoffs for free-riders. This suggests limited social comparison and weak payoff-driven learning within the short game. They do not appear motivated to match others’ payoffs, correct perceived unfairness, or retaliate against low contributors through their own behavior. Their stance toward inequality is behaviorally neutral: they tolerate both others’ disadvantage (high contributors earning less) and others’ advantage (low contributors earning more) without changing their own strategy. Emotional reactions and underlying beliefs about fairness or trust are unknown; only a behavioral pattern of persistence and low reactivity to others’ outcomes is evident.

The player consistently benefits more than the contributors whenever others cooperate, and they make no behavioral move toward reciprocity or compensation. They continue to free-ride even when others’ payoffs are clearly lower than their own, indicating low or absent concern for distributive fairness or for equalizing outcomes. They observe that some peers repeatedly sacrifice part or all of their endowment yet end up with lower earnings, while the player themselves and a few non-contributors earn more. Despite this visible payoff asymmetry, the player does not reduce exploitation or attempt to align with the cooperative majority. This suggests that they interpret others’ lower payoffs as acceptable collateral to maximize their own gains, rather than as a signal to adjust toward more equitable outcomes. Because explicit punishment and reward are not available, direct reactions via sanctions cannot be observed. However, their lack of contribution in a context where many others keep contributing implies that they are either indifferent to others’ welfare and relative earnings, or they believe others will continue to cooperate regardless of their own defection. Emotional reactions (such as guilt, pride, or shame) are unobservable and thus remain unknown. From behavior alone, they appear payoff-focused, comfortable with advantageous inequality, and unmotivated by reputational or reciprocity concerns within the constraints of this game.

This player observes that others frequently contribute and often receive lower payoffs than they do when they defect, yet they do not respond by joining early cooperation or by stabilizing cooperation later. Their reaction to others’ outcomes is thus largely exploitative in practice: they continue to free-ride while the group provides a strong public good, and they do not incur any cost to support or protect high contributors. They never have access to punishment or reward in this game, so direct behavioral responses via sanctions or bonuses are impossible to observe. However, their contribution choices in the face of others’ payoffs serve as an indirect measure of their social response. When others’ generous behavior yields them high personal payoffs, they continue to defect rather than reciprocate, indicating a low tendency toward gratitude-based or reciprocity-based adjustment. When some others reduce their contributions and overall outcomes fall, they still maintain defection, suggesting limited conditional cooperation or moralistic response to declines in public-good provision. Their one cooperative move does not clearly align with an apparent change in others’ outcomes that would make cooperation especially attractive or justified; it places them in a worse relative position compared to free-riders. This suggests that, if they are responding to others’ outcomes at all, they are doing so in a noisy or poorly calibrated way—perhaps experimenting, perhaps briefly conforming, but not in a way that systemically rewards or stabilizes cooperation. There is no observed effort to manage inequality in payoffs. They are comfortable benefiting when cooperators earn less than them, and they also tolerate earning less than defectors later without clearly reversing course. This combination points to a player who is not strongly inequality-averse and who does not consistently adjust behavior to protect either their dominance or their status relative to others. Overall, their implicit response to others’ outcomes is: tolerate and exploit others’ cooperation for personal gain; do not systematically reciprocate high contributions; show at most a weak, one-off cooperative response that does not reliably encourage or reward others’ behavior. Their beliefs and emotional reactions to others’ outcomes remain unknown, but their behavioral pattern fits a mostly self-interested, low-reciprocity type.

The player observes repeated instances where noncontributors earn higher payoffs than themselves, as well as variation in others’ contribution patterns. Despite this, they do not change their own behavior or attempt any strategic response via available mechanisms; they maintain full contributions without any punitive or rewarding actions (with the caveat that no formal punishment/reward tools are available in this game). Their nonreaction in terms of behavior suggests low behavioral sensitivity to outcome inequality and exploitation. They do not appear to engage in payoff-based reciprocity—there is no shift toward self-protection when others benefit from their cooperation without contributing. Likewise, there is no attempt to align with emerging group norms (which are more selfish than their own stance). Instead, they persist with their initial cooperative policy. There is no direct evidence of emotional responses (such as anger or disappointment) because there is no chat and no available punishment/reward. However, inferred from their unwavering contributions, they either (a) downplay or accept payoff differences as part of playing cooperatively, or (b) prioritize adherence to their own cooperative standard over correcting others’ behavior. In summary, when faced with others’ higher or lower payoffs, the player continues to act according to a stable cooperative rule rather than adapting to reduce inequality, punish freeriders, or reward cooperators. Their type is best characterized as a steadfast, efficiency-oriented cooperator with low observed responsiveness to others’ payoff outcomes.

The player pays close implicit attention to payoff rankings and uses them to guide behavior. After seeing that defectors earn more than contributors under the existing group pattern, they align themselves with the better-paid group (the non-contributors) and remain there. Their one cooperative act is immediately followed by a shift back to free-riding once it yields a lower payoff than that of non-contributors while still leaving contributors in the group. They show no evidence of reciprocity toward high contributors in terms of adjusting their own behavior to protect or reward them. Even when others continue to invest in the public fund alongside clear non-contributors, this player chooses to remain in the advantaged defector position. This suggests low concern for inequality that favors them and little inclination to incur costs to support cooperators. There is no punishment or reward mechanism in this environment, so direct enforcement responses cannot be observed. However, their behavioral adjustments indirectly “reward” free-riders (by joining them and confirming that defection is payoff-maximizing) and “penalize” cooperators (by refusing to share the contribution burden). This is consistent with a competitive or at least self-centered orientation: they appear more motivated by maintaining a favorable payoff position than by maximizing group welfare. They exhibit rapid learning from outcome feedback: a single experience of being worse off than defectors while contributing is sufficient to shift them back to defection and keep them there. There is no sign of longer-term experimentation with different strategies to see if group outcomes improve; outcome sensitivity is short-horizon and self-focused. Their likely response pattern in new PGGs is: - Track whether cooperators earn more or less than defectors. - If defectors are doing at least as well or better, continue to defect. - If cooperation were somehow tied to strictly higher personal payoffs, they might be willing to cooperate instrumentally, but not out of principle. Their personality style, as inferred from outcomes, is instrumental and self-regarding rather than norm-driven or pro-social. They are responsive to others’ outcomes mainly as signals for how to secure or preserve their own advantageous position. Concern for relative payoff and avoidance of being the “sucker” being exploited by others’ defection are prominent; concern for mutual benefit beyond their own gain is weak or unknown but likely low.

The player can observe others’ payoffs but has no punishment or reward tools here, so responses must be inferred from changes in their own contribution choices. Early on, they continue to cooperate despite visible evidence that non-contributors systematically earn more than contributors. This persistence indicates a relatively high tolerance for being out-earned in the short term and a willingness to “give the group a chance,” even when freeriders are clearly benefiting. Over time, as freeriding remains common and the payoff gap between contributors and non-contributors stays unfavorable, they eventually pivot to the self-serving option. This change appears to be triggered not by a single outcome but by the cumulative pattern: others often keep more for themselves yet enjoy the benefits of the shared fund. Their eventual defection therefore signals that they are attentive to relative outcomes and react when they judge that cooperative behavior is not reciprocated enough. They do not show evidence of strong spite or vindictiveness; when they finally free-ride, they do so in a context where many others are also contributing, which maximizes their own payoff rather than specifically targeting any individual. Since they lack targeted punishment or reward options, their only available means of responding to others’ outcomes is to adjust their own contribution. They use this mechanism once, in a way that closes the payoff gap for themselves and resembles a threshold-based, self-protective response rather than emotional retaliation. Their behavior suggests they track group-level patterns more than individual histories: their adjustment seems keyed to the overall mix of contributors and non-contributors, rather than mirroring any one peer. Overall, they behave like a cooperative but outcome-aware participant who is initially willing to bear inequality but eventually reacts when others’ better outcomes appear persistently unearned.

This player is observant of outcome patterns and adjusts strategy based on realized payoffs, but there is no evidence of punitive or rewarding behavior, nor of direct emotional reactions, because no explicit punishment/reward actions occur in the game. They repeatedly experience situations where non-contributors earn as much or more than contributors, and they update their behavior in a manner consistent with learning from these comparisons. When they and other full contributors earn less than free-riders, they subsequently align with the more profitable free-riding pattern. This suggests that they attend to relative earnings and are sensitive to being at a disadvantage, even if they do not have instruments to express disapproval directly. They do not respond to cooperative behavior by reciprocating once they have turned to free-riding. Even when multiple peers continue contributing and thereby sustain a highly profitable public fund, they keep their own contributions at zero while benefiting from others’ generosity. This indicates that positive outcomes for others, by themselves, are not sufficient to elicit renewed cooperation once they have reclassified the situation as one where free-riding is acceptable or norm-consistent. Because formal punishment and reward are unavailable, it is unknown whether they would be willing to incur a cost to sanction free-riders or to reward cooperators in other environments. There is also no direct information about emotional responses (e.g., guilt, resentment, gratitude); all inferences are based on strategy changes. With that caveat, their pattern suggests a primarily payoff-driven response to others’ outcomes: they imitate the more profitable role (free-rider) rather than trying to influence the group, and they tolerate inequality when it is in their favor.

The player observes that free riders consistently earn higher payoffs than contributors in many situations, yet they do not alter their own behavior in response. This indicates that, in practice, they do not retaliate, compensate, or otherwise react strategically to others’ payoff outcomes. They show no evidence of behavioral response to the presence of systematic exploitation: no drift toward self-protection, no apparent attempt to match others’ low contributions, and no visible concern with equalizing payoffs. Because explicit punishment and reward mechanisms are disabled, there is no direct evidence of how they would spend resources to change others’ payoffs if given the option. However, their continued willingness to accept disadvantage while still cooperating is consistent with a low tendency to engage in payoff-targeted responses, whether punitive or rewarding; they appear not to condition their actions on who gains or loses in prior periods. They also do not appear to selectively support high contributors versus low contributors through their own behavior; everyone benefits equally from their full contributions, regardless of past actions. This suggests that, if they form judgments about others, those judgments do not translate into differentiated treatment within the constraints of this game. Their implicit stance can be summarized as: maintain cooperation independent of others’ outcomes, even when those outcomes are clearly unequal and sometimes unfavorable to themselves. Their emotional or cognitive reactions to these inequalities are unknown; only the behavioral side—robust non-response in terms of strategic adjustment—is evident.

The player consistently accepts outcomes in which non-contributors earn more than they do, without any observable behavioral retaliation or payoff-protective adjustment. They keep cooperating even when non-cooperators systematically obtain higher payoffs. Because punishment and reward tools are unavailable, there is no direct evidence of how they would spend resources to influence others; however, their unchanged full cooperation in the face of repeated exploitation suggests low propensity for payoff-based retaliation and high tolerance for inequality when it arises from others’ selfishness. They do not engage in payoff matching: others’ advantage does not induce them to reduce their own contribution to restore parity. This implies that observing others’ outcomes influences them weakly, if at all, in terms of strategic adjustment. Their likely belief is that it is appropriate to maintain their cooperative stance independent of what others earn, or that changing behavior in response to others’ higher payoffs is not desirable. They exhibit no sign of conditional kindness (rewarding high contributors by increasing their own contributions) or conditional spite (reducing cooperation when facing free riders); instead, they follow a fixed cooperative rule that is robust to seeing both fair and unfair payoff distributions. Overall, they present as a steadfast, non-retaliatory cooperator whose behavior is largely insensitive to observed payoff patterns of others.

The player is highly responsive to others’ payoff outcomes and clearly notices the payoff advantage of free-riding over contributing. After observing that defectors systematically earn more than cooperators, they promptly shift to full defection and maintain it while others continue to contribute, which shows that upward payoff comparisons matter and can trigger opportunistic behavior. They appear comfortable benefiting from others’ contributions without immediately reciprocating, so they do not hold a strict norm of avoiding advantageous inequality. Later, they voluntarily return to full contribution even when this choice yields lower earnings than that of some free-riders. This indicates that their response to payoff differences is not purely envy-driven or maximization-based; they tolerate disadvantageous payoff positions when they choose to act cooperatively. This suggests a mixed motivational profile: they react strongly to evidence that “suckers” lose out and temporarily avoid that role, yet they are also willing to re-enter a cooperative role even when it is known to be less lucrative. Since no punishment or reward is available in this environment, their reactions are expressed only through their own contribution choices. They neither exit cooperation permanently in response to exploitation by others nor blindly continue giving in the face of persistent free-riding. Instead, they update their behavior in both directions, which is consistent with an adaptive learner who uses others’ outcomes as feedback about when cooperation seems “worth it” or normatively appropriate. Their social preference type, inferred solely from these outcome-contingent switches, is an outcome-sensitive, ambivalent cooperator: they respond to payoff inequalities by alternating between opportunism and renewed attempts at cooperative contribution.

They observe others’ outcomes—including extreme generosity in some players’ reward spending and occasional free-riding in contributions—but show no explicit, strategic response. Overall, they appear behaviorally tolerant and unreactive: others’ payoff patterns, reward expenditures, and contribution deviations do not visibly change their own choices, aside from their own planned final-round defection.

They show no explicit verbal reaction to others’ payoffs, contribution levels, or reward expenditures, even when some group members contribute nothing or spend large amounts on rewards. Overall, they behave as a quiet, principled cooperator who focuses on maximizing the public good through contributions while largely ignoring the strategic and social “side game” of differential reward and payoff tracking.

They appear highly attentive to others’ contributions and outcomes, as reflected in the structured, group-wide way they allocate rewards when cooperation is high and in how they continue to support contributors even after some players free-ride. There is no evidence of negative responses such as withholding cooperation or introducing punishment-like behavior (which is not available mechanically but could be mimicked by stopping rewards); they do not retaliate materially against high-payoff free-riders by reducing own contributions. Instead, their reactions to others’ high or low payoffs are entirely positive: they reinforce cooperative behavior with rewards and accept that defectors may earn more in some rounds. The absence of complaint or visible frustration suggests either high tolerance for payoff inequality or a primary concern with sustaining the cooperative norm rather than enforcing strict fairness.

They show limited explicit concern with the detailed payoff and reward patterns of others. Their chat focuses on understanding the rules and on mild social commentary rather than on outcome monitoring. Their response to others’ outcomes is therefore tolerant, low-conflict, and relatively non-strategic, consistent with a “quiet cooperator” who cares more about doing the normatively right thing and enjoying the group context than about optimizing or enforcing payoff fairness.

They pay attention to others’ actions and outcomes, especially when someone appears not to have contributed or is unusually generous with rewards. Early in the game, when contributions are incomplete, they quickly notice who has not contributed and mention these players by name, using this information to encourage compliance rather than to threaten or shame. When some players struggle with the interface, they infer that the problem may be procedural (e.g., not pressing the final confirmation) and offer a concrete suggestion, indicating they attribute some low contribution to confusion rather than malice. They also notice and publicly acknowledge a player who gives large amounts of reward, joining others in praising that generosity. However, they do not alter their own contributions or reward patterns in response to these observations: free-riding by a few does not lead them to reduce their own contributions, and observing heavy reward-giving does not lead them to match that behavior. Overall, this player type is a **steadfast, norm-driven cooperator who relies on full contribution and light, polite communication to sustain group efficiency, while using the reward mechanism only sparingly and mainly expressing gratitude and norm enforcement through words rather than coins.**

They show no explicit reactions—positive or negative—to others’ payoffs, contribution choices, or reward expenditures. They do not call out high payoffs of free‑riders, do not comment on generous reward givers, and do not reference others’ outcomes when explaining or justifying their own play (they never do so at all). This points to a type that cooperates unconditionally and largely ignores the detailed distribution of gains and costs within the group.

The player pays attention to others’ contributions and inferred outcomes, especially early on. Once the group is coordinating near-perfectly, they shift from pointing out shirkers to celebrating the group’s success and “teamwork,” and they encourage consistency from prior laggards in a light, humorous way. Overall, they are outcome-aware in terms of cooperation levels and norms but relatively indifferent, at least in overt behavior, to the fine-grained distribution of payoffs, to inequities created by defection, or to the costs borne by extreme rewarders.

They clearly monitor whether others contribute or not and care about group-level outcomes. They notice and implicitly value the group’s shift toward full contribution, joining in celebration when everyone contributes and reinforcing the “we” identity. In contrast, they appear largely indifferent to the details of the reward flows and payoffs: they do not comment on extreme reward spending by others, on free-riders earning more in defecting rounds, or on who is particularly generous with rewards. Their focus is on contribution behavior and overall group cooperation level, not on fine-grained payoff distribution or reputation through reward.

They show minimal explicit reaction to others’ payoffs or reward behavior. When others comment on generous rewarders, they do not add to the praise or tie it to their own strategy. Overall, they quietly adapt their own contributions toward the cooperative norm while largely ignoring or not publicly engaging with others’ detailed payoff and reward decisions. Their stance toward others’ outcomes is passive and reputation-protective rather than actively moralizing or strategically evaluative.

The player observes others’ contributions and outcomes but reacts mildly. They do not publicly shame low contributors or free-riders and never threaten to change their own contributions in response. Their primary behavioral “response” to observing others’ outcomes is to maintain their own cooperative strategy and occasionally give minimal rewards, indicating a preference for stability and harmony rather than active sanctioning or strategic repositioning.

The player is sensitive to how others’ contributions and payoffs evolve and updates behavior accordingly, but they do not display any direct enforcement tendencies because neither punishment nor reward is available. Instead, their “responses” are encoded in their own contribution adjustments after seeing others’ outcomes. When others coordinate on high contributions, yielding equal and high payoffs, the player often continues or escalates their own contribution, supporting the cooperative equilibrium. This indicates that they value mutually beneficial, symmetric outcomes when the group appears cohesive.
