They appear to interpret outcomes primarily through the lens of “who contributed how much,” rather than “who earned how much” or “who was costly generous via rewards.” When others’ contributions are slightly below a de facto group standard (e.g., someone giving just under 20 when nearly all others give 20), they respond with public criticism or pointed questioning. This indicates they use contribution levels as a proxy for fairness and group loyalty. However, when under-contribution is more widespread or when plans fail, they do not launch prolonged blame campaigns; instead they simply adjust their own contributions up or down in later rounds, showing behavioral adaptation rather than extended emotional reaction. They also intermittently support renewed coordination pushes after high-cooperation episodes, even if some players have previously deviated or “played tactically.” This suggests they do not permanently ostracize lower contributors; rather, they are willing to reset expectations and try again if the group signals new willingness to cooperate. Their lack of targeted reward usage reinforces this: they neither materially punish nor materially reward based on observed payoffs, relying instead on verbal norms. In summary, this player tracks and cares about others’ cooperative actions, especially relative to stated agreements, and publicly calls out shortfalls. But they do not deeply integrate others’ payoff sacrifices, reward usage, or net outcomes into their own strategic calculus; their primary response channel is verbal pressure plus conditional adjustment of their own contributions.

This player closely tracks others’ contribution behavior and, to some extent, their payoffs and reward flows. They repeatedly refer to who is “out” or under-contributing and call them out in chat, particularly when a specific player continues to give less than the agreed-upon amount. They also comment on others’ generosity (for instance, calling for certain players to be “more generous”) and celebrate group-wide high-contribution outcomes with positive remarks. They notice discrepancies between their own efforts and the rewards they receive, explicitly remarking that they gave rewards widely but received very little. This indicates a sensitivity to fairness and reciprocity in outcomes. However, they do not escalate to punitive behavior (none is available) or sustained retaliatory under-contribution directed clearly at specific deviants; instead, their under-contribution occurs even when they themselves are leading norm-setting, so it is not cleanly interpretable as response to others’ misbehavior. They are quick to join or echo group sentiment when others criticize chronic deviators, suggesting they use social labeling (“out”) as a low-cost way to sanction. When someone labeled as a deviator responds by increasing their contribution, the focal player acknowledges this with surprise or mild praise, showing that they update their perception and are willing to integrate former deviators back into the cooperative narrative. Overall, this player is attentive to who earns more versus less and who contributes more versus less, and they use chat to socially reward or pressure others. Their own material contributions and rewards, however, do not reliably align with their stated fairness and reciprocity concerns, making them appear verbally pro-cooperation but behaviorally mixed between cooperative norms and opportunistic payoff-seeking.

They pay attention to others’ contributions and inferred strategies more than to exact payoff totals, but they clearly notice who is effectively doing well by exploiting or deviating. They openly call out players who contribute less while the rest contribute more, labeling such behavior as problematic and predicting that persisting in it will reduce that person’s own earnings and harm the group’s gains. This shows an outcome-focused fairness norm: they care that cooperative efforts translate into higher shared earnings and that strategic defectors do not free-ride unchallenged. They show approval and satisfaction when group-wide high contributions succeed, expressing positive emotion when “everyone” finally aligns on a high amount. They also express mild frustration and fatigue toward repeated deviators, aligning themselves with others in the chat who criticize those players. However, they do not escalate into spiteful behavior via the reward system; they respond with persuasion and social commentary rather than costly counter-measures. In summary, this player monitors behavioral and payoff patterns, prefers high-payoff, high-cooperation states, and reacts to deviations mainly by communicative pressure and tactical adjustment of their own contribution level, with only minimal use of formal rewards as a response to others’ outcomes.

They show no direct chat reaction to others’ payoffs, extreme reward expenditures, or large imbalances across players. While some participants comment on who is “out” or who is being stingy or generous, this player does not publicly join those judgments. They are aware of patterns indirectly—since their contributions shift with group behavior and chat—but they do not verbalize any moral approval or disapproval of others based on final payoffs or reward flows. Behaviorally, they respond to others’ actions mainly by adjusting their own contributions in line with the dominant emerging norm rather than discriminating between specific individuals based on their outcomes. They do not appear to target or favor highly cooperative or highly rewarded players via the reward mechanism, nor do they seem to retaliate against low contributors through strategic under-contribution; instead, their shifts (e.g., between full and half contributions) look like responses to overall group climate rather than to specific payoff histories. Their relative silence about others’ extreme reward spending suggests either disinterest in micro-level payoff distribution, a focus on their own earnings, or reluctance to criticize others’ strategies. They appear to care more about whether the group as a whole is moving toward coordinated high contributions than about who is currently winning or losing in payoff terms. When coordination is strong, they are willing to match it even if some individuals are extracting additional benefits through side-payments; when coordination falters, they reduce their contributions without publicly blaming anyone. Overall, their response to others’ outcomes is muted, indirect, and group-level rather than person-specific.

The player appears largely indifferent to the distribution of others’ payoffs and to the detailed structure of who is gaining or losing. They do not comment when some players suffer heavy losses from their own reward spending, nor when others accumulate high gains due to receiving rewards. They do not reference others’ round earnings or complain about free-riding, even when some players contribute much less or when coordination attempts are undermined. The fact that others sometimes reward them does not visibly change their behavior; they neither express gratitude nor reliably reciprocate to specific individuals in later rounds, at least in observable communication. Their heavy reward spending seems disconnected from observed outcomes like “who contributed most,” “who followed the plan,” or “who is currently being ostracized.” They also do not visibly react to public labeling dynamics (such as others calling out specific players as “out” or criticizing low contributors). Overall, their behavioral adjustments show little sensitivity to the pattern of others’ contributions, rewards, or payoffs, suggesting a type that is playful, impulsively generous, and only weakly responsive to social or outcome-based incentives.

The player tracks others’ contributions and outcomes enough to direct rewards, but they do not visibly react by downgrading cooperation when others earn more or behave opportunistically. Even when others gain high payoffs while contributing little or when some players burn large amounts on reward in idiosyncratic ways, the player continues to contribute fully and to spend on rewards. They do not appear sensitive to inequality in favor of others; there is no behavioral evidence of envy‑driven reduction in contributions or rewards. Conversely, they sometimes receive substantial rewards, but this does not change their pattern either—they neither ramp up nor cut back in apparent response to being rewarded. Their behavior suggests that they value maintaining a prosocial, high‑contribution environment more than adjusting to the exact distribution of payoffs. They seem tolerant of others’ noisy or “wasteful” use of the reward mechanism and do not try to correct it via their own actions. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes primarily by continuing to support contributors with rewards and high contributions, rather than by strategically exploiting, punishing, or equalizing payoffs.

Their observed reactions are behavioral rather than verbal: they adjust their punishment targets based on others’ contribution levels and, to a lesser extent, on whether a player previously free-rode despite then cooperating later. They continue to sanction someone who once defected, even if that player later contributes fully, indicating that a prior deviation has lasting consequences in their eyes. They do not visibly respond to who is punished or rewarded by others except in the sense that they join the majority in targeting clear free-riders; they neither protect heavily punished deviants nor counter-punish those who punish. When they themselves are punished, they do not change their contribution strategy or retaliate, suggesting that their primary reference point is the contribution norm rather than their own payoff or treatment. Overall, they interpret payoff and sanction patterns through a fairness-and-discipline lens and act to reinforce that structure regardless of short-term personal gain.

They observe that others both punish and reward, often at substantial cost, but they neither imitate nor respond in kind. Their own contribution behavior remains unchanged when some players defect or receive heavy punishment. They also do not adjust their sanctioning behavior after being personally punished and rewarded in the final round. This suggests emotional restraint or low reactivity: they do not escalate, retaliate, or “reward the rewarders.” They appear to interpret outcome information primarily as confirmation that cooperation is possible and widespread, rather than as input for fine-tuned strategic responses. Their stance toward others’ very high punishment expenditures and mixed reward usage is behaviorally neutral; approval or disapproval is unknown.

They observe that others use punishment heavily against low contributors and occasionally against high contributors, and that rewards are also used in targeted ways. Their own behavior does not visibly adjust in response to these outcome patterns: they neither start punishing, nor reduce contribution, nor stop rewarding after being targeted. They do not explicitly comment on others’ payoff losses or gains. There is no clear evidence that they try to infer identities of their punisher(s) or to respond strategically. Their pattern is consistent with a player who maintains their cooperative and non-punitive norms regardless of whether they personally gain, lose, or are treated unfairly. Their reactions are behavioral rather than verbal, and those behaviors are stable and resilient to others’ sanctions, suggesting a type best described as a quiet, steadfast cooperator with a preference for positive reinforcement and a high tolerance for being exploited or mis-punished.

They observe that some players are heavily punished for low contributions while others invest significantly in sanctioning, yet they do not adjust their own contribution or sanction behavior in response. They neither join the “punish defectors” pattern nor move toward protecting or compensating punished cooperators. Their one use of reward goes to a player who both deviates and is sanctioned, which could indicate sensitivity to perceived over-punishment or sympathy toward disadvantaged players, but this inference is low-confidence. Overall, they appear largely outcome-tolerant: they accept that others will use punishment and reward aggressively without trying to steer or counterbalance that dynamic. They stick to their own cooperative norm and occasional reward use, without visible emotional or strategic reaction to who gains or loses from the sanctioning system.

They observe that fully cooperative players are sometimes punished, that defectors are sometimes heavily sanctioned, and that some players spend significant amounts on punishment and reward. Despite this, they make no visible behavioral adjustment: they neither join the sanctioning activity nor reduce their own contribution levels. They do not comment on others’ payoffs, sanction spending, or perceived fairness of outcomes, and there is no sign of emotional reaction to being punished or to others being punished. This indicates a relatively stoic or detached response to the outcome phase: they register the environment sufficiently to support a simple “everyone should contribute fully” norm but do not become more strategic, more retaliatory, or more reward-oriented when they see how others are treated. Their type is thus a consistent, non-enforcing cooperator who largely maintains their own standard without actively intervening in others’ payoffs.

They consistently observe that some players earn high payoffs while contributing very little, and that some co-players burn resources through frequent, sometimes aggressive use of the reward mechanism. Despite this, the focal player does not adjust their own contribution downward, does not punish via strategic non-reward, and does not shift into a more self-protective style. When others under-contribute but earn high payoffs, the focal player shows no retaliatory behavior; they neither reduce their own cooperation nor spend to counterbalance inequality. When others spend heavily on rewards—sometimes in ways that reduce their own payoffs dramatically—the focal player does not imitate this pattern, suggesting they regard extreme reward spending as non-normative or inefficient. Their rare reward burst goes to clear cooperators, not to those engaging in costly, erratic reward patterns or to low contributors. Overall, they react to others’ outcomes with behavioral inertia: they register that norms in the group are mixed but persist in a stable, high-cooperation, low-intervention strategy, selectively acknowledging high cooperators once but otherwise allowing others’ payoff differences and spending choices to stand without adjustment.

The player observes substantial variation in others’ contributions, reward expenditures, and resulting payoffs. They see some players repeatedly making very low contributions yet sometimes receiving rewards, and others spending heavily on rewarding. Despite this, the player does not imitate costly rewarding nor retaliate against low contributors through the available mechanism (which, in this game, is only positive reward). Their response is limited to moderate early caution in contributions followed by robust cooperation once it becomes clear that the majority is strongly contributing. They tolerate persistent low contributors without targeted countermeasures, apparently accepting a degree of inequity in payoffs as long as the group-level public good remains high. They also do not adjust their behavior to exploit heavy rewarders: even after seeing others spend large amounts on reward, they do not reduce their own contributions or free-ride more aggressively. Overall, they track the group’s cooperative climate and adjust contributions upward as it stabilizes, but they remain behaviorally indifferent to who exactly is earning more or less, who is sacrificing via rewards, and who is freeriding, beyond the coarse effect on their sense that “the group is mostly cooperating.”

This player clearly pays attention to others’ contributions and payoffs, as evidenced by how their own contributions and reward spending shift when others are extremely cooperative versus more mixed. When others’ contributions are very high, they are willing to free‑ride more aggressively, securing high private payoffs and temporarily suspending reward spending. When the contribution landscape is more heterogeneous, they invest more in both contributing and rewarding, suggesting that they are responsive to perceived need for behavioral shaping. They do not appear to be strongly equality‑driven: large payoff advantages for some players, or heavy self‑sacrifice by others through reward spending, do not trigger compensatory behavior to smooth payoffs. Instead, they seem primarily sensitive to how others’ actions affect their own returns and to the perceived stability of high cooperation. They tolerate persistent low contributors in the group, sometimes even reinforcing them with reward, implying an acceptance of some free‑riding as long as overall group output remains high. Overall, they are an outcome‑monitoring, efficiency‑oriented conditional cooperator who opportunistically exploits especially favorable group states and uses rewards selectively to nudge, rather than to enforce strict fairness.

The player is attentive to others’ contributions and payoff patterns and adapts in a measured way. They consistently observe a clear low contributor who earns high payoffs due to others’ generosity yet rarely changes their own contributions in direct retaliation, implying that envy or anger at high earners is limited, or at least does not dominate their behavior. They also witness one counterpart who repeatedly spends heavily on rewards, often in seemingly inefficient or idiosyncratic ways. The player does not imitate that extreme rewarding pattern; instead, they adopt a moderate, more selective rewarding strategy, suggesting independent judgment and some concern for personal payoff efficiency. They respond more strongly to broad shifts in group cooperation than to isolated behavior: when the group collectively increases to very high contribution levels, the player ramps up rewards to the group, indicating a desire to consolidate and encourage that cooperative state. When the group remains mixed or when reward behavior by others looks less coherent or less outcome-improving, the player scales back their own rewards and later stops rewarding entirely. This indicates learning and strategic adaptation: they update their willingness to spend on others based on observed returns in terms of group behavior and their own payoff trajectory, while still preserving a high level of contribution. Overall, they come across as a principled, group-oriented high cooperator who uses rewards to shape norms early and mid-game, then gradually shifts toward conserving resources once it appears that additional rewards add little marginal benefit.

The player observes a group that frequently spends large amounts on punishment, sometimes in ways that drastically reduce their own payoffs. They do not imitate this extreme punitive behavior: they remain comparatively moderate in punishment use and do not join the strongest “punishment coalitions.” They sometimes cooperate when the group is highly cooperative and earning good outcomes, but also sometimes defect in such environments, showing only weak responsiveness to collective welfare patterns. They do not strongly reward high contributors through either increased own contributions or selective non-punishment; nor do they systematically exploit them. Their reaction to others’ large negative outcomes from over-punishment appears neutral: they neither capitalize on others’ self-harm with a clearly opportunistic defection strategy nor align with them by expending similarly large punishment costs. Overall, they seem somewhat detached from the emerging group-level dynamics, experimenting with their own contribution level and light punishment while others engage in intense sanctioning battles.

This player closely observes that some others spend very heavily on punishment and often end up with very low or negative payoffs. In contrast, this player avoids that extreme and maintains relatively higher and more stable payoffs. Their behavior suggests they recognize that heavy, continuous punishment is costly and unsustainable. They seem to use this observation to justify a restrained punishment strategy and frequent free-riding. They experience episodes in which high group contributions produce high payoffs, and also many episodes in which widespread punishment and mixed contributions produce low or negative payoffs for others. They do not clearly align themselves with either “cooperative enforcers” or “pure selfish exploiters.” Instead, they appear to opportunistically choose whether to join the cooperative cluster or to exploit it, depending on how chaotic the environment looks. They do not appear to reward high contributors (since rewards are unavailable), nor do they systematically protect or favor those who end up with low payoffs due to punishment. Their observed response to others’ outcomes is pragmatic: they avoid the extremes of costly altruism and costly retaliation, and they continue playing in a way that keeps their own payoffs relatively favorable compared to several peers. Their pattern suggests that they infer that strong norm enforcement by others is unreliable and often self-destructive, and they adapt by remaining flexible: sometimes cooperating to take advantage of high-contribution phases, sometimes free-riding when contributions or punishments by others create exploitable situations. They treat others’ low payoffs from over-punishing as cautionary examples rather than cues to participate in enforcement.

This player pays attention to others’ contributions and final payoffs, and appears highly reactive to visible punishment flows. They frequently target players who already suffer large deductions, repeatedly driving one particular peer into strongly negative outcomes, which suggests they may derive satisfaction from others doing badly or from maintaining dominance over specific opponents. They are not clearly sympathetic to players who incur large punishment costs themselves; they do not coordinate with them to stabilize cooperation or to protect mutual punishers. Instead, they often continue to pursue their own punishing agenda even if others are also burning resources in punishment, contributing to destructive spirals rather than stepping back when the group as a whole is losing. They do not seem to condition their contributions on others’ payoffs in a prosocial way. When others’ punishment costs and deductions lead to low payoffs for cooperators, this player does not systematically shift to cooperative behavior to help restore group efficiency. Their main adjustment appears to be in whom to punish rather than in whether to contribute. Beliefs about others are inferable only indirectly, but their behavior is consistent with expecting ongoing conflict and low trust: they act as though the environment is adversarial and that it is acceptable or even desirable to hurt others, even at personal cost. They do not move toward a stable high-payoff equilibrium even when the group shows capacity for high mutual contribution. In type terms, this is a reactive, conflict-prone, punishment-focused player who views others’ outcomes more as a battlefield to influence than as a joint resource to optimize.

The player appears largely insensitive to others’ payoffs, except in the sense that they are willing to drive some peers to extreme losses. They do not seem motivated to preserve others’ earnings or the group’s efficiency, given that they repeatedly choose actions that reduce both. Observing others suffer large deductions from multiple punishers does not lead them to reduce their own punishment; they continue to spend heavily even when a target is already being heavily sanctioned, suggesting low concern about “overkill.” Likewise, high contributions and high payoffs for cooperators do not induce them to maintain cooperation; after seeing collective success, they revert to free-riding and often resume heavy punishment. They show no evidence of positively responding to others’ generous behavior, and no clear tendency to protect consistent cooperators from harm; instead, they sometimes punish even when others cooperate. They appear outcome-tolerant in the sense that they accept very volatile and sometimes negative personal payoffs as a cost of pursuing their punitive pattern. Their behavioral responses imply a type that prioritizes damaging disliked others over maximizing own or group earnings, and that does not use others’ outcomes as feedback for adapting toward more mutually beneficial behavior.

They do not visibly react to others’ payoff swings, extreme punishment spending, or reward cascades through changes in contribution, punishment, or chat. They remain unmoved by the dramatic and repeated sanctions on a low‑contributor and by the very large reward and punishment costs some players incur. They neither join piling on the punished player nor attempt to offset their losses with compensating rewards. At the same time, they do recognize and reinforce pro‑social behavior: they selectively reward those who contribute highly and sometimes those who bear costs to support others. This suggests a focus on reinforcing the cooperative “center” of the group rather than intervening directly in conflicts or extremes. Their beliefs about the efficiency or fairness of the group’s heavy punishment dynamics are unknown, but their behavior is to stay cooperative, avoid sanctions, and give modest encouragement to visible cooperators without being drawn into the more aggressive or dramatic strategies of others.

The player pays attention to both others’ contributions and the downstream payoffs that result from punishment and reward decisions. Their sanctioning is sensitive to revealed contribution patterns rather than to final wealth levels: they target low contributors even when those players are not the top earners, and they ignore some high earners who fully contribute. They do not escalate against players who are already heavily punished by others; instead, their own punishment intensity tapers off over time, suggesting some concern about proportionality or diminishing returns. They show no evidence of “second-order” punishment (punishing those who fail to punish) or of targeting aggressive punishers for meta-sanctioning. The overall pattern is: track who cooperates vs. who free-rides, occasionally enforce norms with significant but not constant punishment, briefly attempt positive reinforcement for cooperators, and otherwise accept that others’ outcomes will vary, while maintaining a largely cooperative stance themselves.

The player appears attentive to others’ contribution and sanction patterns but focuses primarily on behavior, not payoffs per se. They do not mirror others’ heavy use of punishment or reward, even when they can see sizable expenditures and large deductions. Instead, they carve out a middle line: occasionally punishing conspicuous free-riders while ignoring much of the sanction “arms race” around them. They are not drawn into spirals of mutual punishment and do not try to manipulate others’ payoffs via reward. Other players’ outcomes seem to matter to them mainly as signals of who is persistently defecting versus who is broadly cooperative. When a peer’s low contributions persist while they themselves are cooperating, that peer becomes a likely target, regardless of that peer’s current payoff level. They show no evidence of pitying low earners or protecting high earners; outcome-based sympathy or envy is not apparent. Their learning or adaptation is modest: they adjust who they punish and how often they contribute in light of emerging free-riding, but they do not radically shift to full defensiveness or full altruism even as the group environment changes.

They appear attentive to others’ contribution patterns and to the flows of punishment and reward across the group, and they adjust their rewarding accordingly. They frequently direct rewards toward players who have recently been punished or experienced net payoff losses due to deductions, even when those players are not the highest contributors. This behavior is consistent with a concern for “second-order fairness” (who is being treated harshly) rather than just “first-order” contribution levels. They do not seem to imitate other players’ punitive behavior; even when punishment becomes frequent or is aimed at particular free-riders, they do not join in. Instead, their reaction to harsh sanctioning is often to reward those on the receiving end, softening the blow. They also sometimes reward moderate or low contributors who receive no punishment, which can be interpreted as an attempt to spread goodwill or maintain cohesion despite conflict elsewhere. Their own contribution choices are weakly linked to group outcomes: they sometimes increase contributions when the group is highly cooperative and seems stable, but do not reliably reduce free-riding when others are punished for it. They therefore respond more via the “reward channel” than by changing their own contribution level. Overall, this player type is: a non-punitive, reward-focused, opportunistic cooperator who mostly free-rides but spends nontrivial resources to provide positive transfers—often to those harmed by punishment or under-contributing—suggesting a conflict-averse, compensatory, and somewhat empathetic social style rather than a strict norm enforcer or pure selfish maximizer.

This player pays close attention to others’ contributions, payoffs, and sanctioning behavior, and updates their own conduct based on these patterns. They react strongly when they see some players earning high payoffs while contributing little, often targeting those players with punishment. They also notice when others spend heavily on punishment or reward and seem willing to complement that by punishing additional defectors rather than duplicating efforts on the same targets, suggesting some responsiveness to the broader sanctioning landscape. As the game progresses, if they observe many others withholding contributions or engaging in chaotic sanctioning, they increasingly withhold their own contributions and reduce or suspend punishment, indicating a conditional willingness to invest in cooperation only when it looks salvageable. They do not appear to chase the absolute highest payoff individuals simply because they are successful; rather, outcomes matter to them mainly insofar as they reveal free‑riding or consistent cooperation. Their overall response style is that of a conditional norm‑enforcing cooperator who gradually shifts toward self‑protection when group‑level norms fail.

They appear attentive to others’ contributions and sanctioning behaviors and adapt both their own contributions and their use of punishment in response. They consistently target low contributors with punishment, particularly a repeated free-rider who continues to invest in reward rather than contribution—suggesting the focal player interprets that pattern as objectionable and deserving of continued sanctions. They do not punish high payoff earners simply for being successful, indicating they are not motivated by envy. Instead, they tolerate and sometimes even help sustain situations where other players earn more than they do, as long as those players contribute. When group contributions deteriorate, they occasionally free-ride themselves, taking advantage of the situation and perhaps signaling frustration or resignation, but they later return to full contribution, which suggests a mixed strategy rather than a collapse into pure defection. They also refrain from punishing some low contributors and from rewarding some high contributors in later rounds, implying a shift from active norm-shaping toward more self-regarding or minimally involved behavior as the game progresses. Their reactions to conspicuous punishers and rewarders are nuanced: they do not automatically support heavy punishers or heavy rewarders, instead continuing to focus on contribution behavior as their main cue. Overall, they behave like a conditional cooperator with a norm-enforcing streak, willing to incur personal costs to discipline chronic free-riders, but also occasionally exploiting cooperative environments and gradually scaling back costly enforcement.

They observe that many others frequently spend on punishment and reward, often sacrificing substantial payoff to enforce or encourage norms, yet they never imitate or complement these behaviors. Their choices do not track others’ costs or outcomes in a clear way: they continue to free-ride even when cooperators and enforcers are visibly paying heavy costs, and they do not step in to help stabilize cooperation when some players are exploiting or being exploited. They appear to treat others’ complex sanctioning and rewarding patterns as background noise rather than information to update on. There is no sign they differentiate between generous cooperators, harsh punishers, or lenient free riders in their own decisions. This suggests a low sensitivity to social structure and reputations in the game, or a belief that tracking such details offers little personal advantage. Their overall style is that of a detached, individually focused player who watches others’ strategic struggles without joining in the social enforcement dynamics.

This player monitors others’ outcomes sufficiently to make non-random, selective sanctioning decisions. They focus attention on players who: - repeatedly appear in the punishment/reward columns (frequent users of the mechanism), or - have unusually high or low net outcomes compared to the group. Their own sanctions target such players rather than all defectors or all high-payoff individuals, suggesting that they are sensitive to the “meta-game” of who is actively shaping the environment, not just raw contributions. At the same time, they show tolerance of widespread free-riding in many later rounds: when many others contribute nothing or little and the public fund declines, they do not increase either punishment or reward usage to steer the group back toward high cooperation. They continue to free-ride themselves and keep sanction use sparse. This implies that, although they observe the decline in group efficiency, they prioritize their own payoff stability over actively managing the social norm. In sum, they are an observant but self-focused type: they notice who is doing what, occasionally intervene with targeted punishment or a small reward, but they do not take on the role of group norm enforcer or alliance builder. They adapt modestly to the evolving pattern of others’ contributions and sanctions but retain a fundamentally opportunistic, mixed-strategy approach.

They observe a rich pattern of behavior from others: some players contribute consistently, some free-ride, some allocate large amounts to punishment or reward. Despite this, they keep their own post-redistribution behavior in the punishment/reward stage at zero throughout, and their contributions do not track particular shifts in group sanctioning or payoff structures. This suggests they monitor outcomes but prioritize immediate, private payoff maximization over norm-based responses. They do not seem concerned with equalizing payoffs or protecting exploited cooperators; they tolerate significant inequality, including their own temporary disadvantage due to being punished, as long as they can continue to benefit overall. Their lack of response to heavy punishment and reward episodes directed at others indicates that they do not update beliefs in a strongly contingent way on observed enforcement—there is no sign that seeing defectors punished leads them to consistently cooperate, nor that seeing cooperators rewarded leads them to adopt similar behavior to “join the winners.” Overall, they behave as a largely detached observer of others’ outcomes, treating the social environment as exogenous and focusing on extracting favorable payoffs within it, rather than trying to shape it.

The player clearly pays attention to others’ contributions and to the pattern of punishment and reward costs, because their own strategy shifts over time in ways that correlate with group-level behavior. They sometimes increase contributions when many others are contributing, and sometimes defect in cooperative environments, indicating that they notice but do not rigidly follow the majority. They observe that several peers spend heavily on punishment and/or rewards, yet they refrain from matching this intensity. Instead, they adopt a more restrained approach, occasionally punishing but usually abstaining, and never rewarding. This points to a comparative evaluation: they see others sacrifice large amounts for norm enforcement or signaling, but they do not emulate that degree of generosity or aggressiveness. Their reaction to others’ high or low payoffs is implicit: they do not intervene consistently to correct “unjust” high payoffs of defectors or to support “unfairly” low payoffs of cooperators. They appear more focused on their own payoff trajectory and on testing different mixes of cooperation and defection than on tightly managing the group’s distribution. In summary, this is an independent, pragmatic type who monitors the social environment but responds in a selective and somewhat idiosyncratic way: willing to cooperate or defect as it suits them, occasionally using punishment as a blunt tool, ignoring rewards as a strategic instrument, and only weakly swayed by being punished or rewarded themselves.

The player pays close attention to others’ contributions and the pattern of punishments and rewards, but they respond more through occasional targeted sanctions than through broad shifts in contribution. They tolerate substantial inequality in realized payoffs: they continue to contribute fully even when low contributors, or those avoiding sanction, end up with significantly higher earnings. This indicates a relatively high threshold for reacting to unfair outcomes. When they do respond, it is aimed at specific individuals whose outcomes and histories likely stand out as unfair or strategically harmful (e.g., chronic free‑riders or those benefiting repeatedly while contributing little). They do not attempt to equalize payoffs across the group via widespread punishment, nor do they systematically subsidize low earners or high cooperators with reward. Instead, they apply “spot corrections” to a few players, sometimes coupled with expressive reward to others, suggesting a mental model that focuses on key actors rather than overall distribution. They seem to infer that the group can sustain cooperation if a few outliers are disciplined or encouraged, while they themselves keep playing as a consistent cooperator regardless of short‑term payoff disparities.

They observe others’ payoffs, including how much each player pays to punish or reward and the resulting deductions and bonuses. They see that many others spend heavily on punishment, often aiming at low contributors, and that these actions significantly change others’ outcomes. Despite this, they do not imitate punishment behavior, suggesting either disapproval of harsh enforcement, skepticism about its value, or a desire to maintain a “positive-only” reputation. Their rewards tend to track who is helping the public good rather than who is being punished or rewarded by the group. They do not appear to chase high-payoff players with rewards, and they do not reward or punish in order to align themselves with apparent “winners.” Outcomes that reflect free riding and heavy sanctioning do not lead them to reduce contributions or become more self-protective; they maintain principle-driven cooperation and selective positive reinforcement. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes by continuing to support cooperators through reward, while ignoring both the temptation to defect for higher payoffs and the ongoing punitive dynamics among peers.

When some players free-ride and earn higher payoffs than contributors, this player shows no overt negative reaction, no attempt at shaming, and no indication of withdrawal of cooperation. Their only explicit reaction related to others’ strategic commentary is to confirm agreement with a general efficiency principle, not to address the payoff inequality that arises. They neither punish nor reward (none is available) and do not verbally substitute for punishment by blaming or moralizing. The consistent continuation of full contributions after observing free-rider gains suggests either high tolerance for inequity in the short run, a focus on maintaining a cooperative norm despite defections, or a low salience of others’ relative payoffs for their own behavioral rule. Their responses indicate that they update their understanding of group strategy (they acknowledge the condition “as long as the multiplier is over 1 we contribute”) but they do not let negative outcome asymmetries disrupt their cooperative stance. Their internal beliefs about fairness standards beyond this are unknown; what is clear is that they prioritize sustaining efficient cooperation over reacting to opportunistic behavior.

They clearly monitor group outcomes and others’ choices, as suggested by their early explanation that everyone contributing the maximum leads to a higher collective bonus and by their later reaffirmations to “do the same” when the cooperative profile is working. When some players temporarily free ride and gain higher payoffs, there is no observable retaliation, shaming, or visible frustration from this player; they continue to contribute fully and do not mention the payoff disparity. This suggests a tolerance for some inequity or at least a reluctance to respond punitively or conditionally, even when they can see that others’ strategies give those others higher payoffs in that situation. Their behavior indicates a belief that maintaining or restoring full cooperation is more important than reacting to short-term unfairness. They appear to infer that as long as the game’s multiplier is favorable, the right collective strategy is full contribution, and their reaction to outcomes is to keep advocating that norm rather than to adjust behavior in response to others’ deviations. Overall, they respond to outcome information by reinforcing cooperative coordination, not by protecting themselves against exploitation or trying to enforce fairness through behavioral change.

When others deviate and earn higher payoffs by not contributing, this player shows no visible backlash, no attempt to shame, and no adjustment in their own contribution pattern. They neither call out free-riders nor try to renegotiate norms after exploitation occurs. Instead, they continue contributing fully on subsequent rounds, suggesting that they either downplay the significance of short-term unfairness, prioritize maintaining group cooperation, or accept occasional exploitation as part of the game. Their reactions are thus characterized by tolerance and non-retaliation. They also show some attention to the payoff-generating mechanism, as reflected in their later comment about the multiplier, but they do not explicitly tie that observation to others’ payoffs or use it to pressure behavior. Overall, they respond to outcome information by quietly persisting in cooperative behavior rather than by adapting strategically or expressing emotion.

When everyone cooperates and payoffs are uniformly high, they respond positively and succinctly, expressing satisfaction with the outcome. When a subset of others free-ride and earn more than contributors, they do not express anger, blame, or calls for retaliation or adjustment; they also do not reduce their own contribution afterward. There is no observable attempt to shame or pressure the defectors after seeing their higher payoffs. Instead, they quietly maintain the cooperative strategy and remain aligned with the general “as long as the multiplier is above 1, we should contribute” reasoning voiced by others. Overall, they appear resilient to short-term inequality in outcomes and prioritize continuing the efficient, high-contribution equilibrium over reacting to defections. Emotional reactions to others’ specific payoffs beyond mild satisfaction with mutual cooperation are unknown or low-confidence due to limited explicit commentary.

When others’ choices temporarily reduce the public return and create unequal payoffs in favor of free‑riders, the player shows no explicit anger, protest, or attempts at sanctioning via social pressure. They do not single out or confront the higher‑earning noncontributors, nor do they threaten to change their own behavior in response. Instead, they persist with full contributions and seamlessly return to the cooperative pattern once others do so. This suggests relatively high tolerance for short‑term exploitation and a focus on restoring or maintaining group efficiency rather than enforcing strict reciprocity or punishment norms. They do not comment on individual payoffs, which makes their sensitivity to outcome inequality largely unknown, but their unchanged behavior in the face of temporary unfairness implies they are not strongly reactive or retaliatory. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes by maintaining their cooperative standard and trusting the group to re‑coordinate, rather than by adjusting contributions to punish or protect themselves.

The player sees detailed end-of-round summaries, including who punished whom and how much each person earned, but they show very little behavioral reaction to these outcomes. They continue to contribute fully regardless of others’ payoffs, the distribution of punishment costs, or free-riders’ high earnings. Their one small punishment coincides with someone contributing markedly less than the cooperative majority, so they are not entirely insensitive to misalignment, but their reaction is extremely muted and not repeated. They do not differentially target high earners or consistent defectors across rounds, and they do not break cooperation even when some members profit more by contributing less. They also do not comment on others’ outcomes via chat, never congratulating, criticizing, or calling out anyone for their payoffs or punishment behavior. This points to a “stay the course” strategy: they monitor enough to recognize who is not aligned but largely decouple their own behavior from these observations, maintaining personal cooperation and mostly declining to use outcome information to adapt in a punitive or strategic way.

They track group outcomes and others’ payoffs at a coarse level and repeatedly translate them into projected bonuses for “all of us.” They show awareness that some players are earning slightly more by contributing less, and they remark on this, but they minimize its magnitude and refrain from moral condemnation. They explicitly note that under-contributors are “not making much more” and that the extra is small compared to the group’s total gains. They praise the group when contributions are high and outcomes are strong, framing successful rounds as collective achievements. They do not comment on specific individuals’ punishment costs or deductions except in a general way (e.g., rejecting punishment as harmful to all). Their attention is on aggregate group performance and shared payoff levels rather than relative rankings or individual advantages. Overall, they react to others’ outcomes by reinforcing cooperative norms and expressing satisfaction with good group-level earnings, rather than seeking to correct or equalize payoffs via sanctions or defection.

They observe that group coordination is working and explicitly praise it, suggesting they track aggregate outcomes and value collective success. They do not comment on individual payoff differences, disproportionate gains of free-riders, or on the costs punishers incur; they focus on the fact that “we” are doing well and that cooperation is effective. They acknowledge the group’s coordination and express appreciation and closure (e.g., thanking others, saying “well done”), indicating satisfaction with joint outcomes despite personally bearing costs from others’ under-contribution and punishment attacks. They appear outcome-sensitive at the group level (happy that the group earns bonuses) rather than at an individual relative-payoff level. How precisely they feel about the distributional inequities between high contributors and partial free-riders remains unknown, as they never explicitly address it.

They occasionally comment approvingly when total earnings or bonuses rise, and they are motivated by the idea of “getting paid” and increasing group bonus levels. They do not single out individual earnings or complain about specific high or low payoffs. When others talk about the group’s expected bonus, they respond with positive short remarks, aligning themselves with a shared success frame. They show no explicit sympathy or gloating regarding punished players’ reduced payoffs; they do not verbally react to who is losing from punishment. At the same time, they continue to punish even when others point out that punishment lowers everyone’s payoff, which implies that the disutility they assign to those outcomes is either low or overridden by a desire to sanction perceived non‑cooperation. Overall, they pay attention to group‑level payoff improvements, endorse them, and are behaviorally willing to trade off some group efficiency for enforcing their own, partially opaque fairness or emotional norms.

They see detailed information on others’ contributions, punishments, and payoffs each round. Their observed behavior indicates that this feedback influences them, but only modestly. They remain highly cooperative even while some low contributors and non-punishers earn higher payoffs. This shows relatively low envy and a willingness to prioritize group earnings over personal relative standing most of the time. Their selective use of punishment suggests that they do pay attention to outcome inequalities and norm violations: they are willing to spend coins to reduce the payoff advantage of some players when deviations from the full-contribution norm are salient. However, they never vocalize such reactions in chat, instead expressing only positive or neutral sentiments (smile, weekend wishes, thanks). This implies that while unequal or “unfair” outcomes matter enough to motivate occasional punishment, they do not spill over into overt social hostility. They also do not visibly “reward” high contributors behaviorally by reducing their own contributions or withholding punishment; instead, they keep cooperating and remain mostly hands-off with punishment toward consistently cooperative players. In essence, they monitor outcomes, accept some unfairness, and correct only the most salient deviations via limited punishment, while keeping their social signals friendly and low-drama.

The player can observe everyone’s payoffs, punishment costs, and deductions, but their strategic adjustments relative to these outcomes are subtle. They do not imitate the more extreme strategies they see: they do not join the heavy punishers, and they do not follow the lowest contributors even when those low contributors appear to profit in some periods. They also do not obviously react to the suffering of heavily punished players by either siding with them (reducing their own contributions in solidarity) or siding with punishers (increasing contributions to avoid similar treatment). Likewise, they do not appear to reward the consistently high contributors by matching their level; they continue to contribute less than these group leaders, even while benefiting from their generosity. The few times when the cooperative structure breaks down or becomes more erratic, the player’s contributions shift only modestly. This indicates that they notice group-level outcomes enough to adjust slightly but are not strongly outcome-driven or emotionally reactive. They accept that others incur punishment costs and experience deductions without visibly trying to reshape their own strategy in response. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes in a mildly opportunistic but steady manner: they keep a personally profitable contribution level while letting others’ behavior and payoffs fluctuate around them, neither exploiting breakdowns aggressively nor investing to repair them.

They see detailed outcomes each round, including who punishes whom and how payoffs shift, but they remain publicly neutral about these patterns. They neither praise punishers nor defend punished players in chat. Their only explicit evaluative communication is positive reinforcement of collective cooperation and agreement with claims that over-punishment is bad for the group. They do not appear to track or respond to specific individuals’ payoff trajectories in a way that changes their own strategy. The main visible influence of others’ outcomes on them is indirect: they support the high-cooperation equilibrium others help establish and sustain, and they do not contest how other players choose to spend on punishment, even when that harms them. Overall, they act as a cooperative follower who orients to group-level success, not as a payoff-sensitive enforcer or critic.

The player observes others’ contributions, payoffs, and punishment decisions and clearly incorporates this information. They initially maintain high cooperation even when some players undercontribute, but over time they react by both lowering contributions in some periods and using punishment. This indicates that others’ outcomes influence their strategy: they are sensitive to apparent free‑riding and to the group’s overall payoff trajectory. They explicitly call for “penalize” in chat after seeing ongoing undercontribution and relatively high payoffs for some players, indicating they monitor inequality and are willing to reduce both their own and others’ payoffs to address it. At the same time, when others are highly cooperative and punishment is heavy from other players, they often refrain from punishing, suggesting they are not fixated on maximizing punishment or equalizing payoffs at all costs. They do not comment on specific individuals’ earnings or on fairness in explicit moral language; their one comment instead focuses on enforcement as a means to align behavior. Their observed adaptation—switching between full cooperation, partial free‑riding, and selective punishment—suggests they learn from others’ actions but do so in a somewhat noisy, ad‑hoc way rather than following a simple rule such as “match the group average” or “never punish if it hurts the group too much.”

The player observes a highly active social sanction environment: frequent, often costly punishments and rewards, significant deductions from both low and high contributors, and shifting payoff distributions. They also observe dramatic swings in total contributions and in others’ earnings across phases with high cooperation, collapse, and resurgence. Despite this, they do not adapt any aspect of their own behavior: they keep contributing at the same level and never join in sanctioning. They show no visible discrimination between exploited cooperators, opportunistic free-riders, heavy punishers, or generous rewarders. This indicates a detached or stoic stance toward others’ outcomes: they register that others are spending and losing coins but do not imitate, counteract, or strategically respond. Social signals via punishment/reward and payoff changes appear behaviorally irrelevant to them. They accept whatever group outcome results from others’ actions while following their own fixed contribution rule and avoiding all costly interventions.

This player appears to carefully observe the rich information about others’ contributions, payoffs, and punishment/reward activity and uses it to inform selective social responses, rather than ignoring it. They notice and repeatedly support a consistently high contributor who is often targeted by punishers, indicating sensitivity not only to contribution levels but also to how others are treating that player. They appear willing to counteract what looks like harsh or disproportionate punishment of a cooperator, by adding reward. This suggests a concern with procedural or interpersonal fairness in how outcomes arise, not just the final payoffs. Conversely, when they see individuals contribute almost nothing in group contexts where many others are giving more, they are willing to step in and punish, sometimes heavily. They are sensitive to context: they are more likely to sanction low contributors when the rest of the group is being relatively cooperative, and they do so even if the targeted low contributor is not uniquely the lowest in every instance. This points to a norm that “egregious” or repeated low effort in a cooperative environment deserves sanction, particularly when it stands out. However, they do not globally adjust their contributions toward fairness after seeing inequality; they remain content to both exploit and support the group at different times. Their responses to others’ outcomes thus combine: (a) selective concern for the treatment of a favored high contributor; (b) occasional moralistic punishment of salient free-riders; and (c) continued opportunistic self-play in their own contribution choices, largely independent of others’ short-term payoffs. Overall, this player’s type can be summarized as a “selective norm-enforcer and opportunist”: they watch others closely, care about certain fairness boundaries and specific individuals, and are willing to spend to punish or reward when those boundaries or loyalties are salient, while otherwise prioritizing their own earnings and tolerating substantial inequality and free-riding—including by themselves.

They observe a highly noisy environment with frequent punishment and reward by others, including antisocial punishment and substantial spending on costly sanctions. Their own reaction is to be selective: early on they are willing to join in norm-enforcing punishment of low contributors, but as it becomes clear that others use the mechanisms in idiosyncratic or extreme ways, they pull back and largely stop sanctioning. They do not mirror others’ extremes: when other players suffer huge deductions or spend heavily, this player does not adjust their own contribution or sanctioning to “match the mood.” Their contributions remain steady across high-payoff and low-payoff signals from peers, and they do not appear to chase the highest earners or protect co-punishers. Overall, they behave like a stable, moderately principled cooperator who initially tries to enforce basic fairness but, observing the broader pattern of chaotic punishment and reward, retreats to a simpler strategy: contribute at a constant moderate level, and ignore the sanctioning game.

The player clearly pays attention to others’ contributions, payoffs, and especially punishment/reward patterns. Many of their punishments are aimed at players who have been central figures in the sanctioning structure—heavy punishers or those who strongly affect others’ payoffs—rather than at clear free-riders. This implies that they respond not just to contribution levels but to the broader pattern of social control in the group. They frequently punish players who are receiving large payoffs or who seem to be doing relatively well, even when those players are contributing at or above average. This could reflect envy, dislike of perceived “dominance,” or a desire to knock down those perceived as advantaged, rather than a simple fairness norm about contributions. They also sometimes target players who are using reward or punishment intensively themselves, which could be interpreted as reaction against perceived moralizing, second-order free-riding concerns, or straightforward retaliation; distinguishing between these is uncertain. Overall, their behavior indicates that they track not just who contributes what, but who exerts control via punishment and reward and who gains from the interaction. Their adjustments, however, are mostly negative (punishment) rather than positive (reward), and they do not stabilize around a cooperative equilibrium. Their type is best described as a volatile, opportunistic contributor with a strong inclination toward costly, often non-standard punishment, weak responsiveness to sanctions, and minimal use of positive reciprocity.

The player observes detailed information about others’ contributions, punishments, rewards, and payoffs, including very aggressive sanctioning patterns and large payoff disparities, yet shows no behavioral response. They do not appear to track or react to who is consistently high- or low-contributing, who is spending heavily on punishment, or who is gaining large payoffs through free-riding or sanctioning. They also do not adjust to shifts in group-level cooperation, neither trying to “prop up” failing cooperation with additional targeted incentives, nor cutting back when exploitation becomes intense. This lack of responsiveness suggests that they give little strategic weight to social feedback and outcome information; they may process it but do not let it influence their fixed rule of full contribution and zero sanctioning. Their personality presentation is that of a steadfast, non-confrontational cooperator who accepts being exploited and sanctioned without attempting to regulate group behavior or protect themselves, prioritizing consistency over adaptation.

They observe extensive punishment and reward activity by others, including large expenditures and severe deductions, but do not imitate this behavior. They do not adjust by joining punishment “coalitions” against common targets, nor do they move to defend frequently punished cooperators with counter-punishment or substantial reward. Their reaction to seeing others sacrifice a lot on sanctions is essentially to ignore that strategic dimension and operate almost entirely through contribution choices. They appear sensitive to the overall contribution and payoff environment: when others as a group contribute more, they are willing to raise their own contributions; when the environment is fractured, with many players either contributing very little or spending heavily on sanctions, they often choose lower contributions and keep sanction spending at zero. They seem to read the presence of heavy sanctioning and chaotic outcomes as a riskier climate where contributing a lot is less attractive, but they still occasionally choose to cooperate at a high level despite this. Taken together, this player’s type is: an opportunistic conditional cooperator who largely opts out of the punishment–reward meta-game. They care about earning good payoffs, are willing to cooperate when group cooperation is visible and not too costly, and accept being punished or seeing others punished without engaging in norm-enforcement themselves. They neither build alliances through sanctions nor retaliate; instead, they adapt their contributions to the perceived cooperation climate and tolerate a substantial amount of external noise and hostility without directly confronting it.

They regularly observe extreme outcomes among others, including players who earn very high payoffs through strong cooperation and heavy use of reward, and players whose payoffs are drastically reduced by intense punishment wars. Despite seeing this, they maintain a fairly stable behavioral baseline: low-to-mid contributions, selective punishment of under-contributors, and no rewards. They do not copy the heavy punishers, nor do they copy those who build reputations via large rewards. They seem sensitive to the overall contribution pattern: when the group environment is more cooperative, they are more willing to contribute higher amounts themselves. However, they do not emulate the highest cooperators; they stop below that level, suggesting they treat the cooperative “surplus” as an opportunity for moderate free-riding. Conversely, when many others fall to low contributions or chaotic punishment/reward patterns, they tend not to “step up” to sustain efficiency; they maintain their own safe, modest participation and let group efficiency fall. Their reaction to others’ heavy use of punishment and reward is largely observational rather than imitative. They see that punishment is widely used and sometimes extremely costly, yet they choose only rare, focused punishment acts and avoid large-scale sanctioning. They also see some players paying substantial amounts to reward others and receiving gratitude or alliances implicitly, yet they never join that dynamic. This hints at a player who updates mildly on the environment but keeps a personal cost–benefit rule: spend only on punishment when a threshold of under-contribution is crossed, otherwise preserve coins. Overall, this is a “cautious, negative norm-enforcing opportunist”: a player who reliably participates but under-contributes relative to strong cooperators; who occasionally pays to sanction clear shirkers; who does not retaliate or reward; and who adapts modestly to the cooperation level around them without fully sacrificing personal payoff for collective welfare.

This player appears largely indifferent to others’ payoffs, punishment costs, and deductions. They observe repeated and sometimes heavy use of punishment among other players, including severe sanctions and costly attacks that dramatically alter others’ round outcomes, yet they never join in or adjust their own sanctioning behavior. Their contribution choices do not reliably react to seeing others lose heavily or earn markedly more or less than themselves; they continue cycling between zero and higher contributions without clear sensitivity to who is benefiting or suffering. This suggests they do not strongly internalize social learning from observed outcomes, nor do they try to align with the most successful apparent strategy of any peer. They also show no tendency to “reward” high contributors via greater own contributions or to “exit” cooperation when seeing generous players get punished; instead, their own pattern stays primarily driven by private payoff considerations and occasional non-systematic cooperation. Emotionally, they appear detached from others’ fortunes: they neither rush to support victims of heavy punishment nor to discipline aggressors. Overall, their response to others’ outcomes is muted and self-focused: they note the environment but do not let others’ payoffs or sanctioning behavior substantially redirect their own strategic path.

They frequently observe cases where others contribute very little and earn much higher payoffs than they do, yet they do not adjust their own behavior to reduce this disparity. This indicates that, despite exposure to clear outcome inequality, they do not visibly prioritize equalizing payoffs or protecting themselves from exploitation via contribution changes or punishment. They also observe episodes where others spend heavily on punishment, often in ways that look non-cooperative or hostile, and again make no move to participate in that dynamic. They do not join in group-sanctioning cascades, do not reinforce others’ punishment choices, and do not react by shifting their own contributions up or down in response to the severity of sanctions others receive. They seem largely behaviorally indifferent to whether others are being punished, over-punished, or free-riding on their cooperation. If they infer that punishment is being used in antisocial or erratic ways, they nonetheless refrain from intervening or adapting. This suggests a personality style that is non-confrontational and internally anchored: they continue to “do their part” regardless of turmoil in others’ payoffs. Whether they privately judge others as unfair, confused, or malicious is unknown; there is no behavioral sign of moralistic escalation or exit. Overall, they function as a steadfast, principle-driven cooperator who focuses on their own contribution rule and largely ignores both the incentive and informational content of others’ outcomes.

They never explicitly comment on others’ payoffs, reward spending, or relative earnings, and there is no direct evidence that they track those details closely. However, their persistent free riding alongside others’ costly cooperation implies at least a coarse understanding that others are bearing more costs. They make light of others’ efforts (“they want to make more money”) rather than sympathizing with or praising contributors, indicating low emotional salience of others’ losses from supporting the public good. When players complain about cheapness or demand that they “pay,” the focal player responds with jokes instead of adjustments, showing limited responsiveness to social pressure. They do not appear to punish or reward in response to others’ reward expenditures; heavy use of reward by others does not induce them to either match that investment or reduce exploitation. Overall, they recognize outcomes and norms but treat them as background to exploit, not as cues to change strategy.

They pay attention to others’ contributions and payoffs and use chat to comment on how others behave, rather than directly adjusting via reward. They see who repeatedly contributes nothing or pays substantial reward costs and react socially: with jokes, mild criticism, or dramatic references. When others incur large reward costs, they do not follow suit but understand those choices well enough to incorporate them into the group’s narrative, for example by teasing “angry” or extreme behaviors. They appear sensitive to asymmetries in benefit: they remark when they feel neglected or not compensated in line with their cooperation level, which indicates that they monitor relative outcomes, not solely their own absolute payoff. Their later free-riding episodes come after long periods where others either free-ride or spend reward on others, and where they themselves are not proportionally acknowledged. This suggests they internalize others’ patterns as a justification license: once they see significant strategic or exploitative behavior from peers, they feel freer to do the same without guilt. Emotionally, their reaction to others’ outcomes is not explosive; it is framed in humorous or sardonic language, but the underlying message is clear: they care about reciprocity and fairness and will adjust their own economic behavior when those norms are not met. In a new game, they are likely to watch others’ payoffs and contribution patterns closely, tolerate some imbalance, voice displeasure verbally, and only later, if imbalance persists, shift toward opportunistic choices rather than immediate retaliation.

The player clearly attends to others’ outcomes and uses that information to shape both their talk and their strategy. They notice when someone contributes nothing while still benefiting, and they call this out, especially when there is a mismatch between cooperative rhetoric and actual free riding. They also react to who is spending heavily on rewards; they show awareness that one player is consistently paying substantial costs through the reward mechanism and jokingly comment about trust and that player’s role, but they do not visibly adjust to reciprocate that reward spending with their own rewards. They are sensitive to being free-ridden upon and show some mild frustration or irony when others profit at their expense, using humor and quoted lines rather than direct anger. At the same time, they are willing to join profitable free-riding patterns once those become common and socially acknowledged, indicating that their reactions to others’ outcomes include both moral and opportunistic components. They do not appear to systematically punish high earners or reward low earners; instead, they monitor the structure of who is paying and who is gaining and adjust their own contributions and comments to keep themselves from being the persistent sucker, while maintaining an amiable social front.

They appear indifferent in their behavior to others’ payoffs, reward spending, and reward receipts. Large, repeated reward expenditures by another player that benefit others more than them do not elicit any detectable changes in their own strategy: they neither join in rewarding, adjust contributions, nor comment. They also do not visibly react to who is being rewarded or to who profits most. The only explicit social feedback they provide is an early admonition against free riding, before a long run of stable non‑rewarding, full cooperation. After that, even as others joke about free riding, rewards, and interpersonal dynamics, their action pattern stays unchanged and they remain silent. This suggests they encode others’ behavior mainly as background noise relative to a fixed personal rule: continue contributing fully, ignore payoff fluctuations and incentive patterns, and do not engage in targeted responses to individual outcomes. Their learning or adaptation to the observed outcome structure is minimal or absent; they behave as if committed to a norm, not as a responsive strategist.

This player observes others’ outcomes but reacts in a muted and non-retaliatory way. They repeatedly benefit from substantial reward given by others, including when they themselves are not reliably cooperative, yet they do not escalate their own reward spending in response. This indicates that gratitude signaling via reward is weak or absent: they accept windfall gains but do not strongly adjust their behavior to “pay back” or amplify cooperative partners through the reward system. They also do not appear to punish indirectly (for example, by withholding cooperation entirely from those who spend heavily or defect heavily); their shifts between contribution and free-riding do not systematically track any particular peer’s payoff, spending, or grant patterns. They tolerate large disparities where some players incur heavy costs and end with low payoffs while others, including themselves, do better, without visibly compensating for these imbalances. This suggests a relatively low sensitivity to others’ welfare outcomes, or at least a preference not to spend personal resources to correct them. Their adaptation over time shows some responsiveness to the general environment—cooperation and reward levels falling or changing—but no tight, model-based linkage to any single player’s outcome or to group-level sacrifice. In sum, they respond to the incentive structure more than to others’ specific fortunes: they notice but largely do not internalize others’ costs and payoffs in their own decisions.

The player is tolerant of unequal outcomes and does not react by withdrawing cooperation. When others earn much higher payoffs—often because they contribute less or spend less on reward—the player does not move toward self-protection or defection. Instead, they persist in both full contribution and costly rewarding, even as their own relative position in the payoff distribution becomes weak. They thus appear relatively insensitive to disadvantageous inequality; they do not attempt to correct it via reduced cooperation or reduced reward. They seem to interpret others’ actions and payoffs through a cooperative lens: observing that some peers incur costs to reward, and that some continue to contribute, does not lead them to discriminate sharply between “good” and “bad” partners in a way that would preserve their own earnings. Rather, they behave as if the presence of any cooperative or rewarding behavior justifies continuing to support the group at large. Because punishers and rewarders are anonymous, they cannot know exactly who is responsible for particular outcomes, yet they still invest in the reward channel, suggesting that their primary concern is maintaining or signaling a prosocial climate, not targeting individuals based on detailed outcome histories. Their learning and adaptation style is non-retaliatory and non-cynical: as the environment becomes less cooperative and more unequal, they do not shift toward self-interest. If anything, they increase their costly prosocial engagement. This indicates a “principled cooperator” or “prosocial idealist” type, who prioritizes adherence to cooperative norms and mutual support over personal payoff, and who maintains that stance even when the social environment is mixed or exploitative.

The player is highly sensitive to the overall social environment as reflected in others’ contributions and reward costs, but they respond through rewards rather than through changing their own contribution. When most others are contributing and several peers are spending heavily on rewards, they continue to free-ride yet gradually increase their own reward spending, suggesting they notice others’ generosity and choose to participate in that second-order norm without matching first-order cooperation. When the group’s contribution level falls and payoffs decline for many players, they still do not contribute, but they become more willing to sacrifice their own payoff through higher reward expenditure, indicating that poor group outcomes do not shift their contribution rule but can shift their redistribution behavior. They show tolerance for others who free-ride or who invest in rewards in ways that hurt their own payoff; there is no sign of punitive or exclusionary response, only positive reinforcement. They also appear unconcerned with relative earnings: they accept both being one of the top earners when they spend little on rewards and being less advantaged when they later spend more, as long as they preserve their zero-contribution rule. Overall, they behave as a calculated, self-interested free-rider who is nevertheless willing to invest in others via rewards once they observe widespread prosociality, using rewards as their primary lever to respond to others’ behaviors and outcomes.

The player appears responsive to the general pattern of others’ outcomes but not in a way that restores their own contributions. After observing that noncontributors can still receive rewards and earn high payoffs, they maintain free-riding. The fact that they continue to reward even when others’ payoffs and contributions fluctuate suggests they are not discouraged by observing inequality or by seeing some players exploit the system; instead, they keep engaging in reward allocation. They do not react by punishing (not available) or by withholding reward entirely when others earn high payoffs; instead, they remain willing to spend to alter others’ outcomes. This implies they may be using rewards to shape the distribution of payoffs—possibly favoring some players over others—rather than simply mirroring whoever currently earns the most. Their adaptation pattern is asymmetric: they adjust their own contributions downward in response to the environment but keep their reward activity relatively high, indicating a belief that personal contribution is dispensable while influencing others’ payoffs through reward remains valuable.
