The precise mapping from others’ costs and gains to the player’s reward decisions is unknown, but overall they are best characterized as a noncontributing yet engagement-seeking participant who reacts to observed outcomes mainly through ongoing, selective rewarding rather than through changes in their own provision to the public good.

This player repeatedly receives positive net rewards while never paying to reward others, and they do not adjust their reward behavior in response; they remain non-rewarding throughout. Their contribution pattern shows some responsiveness to the group’s overall cooperation level: when group contributions fall substantially, they join in free-riding once, but they later resume full contribution even though others still vary, implying they are not simply copying the majority. There is no evidence they condition on who specifically rewards or spends in the mechanism, likely because individual rewarders and recipients are anonymous; instead they appear to respond only to the aggregate environment (overall contribution level and payoff landscape). They do not appear to punish low contributors indirectly via withholding cooperation in a consistent, targeted way, nor do they systematically “reward” high contributors via higher own contributions. Their reactions to others’ payoff outcomes and mechanism spending thus remain limited and somewhat opaque: they adjust their own contribution at least once in response to a less cooperative group, but they do not engage in active reward-based norm enforcement. Any deeper beliefs about fairness, gratitude, or reciprocity beyond this pattern are unknown.

This player appears highly sensitive to the idea that others should not be left entirely unsupported, even when some are free-riding or when their own payoff is low. They keep contributing and rewarding despite observing others who earn high payoffs while spending little or nothing on rewards, and they do not visibly shift into self-protective behavior in response. There is no clear evidence of retaliatory withdrawal or of conditioning their actions on whether others are “fair” in terms of contribution-plus-reward costs. They seem relatively indifferent to distributional inequality when choosing their own actions: they accept doing worse than many others as a consequence of continued cooperation and reward-giving. At the same time, their persistent reward use indicates they want at least some others’ payoffs to be higher than they would otherwise be. They thus behave more like an inequality-tolerant altruist than someone seeking strict fairness or payoff matching. Because individual reward targets are hidden, it is unknown whether they focus support on high contributors, low contributors, or those who previously rewarded them; their internal targeting rule is therefore uncertain (low confidence). What can be inferred is that seeing others spend on reward does not discourage their own rewarding, and seeing others free-ride does not push them into defection. Overall, they respond to the outcome environment by doubling down on cooperation and costly prosocial reinforcement, rather than adapting toward self-protection or selective reciprocity.

This player observes substantial heterogeneity in others’ contributions and reward expenditures over time. While they initially keep contributing despite others obtaining high payoffs from low contributions and from heavy use of rewards, they eventually adjust by stopping their own contribution, coinciding with a more fragmented contribution pattern around them. This shift suggests that they update on others’ revealed priorities—specifically, that many peers are willing to spend heavily on reward or to free-ride—and infer that maintaining full cooperation is no longer worthwhile. They do not use reward behavior (who spends or receives a lot) as a cue for targeted responses; they neither imitate high rewarders nor counteract perceived exploiters via selective rewarding. Instead, they respond at a coarse level: when group behavior looks less collectively oriented, they switch to a more self-regarding strategy. Their emotional reactions (e.g., resentment, approval) are unknown, but behaviorally they move from “socially hopeful” to “defensively self-interested” while continuing to accept any positive externalities others generate.

This player’s behavior implies that they pay attention to group outcomes but respond only through their own contribution level, not through reward usage. They observe that many others are willing to spend substantial amounts on rewards, including in ways that allow some individuals to earn high payoffs while contributing little or nothing. Initially, they continue to fully contribute despite these imbalances, indicating some tolerance for inequality and for free-riding by others. Over time, as overall contributions drop and payoff patterns increasingly reward non-contributors through the reward channel, they adapt by abandoning full cooperation and becoming a non-contributor themselves. This shift suggests they update their beliefs toward expecting low or unstable cooperation and adjust to protect their own earnings. They do not visibly retaliate against low contributors (no punishment option exists) and do not positively reinforce high contributors. Their response to others’ spending heavily on rewards—including cases where costly generosity leaves some players with low net payoffs—appears to be observational rather than participatory: they do not imitate that generosity, and they do not “join” any emergent reward coalition. In sum, they are sensitive to the evolving structure of payoffs and contributions, but they respond privately and defensively rather than through active social sanctioning or support.

They pay attention to others’ contributions and final outcomes but react more to contribution patterns than to who is currently earning the most. High earners who also contribute and sometimes reward are treated as legitimate beneficiaries of the system; there is no sign of envy-motivated withdrawal of cooperation. Free-riders and low contributors are not targeted with countermeasures (since punishment is unavailable), but the player does not visibly redirect significant reward toward them either, signaling disapproval by omission rather than active sanction. When some players spend extremely large amounts on rewards and thereby lower their own payoffs, the focal player does not exploit this by cutting back their own effort; instead, they continue to cooperate and reward, suggesting that they view such generosity as something to be supported, not taken advantage of. Shifts in group behavior—periods of high universal contribution versus more uneven participation—are met with relatively stable high contribution and sustained, though slightly adjusted, reward. This indicates a patient, stabilizing role orientation: they aim to keep the group in a cooperative, high-payoff regime and are willing to absorb payoff disparities to do so.

The player observes substantial variation in others’ contributions, rewards given, and resulting payoffs, but their own behavior remains remarkably unchanged. They do not increase or decrease contributions in response to free‑riding, to extreme generosity, or to others’ high or low earnings. They also do not react via the reward channel: they ignore both very generous and very stingy reward behavior. High‑spending rewarders, low contributors, and consistently rewarded peers all receive the same response: continued high contributions from this player and zero reward. This insensitivity to others’ outcomes suggests that they are either (a) focused primarily on their own simple contribution rule regardless of the social landscape, or (b) following a principle like “always cooperate, ignore side‑payments.” They tolerate payoff inequalities and heavy second‑order prosocial investments by others without adjustment, indicating low reactivity and low concern with enforcing fairness through available mechanisms.

They monitor others’ contributions and implicitly, the associated payoffs, and use that information to shape both behavior and commentary. When certain players contribute less, they join in labeling those players as greedy or problematic, even though they themselves also under-contribute in some rounds. This indicates they are sensitive to fairness violations by others and willing to use social disapproval as a soft enforcement tool, but they apply that standard asymmetrically. They do not have access to formal punishment or reward tools here, so their “response cost” is in words only. There is no evidence that they would spend their own payoff to punish or reward, as that mechanism is absent. Instead, they rely on sarcasm and jokes to stigmatize defectors and to signal alignment with the cooperative majority, which likely reduces the reputational risk of their own mild free-riding. They also show concern about the overall payoff level and duration of the game. They comment on expected payment magnitude and hourly earnings, and they reassure another participant that they can contact the platform if duration is excessive. This suggests they care about total income and about the process being “fair” or at least properly compensated. Their willingness to keep contributing fully once the group norm is strong, even after seeing others deviate and earn more, indicates they are not purely maximizing immediate private payoff; they appear to balance income with maintaining a functioning cooperative environment that continues generating high returns. Belief-wise, they seem to assume that the group will generally keep cooperating as long as no one pushes greed too far, and that mild defections (their own or others’) are acceptable as long as they do not become widespread. Their learning appears incremental: after a few early deviations (by themselves and others) and the group’s negative reaction, they mostly settle into high contributions with smaller, less conspicuous tests. Overall, they respond to others’ outcomes and behaviors by publicly endorsing cooperation and fairness, privately exploiting opportunities at the margin, and aiming to keep the group equilibrium intact.

They attend to others’ contribution behavior and implicitly to payoff differences but respond in a non‑retaliatory, light‑touch way. When some players contribute less and earn more in those rounds, this player remarks that “someone always wants just that little bit extra,” acknowledging greed as a predictable pattern rather than a personal affront. They do not lower their own contributions in response, nor do they try to organize a punitive response; their cooperation is robust to observed defection. When another known free‑rider defects again, they jokingly label them “being bad again,” but they keep full contributions and maintain a friendly tone. They also express mild hope that earlier cooperation will keep “the ball rolling,” indicating they see group outcomes as path‑dependent but still expect that sustained cooperation is possible despite occasional defection. Their focus is more on the overall profitability (“easy money,” approximate earnings calculations, mentioning UK conversion) and on the social experience than on equalizing or retaliating against individual payoffs. In the absence of explicit punishment tools, they default to norm‑reinforcement through soft comments and humor rather than any form of costly or strategic sanctioning.

They observe repeated instances of others reducing contributions and gaining higher relative payoffs in those periods, yet they make no behavioral adjustment: their contribution remains maximal and unchanged. They do not mirror defection, retaliate, or shift to more self-protective play when exploitation is evident. Nor do they visibly reward high contributors, for example by over-contributing relative to a cooperative norm or varying their behavior to signal approval. Since punishment and reward mechanics are unavailable, their “response” is purely in their own contribution choice. That choice is effectively invariant to others’ outcomes: - Opportunistic play by specific avatars (getting strictly higher payoffs by contributing less) does not trigger a reduction in their own contributions. - Temporary drops in average group contributions and corresponding lower payoffs do not alter their strategy. - Restoration of full cooperation by previously greedy players also does not elicit any change; they do not “forgive” or “reward” because they never deviated to begin with. This pattern is consistent with an outcome-insensitive cooperator type: they uphold the cooperative norm regardless of whether they are currently benefiting more or less than potential unilateral defection would allow. Their emotional reactions to others’ outcomes are unknown; what is observable is a steady, non-reactive, norm-following contribution behavior.

They monitor others’ contributions and implicitly, their payoffs, and they clearly notice who is gaining from under‑contribution. Their reactions, however, are purely verbal and playful. They publicly mark low contributors as “not playing ball” or “choosing violence,” and later joke that such players will be excluded from a hypothetical future “reunion,” but there is no sign of actual sanctioning behavior (and no punishment mechanism exists here). They seem more interested in norm reinforcement and group identity than in retribution. Defectors are framed as “birds” or “red bird,” treated as amusing antagonists rather than serious enemies. When previously greedy players switch to full contribution, the player responds positively—welcoming them to the “cool kid team” and expressing happiness that “not all birds are bad” and that former defectors have “seen the light.” This indicates a forgiving orientation: they recognize exploitation but are quick to re‑include former defectors into the cooperative in‑group once behavior changes. Crucially, their own contributions are not adjusted in response to others’ outcomes. Even when free‑riders visibly earn more in specific rounds, they neither retaliate nor attempt to match that strategy. Their behavioral response is to stay at full contribution and address deviations via light social commentary only. They also express some empathy with the group’s economic motives (acknowledging the money is meaningful to some participants) while still maintaining their cooperative stance. In sum, they are observant of others’ outcomes and fairness violations but respond with humor, social labeling, and warm re‑acceptance rather than with behavioral defection or demands for reciprocity.

The player consistently observes outcomes but makes no explicit comment in chat about others’ contributions or payoffs. Behaviorally, they respond to free-riding and intermittent greed by continuing to contribute the maximum, even in periods where a few others systematically give less and thereby earn more. They never mirror the selfish behavior, never punish by reducing their own contributions, and never try to “discipline” deviants through their actions. They do not visibly reward or verbally praise high contributors, nor do they shame or criticize low contributors. They also do not follow the informal social sanctions that others apply via joking or calling out “greedy” players. Instead, their contribution pattern is insensitive—at least in observable terms—to who is currently benefiting more or less. Collectively, this suggests a type who maintains cooperative behavior regardless of inequity, and who does not translate frustration (if any exists) into either strategic adjustment or social commentary. Their internal beliefs about others’ motives (e.g., “bots,” “greedy,” “mistakes”) are unknown, as they never articulate them. What is clear is that they act as a stable, silent cooperator who supports group efficiency without engaging in social enforcement or reciprocity dynamics.

This player is attentive to others’ contributions and the pattern of payoffs and reacts in a socially oriented, norm‑enforcing but non‑punitive way. When others under‑contribute and earn higher individual payoffs, they openly label that behavior as greedy but keep the tone light and collective, joining group jokes about those avatars rather than spearheading any campaign. They also express approval and even enthusiasm when previously greedy players switch to full cooperation, reinforcing the idea that “seeing the light” is praiseworthy and that defectors can be welcomed back into the group. They do not reciprocate greed: despite repeated observation of others taking advantage, they do not systematically reduce their own contributions or try to exploit the group in turn. Their single partial contribution does not appear tied to a specific retaliatory response and is not accompanied by complaint or justification referencing others’ outcomes. They show sensitivity to fairness and to who is “on the team,” but they resolve inequity through humor, social comparison, and encouragement rather than through strategic withdrawal or aggression. Their reactions suggest they believe that maintaining a cooperative, friendly climate yields enough payoff—and they are willing to absorb some disadvantage as long as the group as a whole remains functional and sociable.

The player observes repeated deviations by a few others and the resulting drops in everyone’s payoffs, yet does not change their own contributions or shift into criticism. Their only explicit reaction to the “dirty birds” narrative is to mildly reframe their own avatar as cooperative, rather than attacking the low contributors. They neither threaten nor shame defectors, and they never suggest retaliation or reduced contributions. When group earnings are high, they express no special celebration; when earnings fall due to free-riding, they show no direct reproach, only a pragmatic comment about hoping the real-world payment justifies the time. They also show solidarity with others who mention financial need, indicating concern for shared outcomes rather than resentment toward those who occasionally grab extra coins. In sum, they are tolerant of exploitation, emotionally even-keeled about payoff fluctuations, and socially focused on affirming cooperation and shared benefit rather than on enforcing fairness through behavioral change.

They closely monitor others’ contribution patterns and implicitly, therefore, their payoffs. When some players contribute less or zero, they notice immediately, name the behavior, and classify it as greed or “sneaky,” but they do not retaliate by defecting themselves. Instead, they use social observation and mild public commentary as their only “sanction,” relying on group pressure rather than changing their own actions. They differentiate between accidental and deliberate low contributions, arguing that defaults make under‑contribution intentional, which shows a belief that responsibility and blame matter. When previously low contributors switch to full cooperation, they openly acknowledge and welcome the change, offering positive social recognition and signaling that re‑joining the cooperative norm restores acceptance. Overall, they react to others’ outcomes with norm‑enforcing talk (calling out deviance, celebrating compliance) while maintaining stable high contributions and a friendly group orientation. Punishment and reward mechanisms are absent, but if they were available, this pattern suggests they would likely favor low‑cost social or formal sanctions for clear free‑riding while continuing to support and praise consistent cooperators.

They clearly monitor others’ contributions and infer the associated payoff differences, as shown by their targeted remarks about particular under-contributors and recognition of “getting away with it.” They notice patterns (e.g., a player who repeatedly gives slightly less or much less) and label these behaviors as greedy or sneaky, but their reactions remain light and teasing instead of angry or punitive. Despite observing that defectors earn more in specific rounds, they do not alter their own contributions in response, indicating that observing unequal outcomes does not trigger behavioral retaliation. Their comments suggest a detached amusement at minor exploitation rather than emotional distress. They seem more interested in the social narrative (who is “greedy,” who might be a “plant” or bot) and the meta-game (how the platform or researchers are “testing” them) than in redressing payoff imbalances. They also appear comfortable with the idea that the experimenters may be probing reactions to greed or attention. Their reference to possible “plants” and to a twist suggests they interpret unusual or selfish behavior partly as experimental stimuli, which may reduce their inclination to respond with anger or strategic defection. In summary, this player is a steadfast, norm-following full cooperator with a humorous, socially attuned style, who observes and comments on others’ outcomes and norm violations but does not let those observations change their own contribution strategy.

This player is observant of others’ outcomes and behavior but responds in a non‑punitive, reframing way. When others under‑contribute and thereby earn more in that round, the focal player does not retaliate by lowering their own contribution and does not push for exclusion or hostility. Instead, they emphasize how good the payoffs already are when cooperation holds and suggest that defectors are “seeing the light” once they switch back to higher contributions. Their comments show that they notice who has been “greedy” and that they understand the distribution of benefits, but they choose to interpret subsequent cooperation as redemption rather than dwell on past exploitation. They also keep track conceptually of how profitable the game is overall (comparing approximate earnings to dollars and to alternative uses of time), and they use this understanding to maintain a positive attitude toward continued play even as others worry about duration or boredom. When someone expresses concern about the task length, the player responds reassuringly and factually rather than with frustration. Overall, their reaction to others’ outcomes is to stabilize cooperation through positive framing and information sharing, not through sanctioning. They show no sign of conditional cooperation or payoff‑based grudges; instead they act as a robust, optimistic cooperator who expects or encourages others to converge toward the high‑contribution norm and celebrates when that happens.

The player observes that several peers contribute very generously and that some of these peers repeatedly spend their own payoff to reward others. Despite this, the player neither increases their own contribution nor begins to reward, implying limited emotional or normative response to others’ prosocial costs. They do not appear to imitate the highly cooperative or rewarding types, nor to compensate them for their sacrifices. Their behavior is compatible with viewing others’ generosity as an exploitable resource rather than as a cue to reciprocate. They show no discernible concern for the distribution of payoffs among players, focusing instead on maintaining or improving their own earnings while others bear the cost of sustaining cooperation. Overall, they adapt in a self-serving direction rather than aligning with the emerging cooperative norm.

They observe that several peers contribute much more than they do and that some participants spend a substantial amount of their own payoff on rewarding others, including cases where individuals sacrifice notable amounts without gaining direct monetary return. Despite this, the focal player does not adjust toward stronger cooperation or begin rewarding; their main reaction is behavioral inertia with only a small upward change in contribution and continued zero reward spending. This indicates that seeing others incur real costs for the group does not strongly move them toward more prosocial or reciprocal behavior, at least over the short horizon. They do not appear to retaliate against or specifically respond to low contributors, nor do they try to compensate or support high contributors through rewards. Others’ final payoffs and visible reward patterns do not meaningfully alter their strategic stance. Emotional reactions such as guilt, admiration, or resentment are unknown, but their choices suggest a type who acknowledges others’ generosity behaviorally only minimally, if at all, and who keeps their own decisions largely anchored to a self-focused payoff orientation rather than to observed fairness or reciprocity norms.

This player observes others’ varied contributions and reward expenditures yet remains behaviorally steady in cooperation and ultimately withdraws from further rewarding. They tolerate the presence of low and moderate contributors without retaliating, withdrawing cooperation, or trying to “correct” outcomes via costly actions. Highly costly reward behavior by at least one other player does not provoke mimicry; they do not escalate their own reward usage in response. They also do not appear to chase payoff equality: they accept that some players earn more or less than others without adjusting their own contribution strategy. Their pattern is compatible with someone who notes others’ outcomes but is not strongly payoff-comparative or envy-driven, and who adjusts only their auxiliary behavior (reward) rather than their core cooperative stance. Any emotional reactions are unknown, but in terms of choices they exhibit calm, rule-like cooperation with limited, selectively applied generosity.

The player’s contributions remain maximal despite visible heterogeneity in others’ contributions and reward expenditures, indicating that they are not retaliatory or discouraged by free‑riding, nor are they escalatory in response to generous peers. They do not appear to react behaviorally to seeing some players bear extra costs to reward others, or to the payoff advantages enjoyed by lower contributors. Their choice not to use rewards, despite seeing others spend on them and on third parties, suggests a limited inclination to reciprocate material kindness or to “correct” the payoff distribution via rewards. They tolerate both payoff inequality and asymmetric responsibility for rewarding without changing their own strategy. This points to a personality style that is steady, non-confrontational, and norm-driven in contribution, but relatively passive toward redistributive or reciprocal actions. Underlying beliefs about whether others are fair, strategic, or exploitive are unknown, as is whether the player experiences gratitude or resentment internally; only the absence of behavioral adjustment is observable.

The player is highly responsive to others’ aggregate behavior and outcomes, but this responsiveness is asymmetric. They do not imitate others’ use of costly rewards, nor do they reciprocate toward those who incur costs to support the group. Instead, they respond to the clear pattern of high group contributions by moving their own behavior toward the cooperative norm while still preserving some self-benefit advantage. This suggests that they view others’ outcomes primarily as information about the stability and strength of cooperative norms rather than as cues for direct reciprocity. They show no observable tendency to target or differentiate among specific individuals based on who sacrifices more or spends on rewards; their behavior remains anonymous and aggregate-oriented. They also do not attempt to exploit those who visibly sacrifice more by further reducing their own cooperation; rather, they partially align with the group once it is obvious that the group is robustly cooperative. Sensitivity to others’ precise payoff differences, emotional reactions to inequality, and attitudes toward extreme cooperators or non-rewarders remain unknown.

They observe that some peers invest substantially in rewards while others do not, and that contributors and non-contributors alike receive various payoffs, but they do not adjust any observable behavior in response. The mix of generous and less generous peers, and the fact that some players repeatedly spend significant amounts on rewards, elicits no visible change in their own contribution or reward pattern. They treat incoming rewards passively and do not mirror or reciprocate them. This suggests that others’ costs, rewards, and resulting payoffs do not meaningfully enter into their decision rule. They appear insensitive, at least behaviorally, to social signals conveyed by the outcome structure (such as who is “kind” or “exploitative”). Their stance is: continue maximal contribution regardless of who benefits more or who pays to support cooperation. Emotional reactions such as gratitude, annoyance at free-riders, or admiration for high spenders on rewards are not inferable and remain unknown.

The player observes substantial variation in others’ contributions and in how much others spend on rewards, but they show no overt behavioral backlash. They do not reduce their own contributions after encountering free-riders, nor do they visibly change their reward spending in response to others’ low or high payoffs. They do not comment on disparities in income, on others’ reward expenditures, or on remaining holdouts; they leave such commentary to others. This indicates low overt concern with distributive fairness in terms of payoffs, or at least low inclination to voice such concerns or to act punitively. Their behavior suggests a tolerant, non-confrontational stance: they continue to cooperate and sometimes reward even in the presence of persistent non-cooperation by some players. Their reactions to specific individual outcomes or identities are largely unknown beyond this general robustness of cooperation.

The player observes that many others contribute fully, some spend substantial amounts on rewards, and contributors sometimes receive extra income from those rewards, yet they do not comment on or visibly adjust to these patterns. They neither praise nor criticize high contributors or reward spenders. Their only reaction is a general, positive remark about how things are going, which does not distinguish between cooperators, non-cooperators, or heavy rewarders. This suggests either low sensitivity to the detailed payoff structure and others’ sacrifices, or a deliberate choice not to engage with social outcomes that might pressure them to change their own behavior. They appear unresponsive to both the material and social signals embodied in others’ contributions and reward expenditures. Overall, they act as a quiet, payoff-focused free-rider who acknowledges the social environment verbally but does not let others’ outcomes alter their own strategy.

They receive information about others’ contributions, payoffs, and reward expenditures but do not comment on or behaviorally react to this information in any obvious way. They continue to cooperate fully regardless of whether high contributors are rewarded or whether some players consistently free-ride and receive higher payoffs. They do not use chat to praise high contributors, criticize defectors, or discuss the fairness of outcomes, suggesting a non-expressive stance toward distributive outcomes. Their strategy appears robust to both positive and negative deviations by others: other players’ costs, gains, or lack of reciprocity do not visibly shift their contributions or reward use. Their behavioral response to others’ outcomes is essentially inertial: they maintain their own cooperative pattern without observable learning or adaptation based on the social outcome structure.

They observe others’ varied contributions and reward behaviors but do not comment on specific individuals’ payoffs, costs, or rewards. Their only group-facing reaction after seeing mixed cooperation is a generic encouraging remark to “keep going,” which focuses on sustaining overall contribution rather than evaluating particular players. They do not express resentment toward high earners who free-ride, nor explicit appreciation for those who spend on rewards. They also do not visibly adjust their own contribution in response to the distribution of payoffs or the presence of apparent altruistic rewarders. Overall, they appear robustly cooperative and non-reactive at the level of individual outcomes, attending to the group’s general cooperation level rather than to interpersonal accounting or fairness corrections.

The player observes others’ contributions, payoffs, and reward expenditures, yet does not adjust either contributions or reward behavior in any visible way. They continue to fully cooperate even when low contributors earn high payoffs and even when some cooperators spend heavily on rewarding. They do not appear to imitate free-riders, retaliate, or reciprocate reward-giving. Their response to high-cost rewarders is neutral; they neither join them in rewarding nor shift behavior away from them. This suggests a degree of behavioral rigidity or principle-based consistency: others’ outcomes, including apparent unfairness or generosity, do not translate into strategic changes. Emotional reactions such as resentment of free-riders or admiration of big rewarders are not expressed, so their internal evaluations of others’ outcomes remain unknown.

They observe that some players invest substantial amounts in rewarding others and that some players consistently free‑ride, yet they remain silent and do not adjust contributions in a clearly reciprocal or retaliatory way toward specific individuals. Their contribution shifts do not track obvious changes in who is being rewarded; instead, they respond more broadly to aggregate cooperation levels. They show no visible emotional or evaluative reaction in chat to others’ high costs or generous rewarding. They also do not express frustration or resentment toward profitable free‑riders. Overall, they treat others’ payoffs and reward expenditures as background information rather than as cues for targeted action, maintaining a detached stance and focusing on their own broad cooperation/defection pattern rather than on fine-grained social accounting.

The player can observe that several peers invest in rewards and that many others incur lower payoffs by contributing more or by paying to reward. Despite this, they maintain the same non-contributing, non-rewarding pattern, suggesting that seeing others’ costly prosocial acts does not prompt imitation, guilt, or strategic adjustment. When they themselves are rewarded, they do not change their subsequent behavior, implying that they are largely unresponsive to others’ attempts at positive reinforcement or inclusion. They appear to treat others’ lower earnings—whether caused by contributions or reward spending—as acceptable collateral that benefits them via the public fund, without any compensating action. Their response to observed outcomes is thus best characterized as passive exploitation: they integrate the benefits of others’ choices into their own payoff but do not modify their own strategy to support or reciprocate those behaviors.

They observe that some peers spend to reward others while others contribute nothing and still earn relatively high payoffs, yet they neither adjust their own contribution nor start rewarding. They remain behaviorally unchanged in the face of free-riding, of generous rewarders who sacrifice payoff, and of their own receipt of rewards. The fact that they benefit from rewards but do not reciprocate suggests they are willing to accept unilateral benefits without feeling compelled—within the game—to balance them through costly actions. There is no visible emotional or normative reaction through chat, so their internal evaluation of fairness is unknown. From actions alone, they appear to separate their own contribution norm (always cooperate) from any obligation to compensate or influence others based on observed payoffs or costs.

They observe others’ outcomes and comment only in a general way. Their concluding remark about lingering non‑contributors shows that they track the pattern of cooperation and free‑riding at the group level and are somewhat dissatisfied with the presence of defectors. However, they never directly reference who rewards whom, who bears cost to reward, or differences in payoffs. There is no evidence of explicit gratitude, resentment, or targeted reactions to individual players’ reward spending or gains. They do not adjust their own actions in response to visible free‑riding or to others’ costly rewarding behavior. Overall, they notice broad outcome patterns (some cooperate, some don’t) but behave as if these patterns do not alter their own obligations to contribute and do not warrant additional intervention through the reward mechanism.

The player appears sensitive to both others’ contributions and the resulting payoff distribution, but in a self-serving, payoff-oriented way. When others’ high contributions create very favorable payoffs for them, they are willing to continue contributing at a low-to-moderate level, keeping themselves on the profitable side of the interaction while not matching others’ efforts. They show no sign of discomfort at earning more than the heavy contributors; there is no compensatory increase in their giving after particularly beneficial free-riding opportunities. Conversely, when others contribute little and everyone’s redistributed payoffs shrink, the player often reduces their contribution or goes to zero, apparently unwilling to “carry” the group. They react to low group outcomes by protecting their private payoff rather than trying to restore cooperation. This indicates that their response to others’ outcomes is primarily instrumental: they adjust to maintain good personal earnings, not to equalize or improve group outcomes for their own sake. Because punishment and reward actions are unavailable in this game, there is no direct evidence of their willingness to spend resources to sanction or support others. However, from the pattern of their reactions to others’ outcomes, they likely prefer passive adaptation (changing their own contribution) over active, costly regulation of others’ behavior. Their social stance is observant and opportunistic rather than norm-enforcing or altruistically stabilizing.

The player is highly outcome-sensitive: their contribution choices track not only others’ contributions but also relative payoffs. When other low contributors secure higher or comparable payoffs to them, they tends to reduce their own contributions in subsequent play, indicating aversion to being exploited. At the same time, when they observes that higher total contributions can generate noticeably larger payoffs for everyone, they is willing to return to relatively generous contributions, even after periods where others have given little. This shows that they does not simply punish low contributors by permanently defecting; rather, they keeps searching for payoff-improving cooperative configurations. They appears especially sensitive to asymmetries where they contributes more than many peers yet earns less than them. Under such conditions, they shifts toward free-riding, which suggests a payoff-comparison mindset and a weak but operative inequity aversion: they dislikes being the “sucker” and alters behavior accordingly. Since explicit punishment and reward mechanisms are absent in the observed game, there is no direct evidence about their willingness to spend resources to discipline or reward others. Their indirect response—adjusting own contributions rather than attempting to influence others more actively—suggests a relatively passive, self-protective style: they manages exploitation risk by changing personal strategy rather than by trying to reshape the group’s incentives. Overall, the player reacts to others’ outcomes by toggling between cooperation and free-riding in a way that protects them from repeated relative losses while still leaving room for renewed cooperation if group behavior becomes more favorable.

The player is highly sensitive to the fact that high contributors often end up with lower payoffs than low contributors, and their behavior aligns with exploiting this structure. When others’ contributions generate large public-good returns, the player benefits significantly while contributing very little themselves, and they do not raise their own contributions in response. This indicates that they attend to payoff outcomes and choose to keep themselves in a favorable payoff position relative to cooperators. They do not show “altruistic” reactions to peers who sacrifice a lot: even when some group members give substantially more than average and get comparatively worse payoffs, the player does not move toward sharing the cost. There is no evidence of guilt-driven or fairness-restoring behavior; instead, they continue the same near-minimal pattern. Conversely, when others’ outcomes worsen because contributions fall, the player does not respond by increasing their own contribution to rescue group payoffs. Their reaction to poor group outcomes is to keep contributions low, not to compensate. This suggests that they treat others’ lower earnings as acceptable collateral to maintain their own self-interested strategy, rather than as a problem to fix. Because punishment and reward are unavailable in this game, we cannot observe whether they would actively sanction others; their stance on costly punishment/reward is unknown. However, given their consistent avoidance of personal cost for group benefit, it is plausible—but not certain—that they would be reluctant to spend on punishing defectors or rewarding cooperators in other environments (low confidence). In social-personality terms (within the game), they present as calculating, strategically self-interested, and largely unresponsive to others’ welfare beyond its instrumental role in generating surplus that they can free-ride on. They do not attempt to influence group norms through their own contributions; they adapt in a way that preserves their advantage rather than one that promotes mutual gain.

The player pays attention to the relationship between others’ contributions and their resulting payoffs and adapts in ways that favor their own earnings. When others contribute substantially more than they do and end up with noticeably lower or similar payoffs, the player subsequently leans into free-riding, maintaining very low or zero contributions while enjoying strong returns from the public pool. This indicates that seeing high contributors earn less than free-riders reinforces their belief that substantial cooperation is individually unprofitable. Conversely, when the group shows coordinated or above-average contributions and the public good becomes sizeable, the player is willing to occasionally “join in” with medium or high contributions; however, this response is fragile. If higher contributions do not clearly translate into superior individual outcomes compared with lower-contributing peers, they rapidly back off. Their experimental spikes in giving therefore appear driven more by payoff comparison and strategic curiosity than by an intrinsic desire to match norms or promote group welfare. Because no punishment or reward is available (and none is used), there is no observable sanctioning behavior. Still, their pattern implies a belief that others will not reliably enforce cooperative norms: they are comfortable repeatedly taking advantage of more generous players without any corrective expectation. Socially, this points to a pragmatic, low-trust orientation: they treat others’ generosity as a resource to exploit when it appears safe, rather than as a signal to build mutual cooperation upon. Their emotional reactions are unobservable in the transcript, but their choices are consistent with a cool, calculated style that prioritizes own payoff in response to observed outcome structures rather than a strong moral or fairness-driven response to others’ fortunes.

The player is clearly sensitive to others’ contribution behavior and the resulting payoff distribution, and they adapt across the game based on what they observe. When others’ contributions are high and create favorable group returns, the player is willing at times to contribute modestly or even substantially, but they also exploit these situations by cutting their own contributions to very low levels, capturing relatively strong payoffs while others bear most of the cost. This indicates a conditional cooperator with a significant free-riding component rather than a pure altruist. When the environment becomes more defect-oriented and average contributions drop, the player occasionally responds with higher-than-average contributions, suggesting some hope or belief that cooperation might re-emerge and that their own contribution could be part of that shift. However, if such efforts do not lead to a stable, high-contribution environment, they quickly revert to lower contributions and protect their own payoffs, indicating a limited willingness to be a “sucker” for long. They do not show strong reactive punishment or reward tendencies through their contributions alone; they neither reliably “reward” high contributors by matching their generosity nor systematically “punish” low contributors by collapsing their own contributions to zero as a norm-enforcement signal. Instead, their reactions are mixed: sometimes they free-ride on the generous, sometimes they modestly join them; sometimes they attempt to lift a low-contribution climate, sometimes they align with it. This pattern fits a learning, payoff-attentive, somewhat opportunistic conditional cooperator, whose adjustments are driven more by short-run incentive tracking than by a stable moral stance toward others’ outcomes.

This player is attentive to others’ contributions and outcomes and responds in a way that reflects a blend of norm sensitivity and self-interest. When a peer who contributes little is heavily punished by others, the player joins in with an additional sanction, aligning with the majority’s apparent judgment and reinforcing group norms against free-riding. At other times, when peers who contribute fully earn similar or even higher payoffs, the player does not attempt to equalize payoffs via punishment or additional rewards, indicating that they accept payoff inequalities that emerge from differential sanctioning or reward choices. They also do not react to others’ heavy spending on rewards or punishments by mimicking that intensity; instead, they keep their own sanctioning usage moderate and transient. As outcomes evolve and the group becomes more cooperative, they reduce their use of both punishment and reward, suggesting adaptive learning: once they infer that the group has converged on high cooperation, they no longer spend to influence others’ payoffs. Overall, they appear responsive to clear deviations from cooperative norms and to the broader pattern of group behavior, but not driven by envy or by a desire to micro-manage others’ final earnings.

They attend to (and act on) patterns linking others’ contributions and outcomes. When others free-ride and still earn strong payoffs, this player responds by imposing costs on those specific individuals, suggesting aversion to payoff-advantaged defectors. They tolerate others’ use of reward and punishment toward them without visible strategic adjustment: receiving both punishment and sizable rewards does not push them into using reward themselves or into retaliatory punishment. Instead, they focus on aligning low contributors’ outcomes downward rather than boosting high contributors further upward. They appear comfortable with inequality that favors cooperators and disfavors defectors, but they react negatively to cases where non-contributors or under-contributors capture large earnings. As the environment becomes more cooperative, they suspend further punishment, indicating that they are responsive to improved group behavior and willing to stop enforcing once norms are largely satisfied. Their adaptations across periods suggest that they update on observed contribution patterns and payoff distributions, using punishment chiefly as a tool to correct perceived unfair advantage rather than to pursue maximum personal earnings.

This player appears highly attentive to others’ outcomes and uses that information to adjust their own behavior over time. They face punishment when defecting yet continue to defect for at least one period afterward, suggesting they are testing the effectiveness and consistency of sanctions rather than immediately capitulating. After observing that cooperation in the group remains high and that others are willing to enforce norms through punishment, they eventually move back to full contribution, indicating that they are responsive to group-level patterns of behavior and enforcement. They observe both generous rewarders and active punishers among the group. Their own withdrawal from both punishment and reward over time suggests an adaptation: once they understand that others will carry the burden of enforcing norms and sustaining cooperation, they reduce their own enforcement and incentive spending. This hints at a “second-order free-riding” tendency regarding enforcement: they benefit from others’ punishment and reward efforts without contributing to them in later stages. When low contributors achieve high payoffs via rewards or lack of sanctions, this player does not visibly react with punitive correction or compensatory reward to cooperators. Thus, they appear relatively tolerant of payoff inequality and inconsistent enforcement, as long as their own payoff remains adequate. Their eventual return to contributing fully in a cooperative environment implies that they accept and accommodate existing norms once they see they are stable, even if those norms include some asymmetry in who bears enforcement costs. Their overall response pattern suggests they believe others are generally cooperative and at least some are willing to enforce norms. They learn from the group’s revealed willingness to punish defectors and reward cooperators, then adjust their own strategy to exploit cooperative periods intermittently, eventually rejoining full cooperation when it seems secure. Personality-wise, they come across as pragmatic, curious, and moderately pro-social, with a tendency to explore mechanisms early and then settle into a less effortful, more self-serving but not aggressively antisocial role.

They pay attention to others’ contributions, punishments, rewards, and payoffs, and adjust their own use of rewards over time while keeping their contribution strategy fixed. When others punish or reward heavily, they do not mirror those behaviors; instead, they remain non‑punishing and gradually withdraw from rewarding as well. Being punished multiple times does not induce them to increase contributions or retaliate; instead, they stay with free‑riding and, if anything, become more passive in the social mechanisms. They appear to infer that contributing is unnecessary for achieving high payoffs in this environment and that engaging in continued reward or punishment is not cost‑effective. Their reactions suggest a calculated, payoff‑oriented learner who is resilient to social pressure and willing to exploit cooperative norms while letting others bear the enforcement costs.

They appear largely non‑reactive in a strategic sense to others’ payoffs, punishment expenditures, or received rewards. Persistent, heavy punishment of a low contributor by some players does not change their own contribution or trigger any punishment or counter‑reward. They do not step in to punish the low contributor, to punish those who punish, or to reward the victim; they simply continue full cooperation and mostly abstain from sanctioning. Likewise, when they themselves receive occasional small rewards or mild punishment, they do not adjust their behavior: contributions remain maximal and their own use of punishment stays at zero. Their rare reward bursts do align with periods of widespread cooperation rather than with shifts in who is being punished or rewarded, suggesting they attend more to contribution levels than to sanctioning patterns. They neither exploit others’ kindness nor retaliate against exploitation; instead, they maintain a stable cooperative strategy, with only limited, positively framed reinforcement toward others. Whether they feel concern about fairness of outcomes or feel gratitude for rewards is behaviorally under‑specified; the only clear pattern is behavioral inertia in the face of others’ changing costs and payoffs. Overall type (implicit): a rule‑driven, “principled cooperator and occasional encourager” who consistently maximizes contributions, rejects punishment as a tool, and uses modest, symmetric rewards to support already cooperative group norms, with little adaptation to end‑game incentives or to others’ sanctioning behavior.

They observe substantial variation in others’ punishment and reward expenditures and in the resulting payoffs—some players frequently spend heavily to punish a particular low contributor, others reward widely, and at least one player occasionally earns extra by contributing less or zero. Despite this, the focal player’s own behavior remains extremely stable: full contribution, no use of sanctioning tools. This stability suggests a low behavioral reactivity to others’ earnings or sanction patterns. They do not appear to copy high earners’ tactics (such as free‑riding) or respond strategically to “moral enforcers” by joining their efforts. They also do not appear spiteful or vindictive toward apparent free-riders; instead, they rely on others’ punishment behavior to handle norm-breaking while keeping their own play unchanged. The occasional rewards they receive do not alter their trajectory, which was already maximally cooperative. Overall, they behave as if others’ outcomes are informational but not directive: they notice them enough to foresee that cooperation is being maintained and that deviants are sanctioned, yet they do not adjust their own plan in response. This aligns with a personality profile of a principled, non-interventionist cooperator: they uphold the public good personally, allow others to manage norm enforcement, and maintain their strategy regardless of fluctuations in peers’ payoffs or sanction costs.

They pay attention to others’ payoffs and to the pattern of punishment and reward expenditures by peers, but they respond in a nuanced and somewhat asymmetric way. When others bear high punishment costs, especially while continuing to cooperate, they sometimes respond with small rewards directed at those players, as if recognizing or supporting their efforts, even though those punishers are harming them. They do not mirror the intensity of punishment they experience; instead of counter-punishing, they mostly continue cooperating and occasionally reward group members, including those likely to have punished them. At the same time, they continue to test occasional under-contribution despite receiving very large fines, suggesting that fear of future losses is not fully disciplining their behavior. They do not particularly target the one clear late free-rider in the group; they neither punish nor reward that player, indicating either a lack of focus on individual payoff disparities or low confidence in identifying who deserves sanctions under anonymity. In summary, they react to others’ outcomes more through supportive or compensatory rewards than through matching punishment, and they tolerate being singled out by punishers without a clear retaliatory pattern, suggesting a somewhat forgiving or stoic social stance combined with periodic self-interested experimentation.

This player shows no direct, systematic concern for the distribution of payoffs themselves, beyond how those payoffs relate to their own earnings. They repeatedly accept rounds where they earn substantially more than others due to free-riding, without any subsequent move toward “making it fair” in later rounds. Conversely, they sometimes accept being one of the worst-paid players in a round when they contribute while many others do not, without clear evidence that this prompts immediate retaliation in the form of sustained defection. They do not appear to reward high contributors or punish low contributors through their own contribution choices in any consistent way. Cooperative peers who repeatedly earn lower payoffs due to their giving do not trigger a stable pattern of “joining” them out of solidarity. Likewise, defectors who profit from others’ contributions do not provoke a clear, targeted response beyond the player’s general tendency to free-ride when doing so is profitable. The absence of punishment and reward mechanisms in this game limits how much can be inferred about how they would spend personal resources to alter others’ outcomes. Based only on their behavior, they appear comfortable benefiting from others’ sacrifices and do not demonstrate a strong aversion to inequality when they are advantaged. When disadvantaged, they sometimes continue to cooperate and sometimes defect, without a clear shift toward offsetting prior disadvantages. Beliefs about others’ outcomes are therefore best inferred as instrumental: others’ payoffs matter mainly insofar as they signal whether free-riding will be profitable or whether contributions might lead to high group returns. There is no observable pattern of sacrificing own payoff to change others’ relative payoffs. Their social preference profile is most consistent with weak or absent inequality aversion, low enforceable reciprocity, and a primary focus on own earnings with occasional, unexplained cooperative deviations.

This player sees full outcome information for others, but there is no punishment or reward mechanism in this game, and they never pay costs to affect others’ payoffs. Thus, all reactions must be inferred from their own subsequent contribution choices. They routinely observe that non-contributors earn more than contributors in many periods and then choose to remain or become a non-contributor. This indicates that they are not repelled by “unfair” advantage; instead, higher relative payoffs for free-riders appear acceptable and may reinforce their own free-riding. At the same time, they do sometimes respond to successful cooperation by joining in with full contributions, even when that lowers their relative payoff compared to non-helpers in those specific contexts. This suggests they recognize and value the higher group-level returns associated with coordinated contributions, and are willing to occasionally sacrifice some personal gain, but not consistently. There is no sign that they attempt to “discipline” low contributors through their own behavior (for example, by reliably withdrawing cooperation whenever others defect, then rejoining when they improve). Instead, they mix cooperative and non-cooperative responses even in similar social states. This points to either a relatively simple alternating heuristic or low sensitivity to the precise distribution of others’ outcomes. They show no clear pattern of targeted reciprocity toward specific peers (e.g., rewarding particular contributors or reacting to particular defectors), only to the general level of contributions/payoffs in the group. Their orientation toward others’ outcomes is aggregate and impersonal rather than relational. Beliefs about others are inferred as moderately cynical but not fully pessimistic: they act as though free-riding is common and acceptable, yet they also act on the belief that enough others will often continue contributing to keep the public fund profitable. They rely on others’ prosociality while only intermittently sharing in the contribution burden. Personality-wise, this outcome-response pattern is consistent with a pragmatic, efficiency-aware, but self-favoring type: they are comfortable gaining more than others when possible, are not strongly motivated to protect contributors from exploitation, and use others’ outcomes mainly as a cue for when group cooperation is “safe enough” to occasionally join.

The player is consistently insensitive in their actions to others’ realized payoffs. They continue free riding when contributors earn substantially less than them and also when contributors occasionally outperform them. No observable pattern indicates that seeing others “win” or “lose” relative to them changes their subsequent contribution choices. When cooperative others effectively subsidize the public good and earn lower payoffs, the player does not adjust toward contributing out of guilt, fairness, or concern about group welfare. They appear comfortable with repeated payoff asymmetries that benefit them. This suggests weak or absent inequity aversion in the direction of advantageous inequality. When their own payoff temporarily falls below that of others—most prominently when they themselves contribute while some others do not—they immediately revert back to non-contribution. This indicates an aversion to being the “sucker,” but this reaction is expressed only by stopping cooperation, not by attempting to discipline defectors (which is impossible here) or trying to coordinate more cooperation. Across a wide range of group contribution levels and group payoff distributions, their response is effectively invariant: they stay with the individually dominant free-riding strategy. They do not seem to infer or rely on any cooperative norms from the outcome patterns; even when most others behave cooperatively, they continue to exploit that cooperation rather than join it. Given that punishment and reward are not available, they cannot directly act on any desire to sanction or reward others; however, their contribution pattern suggests that even if such tools were available, their primary concern would remain avoiding personal disadvantage rather than reducing others’ advantages. In new environments with similar payoffs, they are likely to keep free riding regardless of how others are doing, and only change behavior if their own payoff is clearly and repeatedly harmed by doing so.

Since no punishment or reward actions occur in this environment, there is no direct evidence of how this player uses such tools. However, their contribution behavior in the face of others’ outcomes provides indirect clues. They repeatedly continue full cooperation even when others’ strategies give those others strictly higher payoffs than the focal player. They do not shift to free-riding after episodes where non-contributors earn much more than they do, nor do they appear to react to temporary improvements in group cooperation by relaxing their contributions. This suggests they are relatively insensitive to unequal payoff distributions and do not strongly prioritize correcting disadvantageous inequality through their own strategic adjustments. When others’ outcomes improve due to higher overall contributions, the player does not respond by exploiting the cooperative environment; instead, they maintain their cooperative stance, indicating that they do not opportunistically chase high relative payoffs when conditions are favorable. They also do not appear to condition on the identity of cooperators or defectors; there is no observable pattern of adjusting behavior based on which specific peers gain or lose. Their implicit response to others’ outcomes is thus characterized by persistence rather than calibration: they keep pursuing the cooperative course regardless of being exploited or rewarded by the group dynamic. They never attempt to exploit temporary cooperation by lowering their own contribution to increase their relative payoff, suggesting a stable internal preference for efficient cooperation over opportunistic gains, even in the face of imperfect others.

They pay enough attention to others’ outcomes and behavior to recognize systematic patterns, such as a player consistently contributing zero and the general presence of high cooperation. They explicitly note that one such player seems to be “not playing the game” and contributing nothing, but they do not escalate this awareness into punishment demands, moral criticism, or appeals to fairness. Instead, they maintain a cooperative or near-cooperative contribution level despite that perceived exploitation. Their observed payoff-sensitive behavior is primarily expressed through their own subtle free-riding rather than through social responses. They benefit from group-level cooperation while occasionally reducing their own contributions, yet they do not voice justification, frustration, or moral reasoning about others’ lower contributions. They also do not express resentment toward the free-riders who are clearly earning more; they seem pragmatically resigned or indifferent, focusing instead on the fact that overall earnings remain relatively high. They neither praise specific high contributors nor thank the group explicitly for cooperation, though their continued high contributions and occasional agreement with the cooperative norm imply appreciation of the collective outcome. They show no signs of retaliatory logic (no tit-for-tat in words, and only mild, non-targeted downward adjustments in contributions). Their response style to others’ outcomes is thus best described as tolerant and payoff-aware but non-punitive and non-confrontational, with mild opportunism expressed privately via their own contribution choices rather than publicly in chat.

The player clearly conditions their behavior on others’ contributions and payoffs, but only through their own contribution choices; they never comment in chat. When others contribute very high amounts, they are comfortable joining them and maintaining full contributions, which sustains high group earnings. At the same time, they are willing to contribute less than the prevailing group level in some rounds, thereby earning more than strict cooperators. This shows that they notice payoff differentials and are willing to place themselves on the advantaged side when the opportunity arises. They do not visibly punish or reward others (those tools are absent) and do not verbally call out low or high contributors, so any approval or disapproval remains internal. They tolerate the presence of some free-riding by others and even parallel it in milder form, as long as group contributions remain high overall. This indicates that their primary focus is on maintaining a profitable cooperative environment while opportunistically improving their own position, rather than on enforcing fairness or reciprocity norms through costly or vocal action. Overall, they behave like a quiet, conditional cooperator with some strategic free-riding, responsive to observed outcomes but non-confrontational and non-communicative about them.

The player appears sensitive to the group’s overall cooperation level but not vindictive or reactive in a strongly negative way. When some others deviate from full contributions and gain higher payoffs, the player briefly reduces their own contribution, which shows that they monitor others’ outcomes and are willing to adjust behavior in response. However, they do not escalate this into sustained low contributions; once cooperation returns to near-universal levels, they fully rejoin it, suggesting that they prioritize restoring the cooperative equilibrium over matching others’ self-serving deviations. They respond to good group outcomes with positive verbal reinforcement, praising collective success rather than highlighting inequality in payoffs. There is no direct comment on specific individuals’ higher or lower payoffs, indicating either restraint or indifference toward fine-grained payoff comparisons. Overall, they are a conditional cooperator who tracks group behavior and outcomes, mildly adjusts when they see reduced cooperation, and then realigns with cooperative norms when those norms are re-established.

The player’s contribution shifts track others’ behavior and payoffs, even though they never comment explicitly. Initially they free-ride in the face of near-unanimous high cooperation and thereby earn more than most others, but they then move toward higher contributions once it is clear that others are sustaining generous behavior and that collective payoffs are high. When others later reduce contributions or deviate slightly, the player partly joins this softening once and then re-aligns with the strong cooperative standard when it re-emerges. They do not punish or reward (these tools are unavailable), nor do they verbally criticize or praise; their “response” is entirely through adjusting their own contribution level. They tolerate some inequality when it benefits them and are willing to exploit very cooperative peers at least once, but they do not continue to systematically undercut the group once cooperation is established. Their beliefs about others’ intentions and fairness norms are not stated and remain largely unknown; the observable pattern is consistent with a payoff-oriented conditional cooperator who updates behavior based on the observed actions and payoffs of the group rather than on expressed moral principles.

The player has full visibility of others’ contributions and payoffs, including clear instances where some individuals earn more by contributing less. Despite this, they do not alter their own behavior, and they offer no verbal criticism, blame, or praise tied to any specific player’s outcome. They neither retaliate against free-riders nor attempt to reward cooperators (no mechanisms are available, and they do not try to substitute with social sanctions in chat). Their reaction can be characterized as tolerant and steadfast: they continue to cooperate fully even when others profit disproportionately from their generosity. They implicitly signal acceptance of payoff inequality when it arises from their chosen cooperative strategy, prioritizing group efficiency over outcome parity. Any emotional response to others’ outcomes is unknown; the observable pattern is behavioral invariance in the face of both high collective success and visible free-riding.

This player closely tracks others’ contributions and final payoffs and appears responsive to the payoff structure: they take opportunities to earn more than others when feasible and revert to contributing when cooperation is high and mutually beneficial. Seeing that others remain highly cooperative after being exploited does not lead them to compensate or apologize (at least not in observable chat), suggesting low aversion to advantageous inequality. There is no evidence of emotional or moral reaction—no praise, blame, or appeals to fairness—toward players who consistently contribute or toward occasional low contributors. Instead, their response to others’ outcomes is implicitly behavioral: they align with cooperative norms when group cooperation is stable and high, and they diverge when unilateral deviation provides a clear payoff advantage without visible consequences. Their sensitivity is therefore economic rather than relational or moral.

The player can observe everyone’s contributions and payoffs but does not adjust contributions in response. When others earn more by contributing less, the player continues to fully cooperate, indicating a high tolerance for payoff inequality when it stems from free-riding. They do not escalate contributions in response to high cooperation (they are already at the maximum) and do not de-escalate when cooperation partially breaks. There is no punitive or reciprocal pattern available to them in this design, but they also refrain from the only form of “soft punishment” available—reducing their own contributions to protect themselves from exploitation. They also remain silent in chat when others comment on outcomes, offering neither praise nor criticism. Overall, they behave as a steadfast cooperator who prioritizes maintaining a cooperative norm or efficient group outcome over reacting to deviations in individual payoffs, and they do not visibly express emotional reactions to inequality or opportunism.

The player has full visibility of others’ contributions and payoffs but never comments on them explicitly. Nonetheless, their own payoff-maximizing choices at selected times show that they are attentive to the payoff structure and how others’ contributions generate high earnings for everyone, including lower contributors. They do not punish or reward (no such mechanisms exist), and they do not use language to sanction, praise, or shame others for deviating. When some group members free-ride or under-contribute, the focal player does not visibly retaliate by sharply dropping contributions; instead, they incorporate the situation into modestly self-serving contribution choices while still preserving a generally cooperative posture. This indicates tolerance of some inequality and deviation, as long as the overall cooperative framework remains profitable. Their own intermittent free-riding demonstrates that they understand the asymmetry between contributors and beneficiaries: they are willing to accept higher payoffs relative to more generous others, and they do not appear troubled enough by this to adjust toward strict equality. Their “response” to others’ outcomes is therefore implicit and payoff-driven: they adjust their own contributions to secure favorable personal outcomes while relying on the group’s ongoing cooperative behavior. Explicit emotional or moral reactions to others’ payoffs remain unknown.

The player closely benefits from others’ high contributions yet shows no explicit verbal reaction to payoff distributions. They observe that others often contribute much more than they do in their low-contribution rounds and that these others earn less than them as a result, but they neither apologize, justify, nor comment on this disparity in chat. Their lack of comment suggests low salience of distributional fairness concerns or, at minimum, a willingness to ignore them. At the same time, when the group converges on full cooperation, the player fully joins in, indicating that they value the high-payoff cooperative outcome enough to contribute maximally when the group standard is strong. There is no visible retaliatory behavior toward slight reductions by others; they do not appear to punish emerging small free-riders by lowering their own contributions in a targeted or reactive way. Instead, their contribution choices seem driven primarily by payoff considerations and the overall cooperation level, not by emotional responses to specific others’ payoffs. Overall, they are best characterized as payoff-focused, tolerant of others’ minor deviations, and comfortable with personally advantageous inequality when it arises.

They show clear attention to the joint outcome structure and to relative earnings, but they do not react in a purely retaliatory or exploitative manner. When others benefit more than they do while contributing less, they do not collapse to zero contribution or adopt a strict free-riding strategy; instead, they alter their own contribution up or down within a moderate range. This indicates a tempered, incremental response style rather than sharp punishment-by-defection. Their behavior is consistent with a player who cares about efficiency and mutual gains but also monitors fairness. They tolerate some inequity and occasional disadvantage, suggesting they are not purely driven by envy or strict equality concerns. At the same time, they do not unconditionally sacrifice for others: when others’ contributions drop or when their own payoff falls behind, they reduce their own provision, signaling a desire for more balanced cooperation. Because no explicit punishment or reward mechanisms are available and are not used, their responses manifest only through contribution adjustments. These adjustments suggest an adaptive, learning-oriented player who updates beliefs about partners’ cooperativeness over time and modulates their own generosity accordingly. Their overall pattern is consistent with a conditional cooperator who aims for mutually beneficial outcomes but remains attentive to not being systematically exploited.
